questions.question: According to pooled data from 25 head-to-head comparisons, what was the detection ratio for ISUP GG 1 cancers in biopsy-naïve patients when comparing systematic biopsy to MRI-targeted biopsy?
questions.explanation: The pooled data of 25 head-to-head comparisons showed a detection ratio of 0.63 (95% CI: 0.54-0.74) for ISUP GG 1 cancers in biopsy-naïve patients, meaning systematic biopsy detected more low-grade cancers than MRI-targeted biopsy.

questions.question: According to the 2019 ISUP consensus conference, which approach should be used for grading when reporting MRI-targeted biopsy results?

Using the average of systematic and targeted biopsy ISUP GG values
Using only the first MR-targeted biopsy core for grading

questions.question: According to the guideline, what is the prognosis for patients when the ISUP GG differs between systematic biopsy and MRI-targeted biopsy or prostatectomy specimens?

Prognosis is determined by the higher grade only
Prognosis is determined by the lower grade only

questions.question: What characteristic distinguishes MRI-visible tumours from MRI-invisible lesions according to the guideline?

MRI-visible tumours are associated with smaller tumour volume
MRI-visible tumours have lower rates of extraprostatic extension

questions.question: What is the minimum number of cores required for proper sampling of an MRI-detected lesion?
questions.explanation: Section 5.5.5 explicitly states that 'A minimum of three to five cores is required for proper sampling of an MRI-detected lesion'. This establishes the minimum standard for adequate tissue sampling during MRI-targeted biopsy.

questions.question: In patients with a unilateral MRI-detected lesion, what is the added value of contralateral systematic biopsy for diagnosing clinically significant prostate cancer?
questions.explanation: Section 5.5.5 states that 'in case of a unilateral MRI lesion, contralateral systematic biopsy has little added value for diagnosing csPCa (0.3-4%)'. This demonstrates that systematic biopsy of the MRI-negative lobe provides minimal diagnostic benefit.

questions.question: According to the meta-analysis cited in section 5.5.5, MRI-directed targeted and regional biopsy approach detected significantly more ISUP GG ≥ 2 cancers than MRI-targeted biopsy alone with which relative risk?
questions.explanation: Section 5.5.5 reports that 'the MRI-directed targeted and regional biopsy approach detected significantly more ISUP GG ≥ 2 cancers than MRI-targeted biopsy alone (RR: 1.18, 95% CI: 1.10–1.25; p < 0.001)'. This demonstrates the additional detection benefit of including perilesional biopsies.

questions.question: The targeted and regional biopsy approach compared to the recommended MRI-directed targeted and systematic biopsy approach shows what advantage?

options.text: Significantly higher detection of clinically significant cancer
Avoiding overdiagnosis of ISUP GG 1 cancer

questions.question: In the German PROBASE trial post-hoc analysis, which biopsy method detected the highest proportion of Grade Group ≥2 cancers?

questions.question: According to the guideline, which anatomical regions showed discrepancies in cancer detection due to being more difficult to access via transrectal approach?

questions.fact: Discrepancies for anterior, apical, and lateral lesions were observed, which are often more difficult to reach via transrectal than transperineal access

questions.question: In men aged 45-55 years in the PROBASE trial, what proportion of MRI examinations showed PI-RADS 3 findings?

questions.question: What is the strength of the recommendation against using MRI as an initial screening tool for prostate cancer?

questions.question: According to the guidelines, what PSA density threshold allows omitting biopsy when MRI is negative (PI-RADS ≤ 2) and clinical suspicion is low?
questions.explanation: Section 5.6 specifies that when MRI is negative (PI-RADS ≤ 2) and clinical suspicion of PCa is low, with PSA density < 0.20 ng/mL/cc and no family history, biopsy can be omitted and PSA monitoring offered instead.
questions.fact: Omit biopsy and offer PSA monitoring when MRI is negative (i.e. PI-RADS ≤ 2), and clinical suspicion of PCa is low (PSA density < 0.20 ng/mL/cc and no family history).

questions.question: In which PI-RADS category is targeted biopsy with perilesional sampling recommended?
questions.explanation: Section 5.6 states that targeted biopsy should be combined with perilesional sampling when MRI is positive, specifically when the lesion is classified as PI-RADS ≥ 4, indicating clinically significant findings warranting tissue diagnosis.
questions.fact: Combine targeted biopsy with perilesional sampling when MRI is positive (i.e. PI-RADS ≥ 4).

questions.question: When MRI is indeterminate (PI-RADS = 3), what additional PSA density threshold must be met to consider omitting biopsy?
questions.explanation: Section 5.6 specifies that for an indeterminate MRI (PI-RADS = 3), biopsy may be omitted only when clinical suspicion is very low, which is defined as PSA density < 0.10 ng/mL/cc and no family history. This is a stricter threshold than for PI-RADS ≤ 2.
questions.fact: When MRI is indeterminate (PI-RADS = 3), and clinical suspicion of PCa is very low (PSA density < 0.10 ng/mL/cc and no family history), omit biopsy and offer PSA monitoring.

questions.question: Which of the following is an acceptable method for obtaining MR-targeted biopsy?

options.text: Cognitive guidance, ultrasound/MR fusion software, or direct in-bore guidance

questions.fact: Where MRI has shown a suspicious lesion, MR-targeted biopsy can be obtained through cognitive guidance, ultrasound/MR fusion software or direct in-bore guidance.

questions.question: According to the systematic review and meta-analysis of 8 studies comparing MRI-targeted biopsies, what sensitivity for detection of clinically significant prostate cancer (csPCa) was reported for the transperineal approach?
questions.explanation: Section 5.6.2 states that the systematic review and meta-analysis 'showed a higher sensitivity for detection of csPCa when the transperineal approach was used (86% vs 73%)'. The transperineal approach demonstrated 86% sensitivity compared to 73% for the transrectal approach.
questions.fact: Systematic review and meta-analysis (8 studies) showed 86% sensitivity for transperineal vs 73% for transrectal MRI-targeted biopsy

questions.question: In the TRANSLATE RCT with 1,126 men randomised to compare MRI-guided transperineal and transrectal biopsies, what was the csPCa detection rate in the transperineal arm?
questions.explanation: Section 5.6.2 reports the TRANSLATE RCT findings: 'In 1,126 men randomised in the study, csPCa rates were 60% in TP and 54% in TR arms (OR 1.32 [95% CI 1.03-1.70]; p=0.031)'. The transperineal arm detected csPCa in 60% of cases.

questions.question: According to the PERFECT trial, which biopsy approach demonstrated higher significant cancer detection rates for anterior prostate tumors?

options.text: Both approaches had equal detection rates
Neither approach was effective for anterior tumors

The transperineal approach was superior for anterior tumors.

questions.question: Based on all the evidence presented in section 5.6.2 regarding transperineal versus transrectal MRI-guided prostate biopsy, which approach is recommended as preferred for csPCa detection?

options.text: The guideline does not express a preference

The guideline synthesizes the evidence from TRANSLATE RCT showing 60% vs 54% csPCa detection and fewer adverse events to recommend transperineal as preferred.

options.text: MRI in-bore biopsy in all centres

questions.question: What position are patients placed in for transperineal prostate biopsy under local anaesthesia?

questions.question: According to the systematic review cited in the guidelines, what is the relative risk of increased pain with transperineal compared to transrectal biopsy?

questions.question: Which local anaesthesia technique was shown to be INFERIOR for prostate biopsy according to the guidelines?

questions.question: In the updated meta-analysis cited (28 RCTs, 4,027 patients), what was the finding regarding infectious complications with periprostatic injection of local anaesthesia?

options.text: Periprostatic injection significantly increased infectious complications
Periprostatic injection significantly decreased infectious complications
No evidence that periprostatic injection resulted in more infectious complications than no injection

questions.section_title: 5.6.3. Infection rate after transperineal and transrectal prostate biopsy

questions.question: According to the EAU guidelines, what is the recommended approach for prostate biopsy due to its low risk of infectious complications and better antibiotic stewardship?

options.text: Transrectal biopsy with extended antimicrobial prophylaxis
Transrectal biopsy with rectal povidone-iodine preparation

questions.explanation: The guidelines strongly recommend performing prostate biopsy using the transperineal approach due to the low risk of infectious complications and better antibiotic stewardship. A meta-analysis of 13 studies with 4,516 patients demonstrated significantly reduced infectious complications with transperineal biopsy compared to transrectal biopsy.
questions.fact: Perform prostate biopsy using the transperineal approach due to the low risk of infectious complications and better antibiotic stewardship.

questions.question: In patients without risk factors for infectious complications, what is the recommendation regarding perioperative antibiotic prophylaxis for transperineal biopsy?

Administer augmented prophylaxis with multiple antibiotics

questions.question: What is the recommended first option for antibiotic prophylaxis in transrectal biopsy according to EAU guidelines?

options.text: Augmented prophylaxis using two or more different classes of antibiotics
Targeted prophylaxis based on rectal swab or stool culture

questions.explanation: The guidelines recommend targeted prophylaxis based on rectal swab or stool culture as the first option for antibiotic prophylaxis in transrectal biopsy. questions.fact: First option: Targeted prophylaxis based on rectal swab or stool culture.

questions.question: What additional measure is strongly recommended before transrectal prostate biopsy?
questions.explanation: The guidelines give a Strong recommendation to use rectal cleansing with povidone-iodine prior to transrectal prostate biopsy. A meta-analysis of 12 RCTs including 2,437 men showed that this preparation, in addition to antimicrobial prophylaxis, resulted in a significantly lower rate of infectious complications.
questions.fact: Use rectal cleansing with povidone-iodine prior to transrectal prostate biopsy.

questions.question: Why has the indication for fosfomycin trometamol for prostate biopsy been withdrawn in Germany?

questions.question: What percentage of patients could avoid biopsy using the 'MRI pathway' if a PI-RADS threshold of ≥ 3 is used to trigger biopsy?

questions.question: What type of cancers does the 'MRI pathway' mainly miss when used in biopsy-naïve patients or populations with high prevalence of clinically significant prostate cancer?
questions.explanation: Section 5.7.1 explicitly states that the MRI pathway misses mainly ISUP GG 2 cancers, especially in biopsy-naïve patients or in highly selected populations with high prevalence of csPCa, due to the decreased MRI NPV in these populations.

questions.question: In which patient population are systematic biopsies still necessary despite a negative MRI?

options.text: Patients with positive digital rectal examination
Patients with high clinical suspicion of PCa, such as high PSA density
Patients with ISUP GG 1 at prior biopsy

questions.explanation: Section 5.7.1 states that due to the low NPV of MRI in high-risk populations, systematic biopsies are still necessary in patients with negative MRI and high clinical suspicion of PCa, e.g. high PSA density.
questions.fact: Due to the low NPV of MRI in high-risk populations, systematic biopsies are still necessary in patients with negative MRI and high clinical suspicion of PCa, e.g. high PSA density

questions.question: What strategy could mitigate the drawbacks of the 'MRI pathway' by maintaining good detection of clinically significant prostate cancer while decreasing overdiagnosis of insignificant cancer?

options.text: Adding perilesional sampling to targeted biopsy
Replacing MRI with transperineal template biopsy
Using PSA density alone as the trigger for biopsy

questions.explanation: Section 5.7.1 explicitly states that adding perilesional sampling to targeted biopsy could mitigate the drawbacks of the 'MRI pathway' by maintaining good detection of csPCa while decreasing the overdiagnosis of insignificant cancer.
questions.fact: Adding perilesional sampling to targeted biopsy could mitigate the drawbacks of the 'MRI pathway' by maintaining good detection of csPCa while decreasing the overdiagnosis of insignificant cancer

questions.question: According to the guidelines, what is the detection rate of transition zone sampling during baseline biopsies?
questions.explanation: Section 5.8 explicitly states that transition zone sampling during baseline biopsies has a low detection rate, which is why its use is restricted.
questions.fact: Transition zone sampling during baseline biopsies has a low detection rate

questions.question: According to the guideline, transition zone sampling should be limited to which of the following scenarios?

options.text: MRI detected lesions or repeat template biopsies

questions.explanation: Section 5.8 states that transition zone sampling should be limited to MRI detected lesions or repeat template biopsies due to its low detection rate during baseline biopsies.
questions.fact: Transition zone sampling during baseline biopsies has a low detection rate and should be limited to MRI detected lesions or repeat template biopsies

questions.question: What are the odds of tumour involvement in the seminal vesicles when PSA exceeds 15ng/mL?
questions.explanation: According to section 5.7.4, at a PSA greater than 15ng/mL, the odds of tumour involvement are 20-25%, as referenced by study 481.
questions.fact: At a PSA of > 15ng/mL, the odds of tumour involvement are 20-25% [481].

questions.question: According to the guideline, when is a seminal vesicle (SV) staging biopsy only considered useful?

options.text: When it has a decisive impact on treatment decisions

questions.explanation: Section 5.7.4 states that an SV staging biopsy is only useful if it has a decisive impact on treatment, such as ruling out radical tumour resection or for potential subsequent radiation therapy.
questions.fact: A SV staging biopsy is only useful if it has a decisive impact on treatment, such as ruling out radical tumour resection or for potential subsequent RT.

questions.question: How are the indications for seminal vesicle (SV) staging biopsies defined in the guideline?
questions.explanation: Section 5.7.4 explicitly states that indications for SV (staging) biopsies are poorly defined, highlighting the lack of standardised criteria for their use.
questions.fact: Indications for SV (staging) biopsies are poorly defined.

questions.question: What is the incidence of prostate cancer detected by saturation repeat biopsy (>20 cores)?
questions.explanation: According to Section 5.7.3, the incidence of PCa detected by saturation repeat biopsy (>20 cores) is 30-43%, depending on the number of cores sampled during earlier biopsies.
questions.fact: The incidence of PCa detected by saturation repeat biopsy (>20 cores) is 30–43%

questions.question: Using the transperineal technique, saturation biopsy detects an additional what percentage of prostate cancers?
questions.explanation: Section 5.7.3 states that saturation biopsy performed using the transperineal technique detects an additional 38% of PCa.
questions.fact: Saturation biopsy may be performed using the transperineal technique, which detects an additional 38% of PCa

questions.question: What is the range of urinary retention rates associated with saturation biopsy?
questions.explanation: Section 5.7.3 reports that the rate of urinary retention varies substantially from 1.2% to 10% with saturation biopsy.
questions.fact: The rate of urinary retention varies substantially from 1.2% to 10%

questions.question: According to current guidelines, saturation repeat biopsy schemes should not be routinely used because:

options.text: The risk of subsequent clinically significant PCa after a negative biopsy is very low in the current MRI-driven diagnostic pathway
The transperineal technique has been discontinued
The detection rate is too low to justify the procedure
Urinary retention rates are prohibitively high

questions.explanation: Section 5.7.3 states that given the very low risk of subsequent csPCa after a negative biopsy in the current MRI-driven diagnostic pathway, such saturation biopsy schemes should not be routinely used.
questions.fact: Given the very low risk of subsequent csPCa after a negative biopsy in the current MRI-driven diagnostic pathway, such schemes should not be routinely used

questions.question: What is the stated accuracy of transrectal ultrasound (TRUS) for local staging of prostate cancer?
questions.explanation: Section 5.8.1.b states that 'Transrectal US has limited accuracy for PCa local staging.' The guideline explicitly describes TRUS as having limited accuracy for this purpose, making option A the correct answer.
questions.fact: Transrectal US has limited accuracy for PCa local staging [484]

questions.question: According to the guideline, which imaging modalities may demonstrate rectal or bladder invasion in locally advanced prostate cancer?
questions.explanation: Section 5.8.1.b states that 'In case of locally advanced cancers, abdominopelvic US or CT may show rectal or bladder invasion and dilatation of the upper collecting systems.' This directly supports option B as the correct answer.
questions.fact: In case of locally advanced cancers, abdominopelvic US or CT may show rectal or bladder invasion and dilatation of the upper collecting systems [484]

questions.question: What is the current status of advanced US-based techniques for prostate cancer staging according to the guideline?

options.text: Validated as equivalent to multiparametric MRI in multiple trials
Limited to research settings with published large-scale data
Not yet tested in large-scale studies
Recommended as first-line imaging for local staging

questions.explanation: Section 5.8.1.b states that 'More advanced US-based techniques have not yet been tested in large-scale studies.' This indicates that advanced US techniques lack large-scale validation, making option C correct. Options A, B, and D are not supported by the text.
questions.fact: More advanced US-based techniques have not yet been tested in large-scale studies.

questions.question: According to the guideline, what does the cT category rely on for staging?

options.text: DRE findings combined with imaging parameters
DRE findings combined with biopsy results

questions.explanation: Section 5.8.1.a states that 'the cT category listed in Table 4.1 relies only on DRE findings,' meaning digital rectal examination is the sole determinant for clinical T category classification.
questions.fact: The cT category listed in Table 4.1 relies only on DRE findings.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT currently part of the T staging within TNM for prostate cancer?
questions.explanation: Section 5.8.1.a explicitly states that 'imaging parameters and biopsy results for local staging are, so far, not part of the T staging (within TNM),' indicating imaging parameters should not be incorporated into TNM T classification.
questions.fact: Imaging parameters and biopsy results for local staging are, so far, not part of the T staging (within TNM).

questions.question: According to the guideline, which of the following is NOT included in the EAU risk category stratification?
questions.explanation: Section 5.8.1.a clearly states that 'imaging parameters and biopsy results for local staging are, so far, not part of the...EAU risk category stratification,' making this the correct answer as it is not included.
questions.fact: Imaging parameters and biopsy results for local staging are, so far, not part of the T staging (within TNM) and the EAU risk category stratification.

questions.question: What imaging modality remains the most useful method for local staging on MRI according to the EAU guidelines on prostate cancer?
questions.explanation: The guideline section 5.8.2 on N staging explicitly states that 'T2-weighted imaging remains the most useful method for local staging on MRI.' This is a direct factual statement from the text.
questions.fact: T2-weighted imaging remains the most useful method for local staging on MRI.

questions.question: In a study of 552 men treated by radical prostatectomy at seven Dutch centres, how did MRI compare to digital rectal examination (DRE) for detecting non-organ-confined disease?

options.text: MRI showed higher sensitivity (51% vs 12%) and higher specificity (82% vs 97%)
MRI showed lower sensitivity (12% vs 51%) and higher specificity (97% vs 82%)
MRI showed higher sensitivity (51% vs 12%) and lower specificity (82% vs 97%)
MRI showed lower sensitivity (12% vs 51%) and lower specificity (82% vs 97%)

questions.explanation: The guideline specifically states: 'In 552 men treated by RP at seven different Dutch centres, MRI showed significantly higher sensitivity (51% vs. 12%; p < 0.001), and lower specificity (82% vs. 97%; p < 0.001) than DRE for non-organ-confined disease.' This comparative finding is explicitly documented in the text.
questions.fact: In 552 men treated by RP at seven different Dutch centres, MRI showed significantly higher sensitivity (51% vs 12%), and lower specificity (82% vs 97%) than DRE for non-organ-confined disease.

questions.question: What is the recommended role of MRI for local staging in low-risk prostate cancer patients?

options.text: MRI is recommended as the primary staging tool
MRI is recommended for all patients regardless of risk category
MRI is not recommended for local staging but may be useful for treatment planning
MRI should only be used in combination with PET-CT

questions.explanation: The guideline explicitly states: 'Given its low sensitivity for focal (microscopic) EPE, MRI is not recommended for local staging in low-risk patients. However, MRI can still be useful for treatment planning.' This distinguishes between staging recommendation and treatment planning utility.
questions.fact: Given its low sensitivity for focal (microscopic) EPE, MRI is not recommended for local staging in low-risk patients. However, MRI can still be useful for treatment planning.

questions.question: What are the size thresholds for lymph nodes to be considered malignant on imaging?

options.text: Pelvis: >8mm short axis; Outside pelvis: >10mm short axis
Pelvis: >10mm short axis; Outside pelvis: >8mm short axis
Pelvis: >5mm short axis; Outside pelvis: >8mm short axis
Pelvis: >12mm short axis; Outside pelvis: >15mm short axis

questions.explanation: According to section 5.8.2.b, lymph nodes with a short axis >8mm in the pelvis and >10mm outside the pelvis are considered malignant. Decreasing these thresholds improves sensitivity but decreases specificity.
questions.section_title: 5.8.2.b. Risk calculators incorporating MRI findings and clinical data
questions.fact: LNs with a short axis >8mm in the pelvis and >10mm outside the pelvis are considered malignant

questions.question: What is the sensitivity of computed tomography and MRI for detecting lymph node invasion in prostate cancer staging?
questions.explanation: Section 5.8.2.b states that CT and MRI sensitivity for assessing nodal invasion is less than 40%. This limited sensitivity is one reason why the ideal size threshold for defining malignant lymph nodes remains unclear.
questions.section_title: 5.8.2.b. Risk calculators incorporating MRI findings and clinical data
questions.fact: Computed tomography and MRI sensitivity is less than 40%

questions.question: In which patient population is detection of microscopic lymph node invasion by CT less than 1%?

options.text: Patients with ISUP grade group <4, PSA <20ng/mL or localised disease
Patients with ISUP grade group >4, PSA >20ng/mL or metastatic disease
Patients with any ISUP grade group and PSA <10ng/mL

questions.explanation: Section 5.8.2.b explicitly states that detection of microscopic lymph node invasion by CT is <1% in patients with ISUP grade group <4, PSA <20ng/mL or localised disease. This represents a low-risk population where CT has very limited utility for nodal assessment.
questions.section_title: 5.8.2.b. Risk calculators incorporating MRI findings and clinical data
questions.fact: Detection of microscopic LN invasion by CT is <1% in patients with ISUP grade group <4, PSA <20ng/mL or localised disease

questions.question: According to the phase III imaging trial investigating men with intermediate- and high-risk prostate cancer who underwent radical prostatectomy and pelvic lymph node dissection, what was the sensitivity of 68Ga-PSMA-11 PET for lymph node staging?
questions.explanation: Section 5.8.2d states that the multicentre prospective phase III imaging trial showed a sensitivity of 68Ga-PSMA-11 PET of 0.40 (95% CI: 0.34-0.46) for men with intermediate- and high-risk PCa undergoing RP and PLND.
questions.section_title: 5.8.2.d. Risk calculators incorporating MRI and PSMA findings
questions.fact: Sensitivity of 68Ga-PSMA-11 PET was 0.40 (95% CI: 0.34-0.46) in phase III trial

questions.question: Based on the systematic review and meta-analysis including 37 studies, what were the per-patient-based sensitivity and specificity values for 68Ga-PSMA PET in primary staging after extended pelvic lymph node dissection at the time of radical prostatectomy?
questions.explanation: Section 5.8.2d reports from the meta-analysis including 37 studies that on a per-patient-based analysis, sensitivity was 77% and specificity was 97% after eLND at the time of RP.
questions.section_title: 5.8.2.d. Risk calculators incorporating MRI and PSMA findings
questions.fact: Per-patient-based analysis showed sensitivity 77% and specificity 97%

questions.question: When comparing PSMA PET/CT to MRI for lymph node staging in intermediate- and high-risk prostate cancer, what was the finding regarding sensitivity?

options.text: PSMA PET/CT has lower sensitivity but higher specificity than MRI
PSMA PET/CT has comparable sensitivity but higher specificity than MRI
PSMA PET/CT has higher sensitivity and comparable specificity than MRI
PSMA PET/CT has comparable sensitivity and lower specificity than MRI

questions.explanation: Section 5.8.2d states that the systematic review and meta-analysis including 13 studies found that 68Ga-PSMA had a higher sensitivity and a comparable specificity for staging preoperative LN metastases in intermediate- and high-risk PCa compared to MRI.
questions.section_title: 5.8.2.d. Risk calculators incorporating MRI and PSMA findings
questions.fact: 68Ga-PSMA had higher sensitivity and comparable specificity compared to MRI

questions.question: What is the limitation of CT and MRI in the context of lymph node assessment in prostate cancer?

options.text: They lack sensitivity for direct detection of positive lymph nodes
They cannot accurately visualize the prostate gland
They expose patients to harmful radiation
They are not suitable for patients with implants

questions.explanation: Section 5.8.2.c explicitly states that 'Computed tomography and MRI lack sensitivity for direct detection of positive LNs.' This limitation is why alternative methods like PSMA PET/CT are being evaluated for lymph node staging.
questions.fact: Computed tomography and MRI lack sensitivity for direct detection of positive LNs.

questions.question: Which of the following components are combined in the risk calculators and nomograms for estimating lymph node involvement?

options.text: Clinical data, systematic and/or MRI-targeted biopsy results, and MRI findings
Patient demographics, serum PSA levels, and family history
Imaging reports, pathology slides, and operative notes
Bone scan results, clinical stage, and Gleason score alone

questions.explanation: Section 5.8.2.c states that 'risk calculators and nomograms which combine clinical data, systematic and/or MRI-targeted biopsy results and, for some of them, MRI findings have been developed and externally validated.' This multifactorial approach helps estimate the risk of patients harbouring positive lymph nodes.
questions.fact: Different risk calculators and nomograms which combine clinical data, systematic and/or MRI-targeted biopsy results and, for some of them, MRI findings have been developed and externally validated to estimate the risk of patients harbouring positive LNs.

questions.question: According to the guideline, what is the clinical consequence of applying commonly proposed thresholds in lymph node risk models?

options.text: Considerable proportion of patients undergoing potentially unnecessary pelvic lymph node dissection, particularly in low prevalence settings
Increased number of prostate biopsies performed
Higher rates of biochemical recurrence after treatment

questions.explanation: Section 5.8.2.c explicitly states that 'The application of commonly proposed thresholds is associated with limited specificity, leading to a considerable proportion of patients undergoing potentially unnecessary PLND, particularly in settings with low prevalence of LNI.' This highlights the trade-off between sensitivity and specificity in clinical decision-making.
questions.fact: The application of commonly proposed thresholds is associated with limited specificity, leading to a considerable proportion of patients undergoing potentially unnecessary PLND, particularly in settings with low prevalence of LNI.

questions.question: How many patients were included in the international, multicentre study that incorporated PSMA PET into existing nomograms to predict pelvic lymph node metastatic disease?
questions.explanation: Section 5.8.2.e states that the performance of three nomograms was assessed in 757 patients undergoing RARP and ePLND when PSMA PET was incorporated into existing nomograms to predict pelvic LN metastatic disease.
questions.fact: The study included 757 patients undergoing RARP and ePLND

questions.question: What was the range of cross-validated AUCs achieved when PSMA PET was added to the nomograms for predicting pelvic lymph node metastases?
questions.explanation: Section 5.8.2.e reports that the addition of PSMA PET substantially improved discriminative ability, yielding cross-validated AUCs of 0.76 (95% CI: 0.70–0.82), 0.77 (95% CI: 0.72–0.83) and 0.82 (95% CI: 0.76–0.87), respectively.
questions.fact: Cross-validated AUCs were 0.76, 0.77, and 0.82

questions.question: Compared to other established nomograms, how did the combined MRI and PSMA PET nomogram perform on the external Australian cohort?

options.text: It performed significantly worse than all other nomograms
It performed significantly better than Briganti 2017 and MSKCC, but similar to Briganti 2019
It performed better than Briganti 2019 but worse than MSKCC
Its performance was superior to all established nomograms

questions.explanation: Section 5.8.2.e states that on external validation in 305 Australian patients, the nomogram incorporating staging MRI and PSMA PET performed significantly better than Briganti 2017 and MSKCC nomograms, with performance similar to the Briganti 2019 nomogram.
questions.fact: The nomogram performed better than Briganti 2017 and MSKCC but similar to Briganti 2019 on external validation

questions.question: What is the approximate spatial resolution of PSMA PET imaging that necessitates development of new prediction models for PSMA PET-negative patients?
questions.explanation: Section 5.8.2.e explains that new models have been developed for patients with negative PSMA PET findings to identify those at risk of harbouring micrometastatic disease undetectable by this modality due to its spatial resolution of approximately 5 mm.
questions.fact: PSMA PET has spatial resolution of approximately 5 mm

questions.question: According to a meta-analysis, what are the combined sensitivity and specificity values of 99mTc-bone scan at the patient level?
questions.explanation: Section 5.8.3.b states that a meta-analysis showed combined sensitivity and specificity of 79% (95% CI: 73–83%) and 82% (95% CI: 78–85%) at patient level for 99mTc-bone scan.
questions.fact: A meta-analysis showed combined sensitivity and specificity of 79% (95% CI: 73–83%) and 82% (95% CI: 78–85%) at patient level [541]

questions.question: In which type of patient should bone scanning be performed, regardless of PSA level, ISUP GG or clinical stage?
questions.explanation: Section 5.8.3.b explicitly states that bone scanning should be performed in symptomatic patients, independent of PSA level, ISUP GG or clinical stage [499].
questions.fact: Bone scanning should be performed in symptomatic patients, independent of PSA level, ISUP GG or clinical stage [499]

questions.question: What factors significantly influence bone scan diagnostic yield in prostate cancer patients?

options.text: Patient age and PSA level only
PSA level, clinical stage, and ISUP grade group
ISUP grade group and tumour volume only

questions.explanation: Section 5.8.3.b states that bone scan diagnostic yield is significantly influenced by the PSA level, the clinical stage and the tumour ISUP grade group [499,542].
questions.fact: Bone scan diagnostic yield is significantly influenced by the PSA level, the clinical stage and the tumour ISUP grade group [499,542]

questions.question: What is the relationship between 18F-sodium fluoride (18F-NaF) PET/CT and bone scintigraphy in terms of sensitivity for detecting bone metastases in patients with newly diagnosed high-risk prostate cancer?

options.text: Variable sensitivity depending on PSA level

questions.explanation: According to section 5.8.3.c, the guideline states that 18F-NaF PET was reported to have similar specificity but superior sensitivity to bone scintigraphy for detecting bone metastases in patients with newly diagnosed high-risk PCa.
questions.fact: The tracer was reported to have similar specificity and superior sensitivity to bone scintigraphy for detecting bone metastases in patients with newly diagnosed high-risk PCa

questions.question: According to the guideline, what additional types of metastases can whole-body MRI detect, beyond bone metastases?

options.text: Lymphatic and soft tissue metastases only

questions.explanation: The guideline in section 5.8.3.c states that whole-body MRI can also detect visceral and nodal metastases, having been shown to be more sensitive and specific than combined bone scintigraphy, targeted radiography and abdominopelvic CT.
questions.fact: Whole-body MRI can also detect visceral and nodal metastases

questions.question: What was reported about the interobserver agreement for 18F-sodium fluoride (18F-NaF) PET/CT in detecting bone metastases in prostate cancer patients?

options.text: It was moderate with acceptable variability
It was not evaluated in studies

questions.explanation: Section 5.8.3.c of the guideline explicitly states that interobserver agreement for the detection of bone metastases was excellent, demonstrating that 18F-NaF PET/CT is a robust tool for the detection of osteoblastic lesions in patients with PCa.
questions.fact: Interobserver agreement for the detection of bone metastases was excellent

questions.question: According to the EAU guidelines, what anatomical structures are removed during extended pelvic lymph node dissection (ePLND)?

options.text: Nodes overlying the external iliac artery and vein, nodes within the obturator fossa (cranially and caudally to the obturator nerve), and nodes medial and lateral to the internal iliac artery
Nodes within the obturator fossa and para-aortic lymph nodes
Nodes overlying the external iliac artery and vein only
Nodes medial to the internal iliac artery and common iliac nodes

questions.explanation: The guideline explicitly defines ePLND as including removal of nodes overlying the external iliac artery and vein, nodes within the obturator fossa located cranially and caudally to the obturator nerve, and nodes medial and lateral to the internal iliac artery (section 5.8.2.e.1).
questions.fact: Extended PLND includes removal of the nodes overlying the external iliac artery and vein, the nodes within the obturator fossa located cranially and caudally to the obturator nerve, and the nodes medial and lateral to the internal iliac artery.

questions.question: What was the most common type of complication reported in the systematic review and meta-analysis of postoperative complications after radical prostatectomy with PLND?
questions.explanation: The guideline states that in a systematic review and meta-analysis of postoperative complications after RP, 31.5% of reported complications were due to lymphocele, with 10% requiring surgical/percutaneous drainage (section 5.8.2.e.2).
questions.fact: In a SR and meta-analysis of postoperative complications after RP, 31.5% of reported complications were due to lymphocele, with 10% requiring surgical/percutaneous drainage.

questions.question: According to the guideline section 5.8.5, compared to classical work-up with bone scan and abdominopelvic CT, what do PSMA PET/CT and whole-body MRI provide?

options.text: More sensitive detection of lymph node and bone metastases
More cost-effective staging in all prostate cancer patients
Faster results with equivalent diagnostic accuracy
Better visualization of local tumor extent within the prostate

questions.explanation: Section 5.8.5 explicitly states that 'PSMA PET/CT and whole-body MRI provide a more sensitive detection of LN and bone metastases than the classical work-up with bone scan and abdominopelvic CT.' The guideline does not mention cost-effectiveness, faster results, or local tumor visualization in this comparison.
questions.section_title: 5.8.5. Recommendations for staging of prostate cancer
questions.fact: PSMA PET/CT and whole-body MRI provide a more sensitive detection of LN and bone metastases than the classical work-up with bone scan and abdominopelvic CT.

questions.question: According to the guideline section 5.8.5, what remains unknown regarding patients diagnosed as metastatic by PSMA PET/CT or whole-body MRI?

options.text: Whether PSMA-PET and whole-body MRI are more sensitive than bone scan
The ideal management of these patients
The accuracy of lymph node metastasis detection
Whether these tests provide prognostic information

questions.explanation: Section 5.8.5 explicitly states that 'the ideal management of patients diagnosed as metastatic by these more sensitive tests is yet unknown.' While sensitivity comparisons and prognostic value are discussed in the text, patient management remains the unresolved question specifically mentioned.
questions.section_title: 5.8.5. Recommendations for staging of prostate cancer
questions.fact: The ideal management of patients diagnosed as metastatic by these more sensitive tests is yet unknown.

questions.question: Based on the follow-up study results cited in section 5.8.5, what specific prognostic benefit does presurgical PSMA-PET provide in the surgical cohort?

options.text: Improved biochemical recurrence-free survival risk assessment
Accurate prediction of overall survival outcomes
Definitive determination of optimal surgical technique
Identification of candidates for neoadjuvant therapy

questions.explanation: Section 5.8.5 specifically states that 'presurgical PSMA-PET is a strong prognostic biomarker, improving BCR-FS risk assessment.' The guideline references initial results from follow-up of the surgical cohort in the multicentre prospective phase III imaging trial, but does not mention overall survival prediction, surgical technique determination, or neoadjuvant therapy selection.
questions.section_title: 5.8.5. Recommendations for staging of prostate cancer
questions.fact: Initial results of a follow-up study of the surgical cohort in the multicentre prospective phase III imaging trial demonstrate that presurgical PSMA-PET is a strong prognostic biomarker, improving BCR-FS risk assessment.

questions.question: According to EAU guidelines on prostate cancer, which risk group requires prebiopsy magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) for local staging information?
questions.explanation: The guideline states that for 'Any risk group staging,' prebiopsy MRI should be used for local staging information. This applies universally across all risk categories, making it a broad recommendation rather than risk-specific.
questions.fact: Use prebiopsy magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) for local staging information. Weak recommendation for Any risk group staging.

questions.question: What is the recommendation strength for additional imaging in low-risk and favourable intermediate-risk localised prostate cancer?
questions.explanation: The guideline explicitly states 'Do not use additional imaging for staging purposes' with a Strong recommendation for low-risk and favourable intermediate-risk localised disease. This strong rating indicates high confidence in the recommendation.
questions.fact: Do not use additional imaging for staging purposes. Strong recommendation for Low-risk and favourable intermediate-risk localised disease.

questions.question: For patients with high-risk localised disease or locally advanced disease, what imaging modality is recommended if PSMA PET/CT is not available?

options.text: Cross-sectional abdominopelvic imaging and a bone scan
Chest X-ray and basic blood tests

questions.explanation: The guideline specifies that for high-risk disease, metastatic screening should use PSMA PET/CT 'if available, or at least cross-sectional abdominopelvic imaging and a bone-scan.' The bone scan is essential for detecting skeletal metastases.
questions.fact: Perform metastatic screening using PSMA PET/CT, if available, or at least cross-sectional abdominopelvic imaging and a bone-scan. Strong recommendation for High-risk localised disease/locally advanced disease.

questions.question: What is the recommendation strength for imaging in unfavourable intermediate-risk prostate cancer?
questions.explanation: The guideline provides a Weak recommendation for PSMA PET/CT or cross-sectional imaging with bone scan in unfavourable intermediate-risk disease. This weaker strength suggests greater uncertainty compared to the strong recommendations for other risk categories.
questions.fact: Perform prostate-specific antigen positron emission tomography/computed tomography (PSMA PET/CT), if available, to increase accuracy, or at least cross-sectional abdominopelvic imaging and a bone scan. Weak recommendation for Unfavourable intermediate-risk disease.

questions.question: First-generation Asian immigrants in the USA have approximately how much risk of PCa compared to their US-born Asian-descendant compatriots?
questions.explanation: Section 3.2.1 states that first-generation Asian immigrants in the USA have approximately half the risk of PCa when compared to their US-born Asian-descendant compatriots, suggesting a role for environmental and/or dietary factors.
questions.fact: First-generation Asian immigrants in the USA have approximately half the risk of PCa when compared to their US-born Asian-descendant compatriots

questions.question: According to the guidelines, which of the following is classified as a hereditary risk factor for prostate cancer?
questions.explanation: Section 3.2.1 explicitly categorises known genetic mutations as a hereditary risk factor, whereas metabolic syndrome, dietary factors, and obesity are classified as nonhereditary risk factors.
questions.fact: Hereditary risk factors include ethnicity, family history and known genetic mutations

questions.question: These guidelines divide risk factors for prostate cancer into which two categories?
questions.explanation: Section 3.2.1 states that these guidelines divide risk factors into hereditary (ethnicity, family history, known genetic mutations) and nonhereditary (dietary and medical factors, metabolic syndrome, obesity) categories.
questions.fact: These guidelines divide the risk factors into hereditary and nonhereditary

questions.question: According to the 2022 global estimates, how many men were diagnosed with prostate cancer worldwide?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states 'an estimated 1.46 million diagnoses' worldwide in 2022. The 396,792 figure refers to deaths, not diagnoses. This is a direct recall question from Section 3.2.
questions.section_title: 3.2. Aetiology and risk factors for prostate cancer
questions.fact: Prostate cancer is the second-most diagnosed cancer in men, with an estimated 1.46 million diagnoses and 396,792 deaths worldwide in 2022.

questions.question: What is the reported prevalence of prostate cancer at autopsy in men over 79 years of age according to the systematic review cited?
questions.explanation: The text specifically states prevalence increases to 59% (48-71%) by age >79 years. The 41% figure refers to Japanese men aged 71-80, and 5% refers to men under 30 years. This is a direct recall question from Section 3.2.
questions.section_title: 3.2. Aetiology and risk factors for prostate cancer
questions.fact: An SR of autopsy studies reported a prevalence of PCa at age <30 years of 5% (95% confidence interval [CI]: 3-8%), increasing with age to a prevalence of 59% (48-71%) by age >79 years.

questions.question: Which population has the highest age-standardised mortality rate for prostate cancer according to the guideline?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states mortality rates are generally high in populations of African descent (Caribbean: ASR 29, Sub-Saharan Africa: ASRs 14-19), compared to intermediate in the USA and very low in Asia. This is an application question requiring interpretation of mortality data from Section 3.2.
questions.section_title: 3.2. Aetiology and risk factors for prostate cancer
questions.fact: There is relatively less variation in mortality rates worldwide, although rates are generally high in populations of African descent (e.g. Caribbean: ASR of 29, and Sub-Saharan Africa: ASRs ranging between 14 and 19), intermediate in the USA, and very low in Asia.

questions.question: How many basic inherited risk factors are consistently associated with prostate cancer (PCa)?
questions.explanation: Section 3.2.1.a states that there are three basic inherited risk factors consistently associated with PCa. These are ethnicity/family history, rare germline mutations, and common genetic SNPs.
questions.fact: There are three basic inherited risk factors that are consistently associated with PCa: ethnicity/family history, rare germline mutations in various candidate genes and common genetic single-nucleotide polymorphism (SNPs).

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT listed as one of the three basic inherited risk factors for prostate cancer?

options.text: Rare germline mutations in candidate genes

questions.explanation: According to Section 3.2.1.a, the three basic inherited risk factors are ethnicity/family history, rare germline mutations, and common genetic SNPs. Environmental lifestyle factors are not included in this list of inherited risk factors.
questions.fact: There are three basic inherited risk factors that are consistently associated with PCa: ethnicity/family history, rare germline mutations in various candidate genes and common genetic single-nucleotide polymorphism (SNPs).

questions.question: How many individual SNPs have been identified to be associated with prostate cancer risk?
questions.explanation: The guideline text explicitly states that 'Two hundred and sixty-nine individual SNPs have been identified to be associated with PCa risk' in section 3.2.2. This is a direct factual recall from the epidemiological data provided.
questions.fact: Two hundred and sixty-nine individual SNPs have been identified to be associated with PCa risk

questions.question: What is the clinical utility of polygenic risk scores (PRSs) when added to PSA for prostate cancer risk stratification?

options.text: PRSs significantly improve risk stratification when combined with PSA
PRSs can completely replace PSA as a screening tool
PRSs provide no additive prognostic value and cannot be used for risk stratification
PRSs are useful only in patients with family history of prostate cancer

questions.explanation: Section 3.2.2 explicitly states that 'there appears to be no additive prognostic value in the PRSs when added to PSA and PRSs therefore cannot be used for risk stratification.' This indicates that PRSs have no clinical utility when combined with PSA for risk stratification.
questions.fact: There appears to be no additive prognostic value in the PRSs when added to PSA and PRSs therefore cannot be used for risk stratification

questions.question: Which statement best describes the difference between germline genetic mutations and SNPs in relation to prostate cancer?

options.text: Germline mutations are common with low impact, while SNPs are rare with significant impact
Germline mutations are rare with significant impact, while SNPs are very common but each has low individual impact
Both germline mutations and SNPs are equally common with equal impact on PCa risk
Germline mutations have no role in hereditary prostate cancer development

questions.explanation: The guideline in section 3.2.2 clearly distinguishes that 'germline genetic mutations are relatively rare, but with significant impact on PCa risk, SNPs are very common, but each SNP has low impact on the risk of developing PCa.' This contrasts the two genetic risk factors.
questions.fact: Germline genetic mutations are relatively rare, but with significant impact on PCa risk, SNPs are very common, but each SNP has low impact on the risk of developing PCa

questions.question: According to the EAU guidelines, which of the following is the most obvious risk factor for prostate cancer among those less determined by ethnicity and/or heredity?
questions.explanation: The guideline explicitly states that among risk factors less determined by ethnicity and/or heredity, age is the most obvious risk factor for prostate cancer (Section 3.2.2.a).
questions.fact: Age is the most obvious risk factor for PCa among factors less determined by ethnicity and/or heredity

questions.question: The EAU guidelines describe prostate cancer risk factors as being differentiated based on their determination by ethnicity and/or heredity. Which statement best reflects this classification?

options.text: Age represents the most obvious risk factor among those less determined by ethnicity and/or heredity
Dietary factors are the most obvious risk factors regardless of ethnic background
All prostate cancer risk factors are equally influenced by ethnicity and heredity
Heredity has no influence on any prostate cancer risk factors

questions.explanation: The guideline clearly distinguishes between risk factors, noting that some are less determined by ethnicity/heredity, with age being the most obvious example (Section 3.2.2.a).
questions.fact: There are risk factors for PCa which are less determined by ethnicity and/or heredity

questions.question: According to the EAU guidelines, which factors primarily determine the incidence of prostate cancer?

options.text: Age and extent of PSA testing

questions.explanation: The guideline states that 'incidence mainly dependent on age and extent of PSA testing' in the epidemiology and aetiology chapter. This is a direct recall of stated risk factors for prostate cancer incidence.
questions.fact: Incidence of prostate cancer is mainly dependent on age and extent of PSA testing

questions.question: What effect do testosterone supplements have on prostate cancer risk in hypogonadal men according to EAU guidelines?

options.text: They significantly increase the risk of prostate cancer
They do not increase the risk of prostate cancer
They decrease the risk of aggressive prostate cancer
They have a variable effect depending on dosage

questions.explanation: The guideline explicitly states that 'in hypogonadal men, testosterone supplements do not increase the risk of PCa' with Level of Evidence 2a. This is a direct factual statement from the evidence summary.
questions.fact: Testosterone supplements do not increase the risk of PCa in hypogonadal men

questions.question: Based on the current evidence, what is the status of preventive or dietary measures for reducing prostate cancer risk?

options.text: Several specific measures have been proven effective
No conclusive data exist to support specific preventive measures
Regular screening is the only proven preventive strategy

questions.explanation: The guideline states that 'no conclusive data exist which could support specific preventive or dietary measures aimed at reducing the risk of developing PCa' with Level of Evidence 1a. This directly addresses the lack of conclusive evidence for specific preventive interventions.
questions.fact: No conclusive data exist which could support specific preventive or dietary measures aimed at reducing the risk of developing PCa

questions.question: According to EAU guidelines, which of the following is associated with prostate cancer incidence and prognosis?
questions.explanation: The guideline explicitly states that 'a variety of dietary/exogenous/environmental factors have been associated with PCa incidence and prognosis.' This summarizes the multifactorial nature of prostate cancer risk factors beyond just genetic or hormonal influences.
questions.fact: A variety of dietary/exogenous/environmental factors have been associated with PCa incidence and prognosis

questions.question: According to the EAU guidelines, what is the magnitude of risk reduction for prostate cancer with 5-alpha-reductase inhibitors (5-ARIs)?

options.text: 25% reduction only for ISUP Grade Group 1 cancer

questions.explanation: Section 3.2.2.c.1 states that 5-ARIs have the potential of preventing or delaying the development of PCa by decreasing the risk by 25% but only for ISUP GG 1 cancer. This is a specific limitation that should be noted.
questions.fact: 5-ARIs decrease the risk of PCa by 25% but only for ISUP GG 1 cancer

questions.question: Which statement is correct regarding the regulatory status of 5-alpha-reductase inhibitors for prostate cancer chemoprevention in Europe?

options.text: Finasteride is approved by EMA for chemoprevention
Dutasteride is approved by EMA for chemoprevention
All available 5-ARIs are approved by EMA for chemoprevention
None of the available 5-ARIs have been approved by EMA for chemoprevention

questions.explanation: Section 3.2.2.c.1 explicitly states that none of the available 5-ARIs have been approved by the European Medicines Agency (EMA) for chemoprevention. This regulatory status is important when considering preventive use.
questions.fact: None of the available 5-ARIs have been approved by EMA for chemoprevention

questions.question: According to pooled analysis data, what is the prostate cancer risk for men with very low concentrations of free testosterone (lowest 10%)?
questions.explanation: Section 3.2.2.c.2 reports that a pooled analysis showed men with very low concentrations of free testosterone (lowest 10%) have a below average risk with an odds ratio of 0.77 for prostate cancer.
questions.fact: Men with very low concentrations of free testosterone (lowest 10%) have a below average risk (OR: 0.77) of PCa

questions.question: What is the effect of testosterone supplementation on prostate cancer risk in hypogonadal men?

options.text: Increases the risk of developing prostate cancer
Decreases the risk of developing prostate cancer
Does not increase the risk of developing prostate cancer
Significantly increases high-grade cancer risk only

questions.explanation: Section 3.2.2.c.2 states that hypogonadal men receiving testosterone supplements do not have an increased risk of developing PCa. This addresses a common concern regarding testosterone replacement therapy.
questions.fact: Hypogonadal men receiving testosterone supplements do not have an increased risk of developing PCa

questions.question: What is the odds ratio for prostate cancer risk associated with the highest tertile of plasma chlordecone concentration?
questions.explanation: The section states that plasma concentration of the estrogenic insecticide chlordecone is associated with an increase in the risk of PCa with an OR of 1.77 for the highest tertile of values above the limit of detection.
questions.section_title: 3.2.3. Summary of evidence for epidemiology and aetiology
questions.fact: OR: 1.77 for highest tertile of values above the limit of detection

questions.question: In men with inflammatory bowel disease, what hazard ratio for ISUP Grade Group ≥2 prostate cancer was found compared to the general population?
questions.explanation: The guideline explicitly states that a significantly higher rate of ISUP GG ≥2 PCa was found in men with inflammatory bowel disease with a hazard ratio of 4.04 compared to the general population.
questions.section_title: 3.2.3. Summary of evidence for epidemiology and aetiology
questions.fact: HR: 4.04 for ISUP GG ≥2 PCa in men with IBD

questions.question: Which procedure has been disproved as a risk factor for prostate cancer according to the guideline?
questions.explanation: The guideline explicitly states that a number of factors previously linked to an increased risk of PCa have been disproved, including vasectomy.
questions.section_title: 3.2.3. Summary of evidence for epidemiology and aetiology
questions.fact: Vasectomy has been disproved as a risk factor for PCa

questions.question: According to the guideline, what percentage reduction in prostate cancer risk is associated with higher ejaculation frequency (≥21 times/month vs 4-7 times)?
questions.explanation: The guideline states that higher ejaculation frequency (≥21 times a month vs 4 to 7 times) has been associated with a 20% lower risk of PCa.
questions.section_title: 3.2.3. Summary of evidence for epidemiology and aetiology
questions.fact: 20% lower risk of PCa with ≥21 times/month vs 4-7 times ejaculation frequency

questions.question: For which specific type of inflammatory bowel disease did the meta-analysis show a clear association with prostate cancer risk?
questions.explanation: The guideline states that while results on IBD overall were mixed in the systematic review, except for the subgroup of ulcerative colitis, where a clear association could be seen.
questions.section_title: 3.2.3. Summary of evidence for epidemiology and aetiology
questions.fact: Clear association with PCa seen specifically in ulcerative colitis subgroup

questions.question: What is the relative risk of prostate cancer death associated with current cigarette smoking?
questions.explanation: The guideline explicitly states that current cigarette smoking was associated with an increased risk of PCa death with a relative risk of 1.24.
questions.section_title: 3.2.3. Summary of evidence for epidemiology and aetiology
questions.fact: RR: 1.24 for PCa death with current cigarette smoking

questions.question: According to the guideline, how should patients included in an AS (Active Surveillance) programme be monitored?

options.text: According to the recommendations presented in Section 6.2.1.c
According to annual PSA testing only
According to the recommendations in Section 7.1

questions.explanation: Section 7.3 explicitly states that patients included in an AS programme must be monitored according to the recommendations presented in Section 6.2.1.c.
questions.section_title: 7.3. Follow-up: After local treatment with curative intent
questions.fact: Patients included in an AS programme must be monitored according to the recommendations presented in Section 6.2.1.c.

questions.question: Which of the following is classified as local treatment with curative intent according to the EAU guidelines?

options.text: Radical prostatectomy (RP) or radiotherapy (RT), including IMRT plus IGRT, LDR-BT or HDR-BT, with or without hormonal therapy

questions.explanation: According to Section 7.3.2, local treatment is explicitly defined as RP or RT, including IMRT plus IGRT, LDR-BT or HDR-BT, or any combination including neoadjuvant and adjuvant hormonal therapy. HIFU, cryosurgery and focal therapy are classified as unestablished alternative treatments.
questions.fact: Local treatment is defined as RP or RT, either by IMRT plus IGRT or LDR- or HDR-BT or any combination of these, including neoadjuvant and adjuvant hormonal therapy.

questions.question: What is generally considered a sign of disease recurrence following local treatment for prostate cancer?
questions.explanation: Section 7.3.2 explicitly states that 'a confirmed rising PSA is considered a sign of disease recurrence'. This is the defining criterion mentioned for identifying recurrence.
questions.fact: In general, a confirmed rising PSA is considered a sign of disease recurrence.

questions.question: How do unestablished alternative treatments such as HIFU, cryosurgery and focal therapy follow-up recommendations?

options.text: They follow the general principles presented in the follow-up section
They require more intensive monitoring than conventional local treatments
They have dedicated separate follow-up protocols
They do not require routine follow-up

questions.explanation: According to Section 7.3.2, unestablished alternative treatments including HIFU, cryosurgery and focal therapy options do follow the general principles as presented in this section, despite not being classified as established local treatments.
questions.fact: Unestablished alternative treatments such as HIFU, cryosurgery and focal therapy options do follow the general principles as presented in this section.

questions.question: What type of management does watchful waiting refer to according to the EAU guidelines?
questions.explanation: The guideline text explicitly states that 'Watchful waiting refers to conservative management for patients deemed unsuitable for curative treatment from the outset.'
questions.fact: Watchful waiting refers to conservative management

questions.question: At what point are patients on watchful waiting managed with palliative treatment according to their symptoms?

options.text: From the time of initial diagnosis
When local or systemic progression with disease-related complaints develops
When PSA levels begin to rise

questions.explanation: The guideline specifies that patients are clinically 'watched' for the development of local or systemic progression with (imminent) disease-related complaints, at which stage they are then treated palliatively according to their symptoms.
questions.fact: Palliative treatment is initiated upon development of local or systemic progression with disease-related complaints

questions.question: Which patients are considered suitable for watchful waiting according to the guideline?

options.text: Patients with aggressive disease requiring immediate treatment
Patients with localised disease suitable for curative treatment
Patients deemed unsuitable for curative treatment from the outset
Patients with biochemical recurrence after initial therapy

questions.explanation: The guideline text clearly states that watchful waiting is for 'patients deemed unsuitable for curative treatment from the outset.'
questions.fact: Watchful waiting is for patients unsuitable for curative treatment from the outset

questions.question: What are the primary focuses of the first post-treatment clinic visit after curative prostate cancer treatment?

options.text: Detecting treatment-related complications and assisting patients in coping with their new situation
Scheduling routine imaging scans and conducting PSA testing
Discussing long-term survival statistics and diet recommendations
Planning adjuvant therapy and arranging support group referrals

questions.explanation: The guideline states in section 7.3.3 that the first post-treatment clinic visit focuses on detecting treatment-related complications and assisting patients in coping with their new situation, apart from providing information on the pathological analysis.
questions.fact: The first post-treatment clinic visit focuses on detecting treatment-related complications and assisting patients in coping with their new situation

questions.question: According to the guideline, what psychological condition are men with prostate cancer at increased risk of developing?
questions.explanation: The guideline explicitly states in section 7.3.3 that men with PCa are at increased risk of depression and attention to mental health status is required.
questions.fact: Men with PCa are at increased risk of depression

questions.question: Which factors may prompt changing the follow-up schedule according to the guideline?

options.text: Geographic location and distance to hospital
Insurance coverage and healthcare system protocols
Patient family history of other cancers

questions.explanation: The guideline states in section 7.3.3 that tumour or patient characteristics may prompt changing the follow-up schedule.
questions.fact: Tumour or patient characteristics may prompt changing the follow-up schedule

questions.question: The follow-up programme allows the introduction of additional or salvage treatments based on which of the following criteria?

options.text: Expected life-expectancy, patient symptoms, and EAU risk categories for biochemical recurrence
Patient age, family history, and genetic test results
Tumour grade and histological subtype only

questions.explanation: The guideline specifies in section 7.3.3 that follow-up allows the introduction of additional/salvage treatments should that be considered necessary given the expected life-expectancy, patient symptoms, and EAU risk categories for biochemical recurrence.
questions.fact: Follow-up allows the introduction of additional/salvage treatments based on expected life-expectancy, patient symptoms, and EAU risk categories for biochemical recurrence

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT explicitly stated as a component of the discussion between patient and clinician regarding follow-up?

options.text: How follow-up will be communicated to the patient

questions.explanation: The guideline specifically states that the discussion between patient and clinician must include 'modalities, periodicity and how this will be communicated to the patient' (Section 7.1). Cost of procedures is not mentioned as part of this required discussion.
questions.fact: The discussion must include modalities, periodicity and how the follow-up will be communicated to the patient.

questions.question: According to the guideline, what psychological consequence may occur in patients who are not properly prepared for potential follow-up outcomes?

options.text: Unnecessary fear or panic from even small PSA increases

questions.explanation: Section 7.1 explicitly warns that 'even a very small increase in PSA levels can cause unnecessary fear, even panic' when patients are not prepared for various potential outcomes of the follow-up.
questions.fact: The text states that unprepared patients may experience unnecessary fear or panic from even very small PSA increases.

questions.question: Beyond assessing oncological results and ensuring treatment compliance, follow-up in prostate cancer also serves to provide which of the following to survivors?
questions.explanation: Section 7.1 specifically lists that follow-up provides 'an opportunity to provide psychological support to PCa survivors.' The other options (financial counseling, career advancement, and nutritional meal planning) are not mentioned in the text.
questions.fact: The guideline states that follow-up provides 'an opportunity to provide psychological support to PCa survivors.'

questions.question: What is mandatory at every follow-up visit for prostate cancer according to the guideline?
questions.explanation: The guideline explicitly states that 'A disease-specific history is mandatory at every follow-up visit.'
questions.fact: A disease-specific history is mandatory at every follow-up visit

questions.question: Which of the following components is included in the disease-specific history?
questions.explanation: The guideline explicitly lists the components of the disease-specific history as including 'psychological aspects, signs of disease progression, and treatment-related complications.'
questions.fact: Disease-specific history includes psychological aspects, signs of disease progression, and treatment-related complications

questions.question: According to the guideline, where is the evaluation of treatment-related complications discussed?
questions.explanation: The guideline specifically states that 'Evaluation of treatment-related complications in the post-treatment period is highlighted in Section 8.2.'
questions.fact: Evaluation of treatment-related complications in the post-treatment period is highlighted in Section 8.2

questions.question: When should imaging techniques be omitted in the follow-up of localised prostate cancer?

options.text: When PSA is not rising and the patient is asymptomatic
When the patient has completed adjuvant hormone therapy
When the patient has previously undergone radical prostatectomy
When the patient is on active surveillance protocol

questions.explanation: According to section 7.3.3.d, imaging techniques have no place in routine follow-up of localised prostate cancer as long as PSA is not rising and patients are asymptomatic. Imaging is reserved for situations where findings will affect treatment decisions.
questions.fact: Imaging techniques have no place in routine follow-up of localised PCa, as long as the PSA is not rising and patients are asymptomatic.

questions.question: In which of the following scenarios is imaging justified during follow-up of localised prostate cancer?

options.text: In all patients who have completed curative treatment
During the first year after completing treatment as part of standard protocol
In case of biochemical recurrence or when patients are symptomatic
At every scheduled follow-up appointment regardless of PSA levels

questions.explanation: Section 7.3.3.d states that imaging is only justified when the findings will affect treatment decisions, specifically in cases of BCR (biochemical recurrence) or in patients with symptoms.
questions.fact: Imaging is only justified in patients for whom the findings will affect treatment decisions, either in case of BCR or in patients with symptoms.

questions.question: What is the primary criterion that determines whether imaging should be performed during follow-up of localised prostate cancer?

options.text: The time elapsed since treatment completion
Whether findings will affect treatment decisions
The Gleason score at initial diagnosis

questions.explanation: According to section 7.3.3.d, imaging is only justified in patients for whom the findings will affect treatment decisions, either in case of biochemical recurrence or in patients with symptoms.
questions.fact: Imaging is only justified in patients for whom the findings will affect treatment decisions, either in case of BCR or in patients with symptoms.

questions.question: In which specific patient population has PSA-negative local recurrence after curative treatment been documented?

options.text: Patients with PSA levels below 2 ng/mL

questions.explanation: The text states that PSA-negative local recurrence 'has only been proven in patients with unfavourable, undifferentiated tumours' (Section 7.3.3.c). This is a rare occurrence overall but documented specifically in this high-risk subgroup.
questions.section_title: 7.3.3.c. Transrectal ultrasound, bone scintigraphy, CT, MRI and PET/CT
questions.fact: This has only been proven in patients with unfavourable, undifferentiated tumours

questions.question: What was the outcome of using DRE alone to detect local recurrence in a series of 1,118 prostatectomy patients?

options.text: DRE detected recurrence in 15% of patients with stable PSA
No local histologically proven recurrence was found by DRE alone
DRE was superior to PSA in detecting local recurrence
DRE identified all recurrences when PSA exceeded 0.2 ng/mL

questions.explanation: According to Section 7.3.3.c, 'In a series of 1,118 prostatectomy patients, no local histologically proven recurrence was found by DRE alone.' This evidence suggests limited utility of DRE as a standalone test after radical prostatectomy.
questions.section_title: 7.3.3.c. Transrectal ultrasound, bone scintigraphy, CT, MRI and PET/CT
questions.fact: In a series of 1,118 prostatectomy patients, no local histologically proven recurrence was found by DRE alone

questions.question: In a study of 899 radiotherapy patients, what did DRE fail to detect in the absence of a rising PSA?

options.text: Any local recurrence without concurrent PSA elevation
Biochemical recurrence defined by Phoenix criteria

questions.explanation: Section 7.3.3.c reports that DRE 'failed to detect any local recurrence in the absence of a rising PSA in a series of 899 patients.' This finding questioned the role of DRE as a first-line examination without PSA guidance in post-radiotherapy follow-up.
questions.section_title: 7.3.3.c. Transrectal ultrasound, bone scintigraphy, CT, MRI and PET/CT
questions.fact: DRE failed to detect any local recurrence in the absence of a rising PSA in a series of 899 patients

questions.question: According to current evidence, what is considered the most useful combination for first-line examination in follow-up after radiation therapy?

options.text: Prostate-specific antigen and digital rectal examination

questions.explanation: Section 7.3.3.c states that 'Prostate-specific antigen and DRE comprise the most useful combination for first-line examination in follow-up after RT.' This remains the recommended first-line approach despite evidence questioning DRE's standalone utility.
questions.section_title: 7.3.3.c. Transrectal ultrasound, bone scintigraphy, CT, MRI and PET/CT
questions.fact: Prostate-specific antigen and DRE comprise the most useful combination for first-line examination in follow-up after RT

questions.question: After radical prostatectomy, which single test may be the most efficient examination required for detecting recurrence?
questions.explanation: The guideline in Section 7.3.3.c states that 'PSA measurement may be the most efficient test required after RP.' This conclusion is supported by the finding that DRE alone failed to detect any histologically proven recurrence in 1,118 prostatectomy patients.
questions.section_title: 7.3.3.c. Transrectal ultrasound, bone scintigraphy, CT, MRI and PET/CT
questions.fact: PSA measurement may be the most efficient test required after RP

questions.question: What is the recommended frequency for PSA measurement and disease-specific history during the first three years after curative intent treatment for prostate cancer?
questions.explanation: Section 7.3.5 states that 'Prostate-specific antigen measurement and disease-specific history are recommended every six months for a period of three years, and then annually.' This establishes the standard follow-up schedule for the initial high-risk period after treatment.
questions.section_title: 7.3.5. Summary of evidence and recommendations for follow-up after treatment with curative intent
questions.fact: Prostate-specific antigen measurement and disease-specific history are recommended every six months for a period of three years, and then annually.

questions.question: According to the guideline, what is the typical timeframe within which most patients who fail treatment for prostate cancer do so after local therapy?
questions.explanation: Section 7.3.5 explicitly states that 'Most patients who fail treatment for PCa do so within seven years after local therapy.' This timeframe guides the overall duration of intensive follow-up protocols.
questions.section_title: 7.3.5. Summary of evidence and recommendations for follow-up after treatment with curative intent
questions.fact: Most patients who fail treatment for PCa do so within seven years after local therapy.

questions.question: In men with PSA-DT less than ten months after radical prostatectomy who deferred treatment, what was the observed median metastasis-free survival from the time of surgery?
questions.explanation: Section 7.3.5 reports that 'Even in men with a PSA-DT less than ten months after RP who choose to defer treatment, a median MFS of 192 months and OS of 204 months from RP was observed.' This demonstrates the relatively favourable long-term outcomes even in high-risk patients.
questions.section_title: 7.3.5. Summary of evidence and recommendations for follow-up after treatment with curative intent
questions.fact: Even in men with a PSA-DT less than ten months after RP who choose to defer treatment, a median MFS of 192 months and OS of 204 months from RP was observed.

questions.question: What type of tools have been developed and validated specifically for men with prostate cancer to assess the impact of treatment on quality of life?
questions.explanation: The section explicitly states that 'specific tools or patient-reported outcome measures (PROMs) have been developed and validated for men with PCa.' These questionnaires assess issues after PCa diagnosis and treatment and generate scores reflecting HRQoL impact.
questions.fact: Specific tools or 'patient-reported outcome measures' (PROMs) have been developed and validated for men with PCa.

questions.question: According to the guideline, what is the essential requirement for measuring functional outcomes of any prostate cancer treatment?

options.text: Measurements must be taken by specialists only
Results must be compared across international centres
Outcomes must be measured and registered by validated and reproducible methods
Functional outcomes must be reported within 24 hours of treatment

questions.explanation: The text explicitly states 'it is essential that the functional outcomes of any treatment are measured and registered by validated and reproducible methods.' This requirement applies for quality control and to help patients choose optimal treatment.
questions.fact: It is essential that the functional outcomes of any treatment are measured and registered by validated and reproducible methods.

questions.question: What do scores generated by patient-reported outcome measures (PROMs) for prostate cancer reflect?

options.text: The impact on perceptions of health-related quality of life

questions.explanation: The section clearly states that PROMs 'generate scores that reflect the impact on perceptions of HRQoL.' HRQoL stands for health-related quality of life, which these tools specifically measure from the patient's perspective.
questions.fact: These questionnaires assess common issues after PCa diagnosis and treatment and generate scores that reflect the impact on perceptions of HRQoL.

questions.question: According to the EAU guidelines, how is castrate resistance defined in prostate cancer?

options.text: Rising PSA levels despite ADT treatment
