PCa progression despite a testosterone level < 50ng/dL
Failure to achieve castrate testosterone levels within 6 months
Metastatic disease confirmed by imaging after starting ADT

questions.explanation: Section 7.4.2 explicitly states that castrate resistance is defined as 'PCa progression despite a testosterone level < 50ng/dL'. This definition is specific to the numerical threshold mentioned in the text.
questions.fact: Castrate resistance is defined as PCa progression despite a testosterone level < 50ng/dL.

questions.question: What is the primary mandatory requirement for detecting disease- and treatment-related complaints in patients on ADT?

options.text: Frequent PSA monitoring with trend analysis
Regular clinical follow-up that cannot be replaced by imaging or laboratory tests alone
Regular testosterone level checks to ensure suppression

questions.explanation: Section 7.4.2 explicitly states that 'To detect disease- and treatment-related complaints, regular clinical follow-up is mandatory and cannot be replaced by imaging or laboratory tests alone.' This highlights the essential role of direct clinical assessment.
questions.fact: Regular clinical follow-up is mandatory and cannot be replaced by imaging or laboratory tests alone.

questions.question: According to the EAU guidelines, which of the following methods is NOT used for testosterone blockage or suppression in ADT?
questions.explanation: Section 7.4.2 states that testosterone blockage/suppression is achieved 'either by drugs (LHRH agonists or antagonists) or orchidectomy.' 5-alpha reductase inhibitors are not mentioned as a method for achieving castration levels.
questions.fact: Testosterone blockage/suppression is achieved by drugs (LHRH agonists or antagonists) or orchidectomy.

questions.question: How frequently should patients be evaluated after initiation of androgen deprivation therapy (ADT)?
questions.explanation: According to Section 7.4.3, after the initiation of ADT, it is recommended that patients be evaluated every three to six months, which must be individualized to each patient.
questions.section_title: 7.4.3. General follow-up of men on hormonal therapy
questions.fact: It is recommended that patients be evaluated every three to six months after initiation of ADT.

questions.question: For patients receiving a combination of ADT and androgen receptor pathway inhibitors (ARPI), what is the recommended follow-up frequency during the first three months?
questions.explanation: Section 7.4.3 states that for patients receiving a combination of ADT and other potent medication such as ARPI, the frequency of follow-up is monthly for the first three months.
questions.section_title: 7.4.3. General follow-up of men on hormonal therapy
questions.fact: For patients receiving a combination of ADT and ARPI, the frequency of follow-up is monthly for the first three months.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT one of the main objectives of follow-up in patients receiving ADT?

options.text: To guide treatment at the time point of clinical progression

questions.explanation: Section 7.4.3 lists the main objectives of follow-up as: ensuring treatment compliance, monitoring treatment response, detecting and managing side effects early, and guiding treatment at clinical progression. Determining surgical candidacy is not mentioned.
questions.section_title: 7.4.3. General follow-up of men on hormonal therapy
questions.fact: The main objectives of follow-up are to ensure treatment compliance, monitor treatment response, detect and manage side effects early, and guide treatment at clinical progression.

questions.question: Which of the following components must be included in the routine follow-up of asymptomatic patients receiving first-line hormonal treatment?

options.text: Disease-specific history and digital rectal examination

questions.explanation: According to section 7.4, there is a Strong recommendation to routinely follow-up asymptomatic patients by obtaining at least a disease-specific history and a prostate-specific antigen measurement.
questions.section_title: 7.4. Follow-up: During first line hormonal treatment (androgen sensitive period)
questions.fact: Routinely follow-up asymptomatic patients by obtaining at least a disease-specific history and a prostate-specific antigen measurement.

questions.question: What is the PSA threshold that best predicts further metastases after radical prostatectomy?
questions.explanation: The guideline text explicitly states that the PSA threshold that best predicts further metastases after RP is >0.4ng/mL. This is a specific numerical threshold mentioned in section 7.4.
questions.section_title: 7.4. Follow-up: During first line hormonal treatment (androgen sensitive period)
questions.fact: The PSA threshold that best predicts further metastases after RP is >0.4ng/mL

questions.question: When should imaging be performed in a patient with prostate cancer recurrence during the androgen-sensitive period?

options.text: Only if the result will affect treatment planning
At every follow-up visit regardless of symptoms
Only when PSA levels exceed 10 ng/mL
At the first sign of PSA elevation

questions.explanation: Section 7.4 contains a Strong recommendation stating that at recurrence, imaging should only be performed if the result will affect treatment planning, avoiding unnecessary investigations.
questions.section_title: 7.4. Follow-up: During first line hormonal treatment (androgen sensitive period)
questions.fact: At recurrence, only perform imaging if the result will affect treatment planning.

questions.question: At what intervals should patients under androgen deprivation therapy (ADT) have follow-up monitoring of serum testosterone, creatinine, liver function and metabolic parameters?
questions.explanation: Section 7.4.3.a states that patients under ADT require regular follow-up, including monitoring of serum testosterone, creatinine, liver function and metabolic parameters at three-to six-month intervals.
questions.fact: at three-to six-month intervals

questions.question: Which of the following parameters is NOT listed as part of the routine laboratory monitoring for patients under ADT in this guideline section?
questions.explanation: Section 7.4.3.a specifies that routine monitoring includes serum testosterone, creatinine, liver function and metabolic parameters. Bone density is mentioned separately as requiring assessment before and during treatment, but is not part of the routine laboratory monitoring panel.
questions.fact: monitoring of serum testosterone, creatinine, liver function and metabolic parameters

questions.question: Why is assessment of bone density considered essential in men receiving ADT?

options.text: To monitor for prostate cancer bone metastases
Because ADT-induced bone density loss increases the risk of fractures
To assess response to anti-androgen therapy

questions.explanation: Section 7.4.3.a states that androgen-deprivation therapy induced bone density loss increases the risk of fractures, therefore assessment of bone density before and during treatment with ADT is essential.
questions.fact: Androgen-deprivation therapy induced bone density loss increases the risk of fractures

questions.question: What is the reported percentage of men who may experience temporary testosterone surges during long-term ADT treatment?
questions.explanation: Section 7.4.3b states that up to 24% of men may experience temporary testosterone surges (testosterone > 50ng/dL) during long-term treatment, referred to as 'acute on-chronic effect' or 'breakthrough response'.
questions.fact: Up to 24% of men may experience temporary testosterone surges (testosterone > 50ng/dL) during long-term treatment.

questions.question: What is the suggested frequency for testosterone level assessment to ensure castration is achieved and maintained?
questions.explanation: Section 7.4.3b suggests a three- to six-month testosterone level assessment to ensure castration is achieved (especially during medical castration) and maintained.
questions.fact: A three- to six-month testosterone level assessment has been suggested to ensure castration is achieved and maintained.

questions.question: What percentage of patients fail to achieve castrate testosterone levels on ADT?
questions.explanation: Section 7.4.3b explicitly states that approximately 13-38% of patients fail to achieve castrate testosterone levels (< 20ng/dL or < 50ng/dL) when receiving medical castration.
questions.fact: Approximately 13-38% of patients fail to achieve castrate testosterone levels.

questions.question: How frequently should transaminase levels be checked in all men receiving combined ADT according to the guideline?
questions.explanation: According to section 7.4.3.c, transaminase levels should be checked at least once yearly in all men receiving combined ADT. More frequent monitoring is recommended only during the first six months after treatment initiation.
questions.fact: Transaminase levels should be checked at least once yearly in all men receiving combined ADT.

questions.question: During which period were liver function abnormalities most commonly observed in large clinical trials?

options.text: First six months after treatment initiation
After the first year of treatment
During the second year of treatment

questions.explanation: Section 7.4.3.c states that liver function abnormalities were most commonly observed during the first six months after treatment initiation in large clinical trials, which is why more frequent monitoring is warranted during this period.
questions.fact: Liver function abnormalities were most commonly observed during this period in large clinical trials.

questions.question: Which drug is specifically mentioned as requiring more intensive monitoring due to its higher hepatotoxic potential?
questions.explanation: Section 7.4.3.c explicitly states that more intensive monitoring is advised for agents known to have higher hepatotoxic potential, such as abiraterone acetate. The guideline notes that monitoring frequency may vary depending on the specific drug used.
questions.fact: More intensive monitoring advised for agents known to have higher hepatotoxic potential, such as abiraterone acetate.

questions.question: Why is estimated glomerular filtration rate (eGFR) monitoring recommended during ADT for prostate cancer?

options.text: Because an increase in eGFR may be linked to ureteral obstruction or bladder retention
Because ADT commonly causes acute kidney injury requiring dose adjustment
Because eGFR declines predict cardiovascular events in this population
Because dose adjustments of ADT are based on renal function measurements

questions.explanation: According to Section 7.4.3.d, eGFR monitoring is good clinical practice because an increase may be linked to ureteral obstruction or bladder retention. This monitoring helps detect potential complications early.
questions.fact: Estimated glomerular filtration rate monitoring is good clinical practice because an increase may be linked to ureteral obstruction or bladder retention.

questions.question: According to the guideline, what is the clinical significance of a significant decline in haemoglobin after three months of ADT?

options.text: It is independently associated with shorter progression-free and overall survival rates
It is a normal physiological adaptation that resolves without intervention
It is only clinically relevant if accompanied by symptoms of fatigue
It indicates resistance to hormonal therapy requiring treatment change

questions.explanation: Section 7.4.3.d states that a significant decline in haemoglobin after three months of ADT is independently associated with shorter progression-free and overall survival rates. This finding may explain fatigue, though other causes should be considered.
questions.fact: A significant decline after three months of ADT is independently associated with shorter progression-free and OS rates

questions.question: Based on the SPCG-5 study data cited in the guideline, what is the prognostic significance of the timing of haemoglobin decrease after ADT initiation?

options.text: An early decrease at three months predicts better survival, while a decrease beyond six months is associated with poor outcome
A decrease at any timepoint predicts poor overall survival regardless of timing
Early decreases are associated with poorer prognosis compared to later decreases
Timing of haemoglobin decrease has no prognostic significance according to SPCG-5 data

questions.explanation: According to Section 7.4.3.d, the SPCG-5 study demonstrated that an early decrease in haemoglobin at three months after ADT initiation predicted better survival, whereas a decrease beyond six months was associated with poor outcome in this patient population.
questions.fact: An early decrease in haemoglobin three months after ADT initiation predicted better survival, whereas a decrease beyond six months was associated with poor outcome in the SPCG-5 population

questions.question: According to the guideline, which baseline investigations are recommended for ALL patients prior to starting androgen deprivation therapy (ADT)?

options.text: Fasting glucose, HbA1c, and blood lipid levels
Bone mineral density scan and serum calcium
Liver function tests and complete blood count

questions.explanation: The guideline explicitly states that all patients should be screened for diabetes by checking fasting glucose and HbA1c at baseline and routinely, in addition to checking blood lipid levels. These metabolic assessments are required before and during ADT.
questions.fact: All patients should be screened for diabetes by checking fasting glucose and HbA1c (at baseline and routinely) in addition to checking blood lipid levels.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT listed as a severe complication of androgen suppression?
questions.explanation: The guideline states the most severe complications of androgen suppression are metabolic syndrome, cardiovascular morbidity, mental health problems, and bone resorption. Nephrotoxicity is not mentioned as a complication of ADT in this section.
questions.fact: The most severe complications of androgen suppression are metabolic syndrome, cardiovascular morbidity, mental health problems and bone resorption.

questions.question: A patient on androgen deprivation therapy is found to have impaired glucose tolerance. According to the guideline, what is the recommended management?
questions.explanation: The guideline explicitly states that men with impaired glucose tolerance and/or diabetes should be referred for an endocrine consultation. This is part of the metabolic monitoring protocol for patients on ADT.
questions.fact: Men with impaired glucose tolerance and/or diabetes should be referred for an endocrine consultation.

questions.question: What is the minimum cardiac investigation required before starting ADT in a man with no history of cardiovascular disease?
questions.explanation: The guideline states that prior to starting ADT, an ECG should be done. A cardiology consultation is recommended as a minimum in men with a history of cardiovascular disease, and depending on the combination drug planned, also an ECHO. For patients without cardiovascular history, ECG is the minimum requirement.
questions.fact: Prior to starting ADT, an ECG should be done. A cardiology consultation is recommended, as a minimum, in men with a history of cardiovascular disease, and depending on the combination drug planned, also an ECHO.

questions.question: The guideline states that cardiorespiratory fitness decreased even after how many months of ADT?
questions.explanation: The guideline explicitly mentions that cardiorespiratory fitness decreased even after six months of ADT, highlighting the impact of androgen deprivation on physical function.
questions.fact: Cardiorespiratory fitness decreased even after six months of ADT.

questions.question: According to the EAU guidelines, at what PSA threshold should imaging be considered in patients with androgen-sensitive non-metastasised prostate cancer?
questions.explanation: Section 7.4.4.b states that imaging should be considered when PSA is rising > 2ng/mL in patients with androgen-sensitive non-metastasised PCa.
questions.fact: Imaging should be considered when PSA is rising > 2ng/mL

questions.question: What role does prostate-specific antigen (PSA) play in the follow-up of androgen-sensitive non-metastasised prostate cancer?

options.text: PSA is a key marker for following the course of the disease
PSA is only used for initial diagnosis
PSA is primarily used to assess response to chemotherapy
PSA has limited utility in disease monitoring

questions.explanation: Section 7.4.4.b explicitly states that PSA is a key marker for following the course of androgen-sensitive non-metastasised PCa.
questions.fact: Prostate-specific antigen is a key marker for following the course of androgen-sensitive non-metastasised PCa

questions.question: In which clinical scenario should imaging be considered during follow-up of non-metastasised prostate cancer according to the EAU guidelines?

options.text: When PSA is rising > 2 ng/mL or in case of symptoms suggestive of metastasis
Only when PSA exceeds 10 ng/mL regardless of symptoms
Only when the patient reports bone pain
At every routine clinic visit regardless of PSA levels or symptoms

questions.explanation: Section 7.4.4.b specifies two indications for imaging: when PSA is rising > 2ng/mL or in case of symptoms suggestive of metastasis.
questions.fact: Imaging should be considered when PSA is rising > 2ng/mL or in case of symptoms suggestive of metastasis

questions.question: Which imaging modality offers higher sensitivity for detecting progression in men with hormone-sensitive metastatic prostate cancer under ADT?
questions.explanation: Section 7.4.5 states that the choice of imaging modality is between PSMA-PET/CT, which offers higher sensitivity, and conventional imaging. PSMA-PET/CT's superior sensitivity may allow detection of progression earlier than conventional imaging.
questions.section_title: 7.4.5. Methods for follow-up in men under ADT for hormone-sensitive metastatic PCa
questions.fact: The choice of imaging modality is between PSMA-PET/CT, which offers higher sensitivity, and conventional imaging (CI) with CT or MRI and bone scan

questions.question: According to current guidelines, how should asymptomatic patients with stable PSA levels during ADT be managed regarding imaging?

options.text: No imaging is required as PSA is stable
Imaging is only needed if symptoms develop
PSA testing alone is sufficient for monitoring

questions.explanation: Section 7.4.5 explicitly states that imaging should be scheduled regularly, including in asymptomatic patients with stable PSA. The earlier recommendation that asymptomatic patients with stable PSA do not require further imaging may no longer hold true.
questions.section_title: 7.4.5. Methods for follow-up in men under ADT for hormone-sensitive metastatic PCa
questions.fact: Imaging should be scheduled regularly, including in asymptomatic patients with stable PSA, because the earlier recommendation that asymptomatic patients with a stable PSA level do not require further imaging may no longer hold true

questions.question: In which patient population may PSA levels fail to reflect actual tumour progression despite being stable?

options.text: Patients with only lymph node metastases

questions.explanation: Section 7.4.5 specifically states that in patients with aggressive variants, PSA levels may not reflect tumour progression. This is particularly relevant as it makes regular imaging especially important in this population.
questions.section_title: 7.4.5. Methods for follow-up in men under ADT for hormone-sensitive metastatic PCa
questions.fact: This is particularly the case in patients with aggressive variants when PSA levels may not reflect tumour progression

questions.question: When PSA progression suggests castration-resistant prostate cancer (CRPC) status and treatment modification is being considered, which imaging is currently recommended for restaging?
questions.explanation: Section 7.4.5 states that when PSA progression suggests CRPC status and treatment modification is considered, imaging by means of a bone and CT scan is currently recommended for restaging.
questions.section_title: 7.4.5. Methods for follow-up in men under ADT for hormone-sensitive metastatic PCa
questions.fact: When PSA progression suggests CRPC status and treatment modification is considered, imaging, by means of a bone and CT scan, is currently recommended for restaging

questions.question: According to the guidelines, how does androgen deprivation therapy (ADT) affect the risk of depression in men?

options.text: Men on ADT are slightly more likely to report depression (10-20% increase)
Men on ADT are twice as likely to report depression
Men on ADT are three times more likely to report depression
Men on ADT are half as likely to report depression

questions.explanation: Section 7.4.4 explicitly states that men on ADT are three times more likely to report depression, making this an integral aspect of the follow-up scheme.
questions.section_title: 7.4.4. Follow-up of patients on ADT (non-metastatic mHSPCa)
questions.fact: Androgen deprivation therapy may affect mental and cognitive health and men on ADT are three times more likely to report depression.

questions.question: Within what timeframe after initiating ADT may reduced cognitive performance and fatigue arise, and how may they change over time?

options.text: Within 12 months and worsen progressively without intervention
Within 3 months and persist indefinitely at the same level
Within six months but can improve over time
Only after 12 months and require permanent medication adjustment

questions.explanation: The guideline states that reduced cognitive performance and fatigue may arise within six months after initiation of ADT but can improve over time, indicating a potentially reversible side effect.
questions.section_title: 7.4.4. Follow-up of patients on ADT (non-metastatic mHSPCa)
questions.fact: Reduced cognitive performance and fatigue may arise within six months after initiation of ADT but can improve over time.

questions.question: According to the guideline, what percentage of couples cease sexual activity completely after starting ADT?
questions.explanation: Section 7.4.4 explicitly states that ADT leads to sexual dysfunction, causing greater than 80% of couples to cease sexual activity completely, affecting both patients and partners.
questions.section_title: 7.4.4. Follow-up of patients on ADT (non-metastatic mHSPCa)
questions.fact: Another aspect of starting ADT is that it leads to sexual dysfunction, causing >80% of couples to cease sexual activity completely.

questions.question: What aspect of care should be considered for patients and their partners regarding sexual dysfunction during ADT follow-up?
questions.explanation: The guideline explicitly states that couple counselling should be considered for ADT-related sexual dysfunction, as this aspect affects both patients and their partners.
questions.section_title: 7.4.4. Follow-up of patients on ADT (non-metastatic mHSPCa)
questions.fact: This aspect affects patients as well as their partners, and couple counselling should be considered.

questions.question: According to the guideline, what type of intervention is recommended during ADT follow-up to decrease ADT-related toxicity?
questions.explanation: Section 7.4.4 states that during follow-up, men should be counselled on the beneficial effects of exercise to decrease ADT-related toxicity, linking lifestyle intervention to symptom management.
questions.section_title: 7.4.4. Follow-up of patients on ADT (non-metastatic mHSPCa)
questions.fact: During follow-up, men should be counselled on the beneficial effects of exercise to decrease ADT-related toxicity.

questions.question: According to the guideline, which of the following should be discussed during counseling of metastatic patients receiving hormonal treatment?

options.text: Early signs of spinal cord compression, urinary tract complications (ureteral obstruction, bladder outlet obstruction), and bone lesions at increased fracture risk
Symptoms of hepatotoxicity and renal dysfunction

questions.explanation: The guideline explicitly states that in metastatic patients, it is of utmost importance to counsel about early signs of spinal cord compression, urinary tract complications (ureteral obstruction, bladder outlet obstruction), or bone lesions at increased fracture risk. This counseling is critical during follow-up.
questions.fact: In metastatic patients, it is of the utmost importance to counsel about early signs of spinal cord compression, urinary tract complications (ureteral obstruction, bladder outlet obstruction) or bone lesions that are at an increased fracture risk.

questions.question: What did the secondary analysis of the Titan study demonstrate regarding PSA monitoring?

options.text: Nearly half of patients developing subsequent radiographic progression had no concomitant PSA progression
PSA monitoring detected all cases of radiographic progression
PSA progression always precedes radiographic progression
PSA monitoring is the most reliable method for assessing disease activity

questions.explanation: The guideline cites a secondary analysis of the Titan study which found that nearly half of the patients developing subsequent radiographic progression had no concomitant PSA progression, suggesting that heavy reliance on PSA monitoring may be inadequate for assessing disease activity.
questions.fact: A secondary analysis of the Titan study found that nearly half of the patients developing subsequent radiographic progression had no concomitant PSA progression, suggesting that heavy reliance on PSA monitoring may be inadequate for assessing disease activity in this context.

questions.question: According to the guideline, follow-up frequency during first-line hormonal treatment should be dependent on which of the following factors?
questions.explanation: The guideline states that since most men will receive another anticancer therapy combined with ADT (such as ARPI, chemotherapy, local RT, or combinations), follow-up frequency should also be dependent on the treatment modality. This reflects the need to tailor monitoring to the specific treatment approach.
questions.fact: Follow-up frequency should also be dependent on the treatment modality.

questions.question: According to the guideline, what PSA threshold decline suggests a likely prolonged response in men on ADT?
questions.explanation: Section 7.4.5.b states that 'a PSA decline to < 2.4ng/mL suggests a likely prolonged response and follow-up visits can be scheduled every three to six months, provided the patient is asymptomatic or clinically improving.' The guideline provides the specific threshold of 2.4 ng/mL, not any other value.
questions.section_title: 7.4.5.b. Imaging as a marker of response in metastatic PCa
questions.fact: In men on ADT alone, a PSA decline to < 2.4ng/mL suggests a likely prolonged response

questions.question: What should a rising PSA prompt according to the guideline on monitoring metastatic prostate cancer during ADT?

options.text: Assessment of testosterone level and restaging using imaging
Addition of chemotherapy without further testing

questions.explanation: Section 7.4.5.b explicitly states that 'A rising PSA should prompt assessment of testosterone level, which is mandatory to define CRPC status, as well as restaging using imaging.' The guideline does not recommend stopping therapy or adding chemotherapy without proper assessment.
questions.section_title: 7.4.5.b. Imaging as a marker of response in metastatic PCa
questions.fact: A rising PSA should prompt assessment of testosterone level, which is mandatory to define CRPC status, as well as restaging using imaging

questions.question: According to the Titan study secondary analysis cited in the guideline, what proportion of patients developing subsequent radiographic progression had no concomitant PSA rise during ADT plus ARPI treatment?
questions.explanation: Section 7.4.5.b states that 'a secondary analysis of the Titan study found that nearly half of the patients developing subsequent radiographic progression had no concomitant PSA [1246].' This finding demonstrates why relying on PSA alone during combination therapy is insufficient for detecting progression.
questions.section_title: 7.4.5.b. Imaging as a marker of response in metastatic PCa
questions.fact: A secondary analysis of the Titan study found that nearly half of the patients developing subsequent radiographic progression had no concomitant PSA

questions.question: What is the minimum follow-up schedule for patients with stage M0 prostate cancer according to EAU guidelines?
questions.explanation: The guideline explicitly states that in patients with stage M0 disease, follow-up should be scheduled at least every six months, including disease-specific history, PSA determination, and liver and renal function tests.
questions.fact: In patients with stage M0 disease, schedule follow-up at least every six months.

questions.question: According to the EAU definition, what testosterone level threshold defines castration-resistant prostate cancer (CRPC)?
questions.explanation: The guideline explicitly states that by definition, castration-resistant PCa requires a testosterone level < 50ng/dL (< 1.7nmol/L). This threshold should be assessed in patients with suspected progression.
questions.fact: By definition, castration-resistant PCa requires a testosterone level < 50ng/dL (< 1.7nmol/L).

questions.question: In patients on long-term androgen deprivation therapy (ADT), which of the following is NOT a minimum requirement for follow-up according to EAU guidelines?
questions.explanation: The guideline specifies that long-term ADT follow-up must include: medical history (including ADT-induced complications), haemoglobin, serum creatinine, alkaline phosphatase, lipid profiles, and HbA1c measurements. Troponin levels are not mentioned as a requirement.
questions.fact: Long-term ADT minimum requirements include haemoglobin, serum creatinine, alkaline phosphatase, lipid profiles and HbA1c measurements.

questions.question: How many parts is Chapter 8 on Quality of Life Outcomes in Prostate Cancer presented in?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states 'This chapter is presented in two parts' in Section 8.1. The first part covers long-term consequences while the second part provides evidence-based recommendations for treatment selection.
questions.fact: This chapter is presented in two parts

questions.question: What is the minimum time threshold for defining 'long-term consequences' of therapies for prostate cancer according to Section 8.2?
questions.explanation: Section 8.1 explicitly states that Section 8.2 summarises 'long-term consequences (≥ twelve months) of therapies for PCa'. This twelve-month threshold is the defining criterion for long-term consequences.
questions.fact: Long-term consequences are defined as ≥ twelve months

questions.question: What does Section 8.3 of the Quality of Life chapter specifically address?

options.text: Surgical techniques for prostate cancer treatment
Evidence-based recommendations for supporting patients selecting primary treatment options for localised disease and supportive interventions for improving disease-specific QoL

questions.explanation: The text in Section 8.1 clearly states that Section 8.3 'provides evidence-based recommendations for supporting patients when selecting primary treatment options for localised disease and supportive interventions aimed at improving disease-specific QoL across all stages of disease'.
questions.fact: Section 8.3 provides evidence-based recommendations for supporting patients when selecting primary treatment options for localised disease and supportive interventions aimed at improving disease-specific QoL

questions.question: Which of the following healthcare professionals is specifically mentioned as part of the multidisciplinary team required for holistic prostate cancer care?
questions.explanation: Section 8.2 explicitly states that a multidisciplinary team approach requires the intervention of urologists, medical oncologists, radiation oncologists, oncology nurses, behavioural practitioners, and others including fellow patients. Physical therapists, cardiologists, and endocrinologists are not mentioned in this context.
questions.fact: Approaching care from a holistic perspective requires the intervention of a multidisciplinary team including urologists, medical oncologists, radiation oncologists, oncology nurses, behavioural practitioners and many others, including fellow patients.

questions.question: According to the guideline, which of the following patient subgroups is NOT identified as being at higher risk of long-term mental health issues following prostate cancer treatment?
questions.explanation: Section 8.2 identifies that subgroups including those with poor general health, being unmarried, older age, and/or pre-existing depressive symptoms are more at risk of long-term mental health issues. High socioeconomic status is not listed as a risk factor; in fact, the text indicates clinical trial populations (often higher socioeconomic groups) experience less impact.
questions.fact: Subgroups of people, including those with poor general health, being unmarried, older age and/or pre-existing depressive symptoms, are more at risk of long-term mental health issues following treatment for PCa.

questions.question: How does the impact of prostate cancer treatment on quality of life in general PCa populations compare to clinical trial populations?

options.text: The impact is similar in both populations
The impact is greater in clinical trial populations
The impact is greater in general PCa populations
General populations show no impact on quality of life

questions.explanation: Section 8.2 clearly states that cross-sectional patient-reported outcomes studies show the impact of treatment on global and disease-specific QoL is greater than that described in clinical trial populations, who often have less co-morbidities and belong to higher socio-economic groups.
questions.fact: Cross-sectional patient-reported outcomes studies in general PCa populations show the impact of treatment on global and disease-specific QoL is greater than that described in clinical trial populations, who often have less co-morbidities and belong to higher socio-economic groups.

questions.question: According to the systematic review on long-term health-related quality of life, which treatment approach shows a clear advantage in preserving continence over active treatment, particularly radical prostatectomy?
questions.explanation: The systematic review states that 'in preservation of continence, there is a clear advantage for AS over active treatment, particularly to RP,' indicating that patients on active surveillance maintain superior continence outcomes compared to those who underwent radical prostatectomy.
questions.fact: In preservation of continence, there is a clear advantage for AS over active treatment, particularly to RP.

questions.question: How long does it typically take for most men on active surveillance to report low levels of cancer-specific anxiety according to registry data?
questions.explanation: The text states that 'patients considering active surveillance can be informed that, although it is common experience some anxiety initially, most men rapidly adjust and report low levels of anxiety within two years,' citing registry data from the USA.
questions.fact: Most men rapidly adjust and report low levels of anxiety within two years.

questions.question: According to studies comparing active surveillance with active treatment, what was found regarding general health-related quality of life PROMs?

options.text: Active treatment showed substantially better PROMs than active surveillance
Active surveillance showed substantially better PROMs than active treatment
No substantial or consistent difference in general HRQoL PROMs between groups
Active surveillance showed inconsistent results compared to active treatment

questions.explanation: The systematic review explicitly states that 'all studies comparing AS with active treatment found no substantial or consistent difference in general HRQoL PROMs between groups,' indicating equivalent quality of life outcomes between the approaches.
questions.fact: All studies comparing AS with active treatment found no substantial or consistent difference in general HRQoL PROMs between groups.

questions.question: In which patient group are obstructive voiding symptoms more commonly observed according to the systematic review?

options.text: Patients who received combined radiotherapy and hormone therapy

questions.explanation: The text clearly states that 'obstructive voiding symptoms were more common in patients on AS than in postoperative patients,' suggesting that active surveillance patients experience more obstructive symptoms compared to those who underwent surgery.
questions.fact: Obstructive voiding symptoms were more common in patients on AS than in postoperative patients.

questions.question: Compared to patients without prostate cancer, how do patients on active surveillance report their quality of life according to the systematic review?

options.text: Slightly worse but statistically similar quality of life
Good quality of life, similar to individuals without prostate cancer
Much better quality of life due to avoidance of treatment side effects

questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that 'all patients on AS report good quality of life, similar to that in individuals without prostate cancer,' indicating that active surveillance does not significantly impair overall quality of life compared to the general population.
questions.fact: All patients on AS report good quality of life, similar to that in individuals without prostate cancer.

questions.question: What percentage of patients experience urinary retention following brachytherapy implantation?
questions.explanation: The guideline states that urinary retention occurs in 1.5-22% of patients following implantation. This statistic appears in section 8.2.4 under the discussion of urinary complications from whole-gland treatments.
questions.section_title: 8.2.4. Local primary whole-gland treatments other than surgery or radiotherapy
questions.fact: urinary retention (1.5-22%)

questions.question: What proportion of urinary complications following brachytherapy are attributed to urethral strictures?
questions.explanation: According to section 8.2.4, urethral strictures account for at least 50% of urinary complications and can be resolved with dilation in the majority of cases. This is one of the most significant urinary adverse effects reported in the guideline.
questions.section_title: 8.2.4. Local primary whole-gland treatments other than surgery or radiotherapy
questions.fact: Urethral strictures account for at least 50% of urinary complications

questions.question: Prevention of morbidity from brachytherapy depends on which combination of factors?

options.text: Careful patient selection and IPSS score, backed up by urodynamic studies
Pre-treatment PSA levels and Gleason score assessment
Use of adjuvant hormone therapy and regular MRI monitoring
Patient age and body mass index optimization

questions.explanation: Section 8.2.4 explicitly states that prevention of morbidity depends on careful patient selection and IPSS score, backed up by urodynamic studies. This highlights the importance of baseline urinary function assessment before brachytherapy.
questions.section_title: 8.2.4. Local primary whole-gland treatments other than surgery or radiotherapy
questions.fact: Prevention of morbidity depends on careful patient selection and IPSS score, backed up by urodynamic studies

questions.question: According to the ProtecT trial analysis, which of the following bowel toxicities was reported in patients treated with EBRT and six months of ADT?

options.text: Transient constipation and mild abdominal discomfort
Persistent diarrhoea, bowel urgency and/or incontinence, and rectal bleeding
Acute intestinal obstruction requiring surgical intervention

questions.explanation: The text explicitly states in Section 8.2.3.b that analysis of the ProtecT trial shows patients treated with EBRT and six months of ADT report 'bowel toxicity including persistent diarrhoea, bowel urgency and/or incontinence and rectal bleeding'.
questions.fact: Analysis of the toxicity outcomes of the ProtecT trial shows that patients treated with EBRT and six months of ADT report bowel toxicity including persistent diarrhoea, bowel urgency and/or incontinence and rectal bleeding

questions.question: Based on the systematic review and meta-analysis cited in the guidelines, what are the approximate odds ratios for developing second cancers after prostate radiotherapy?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states in Section 8.2.3.b that the SR and meta-analysis 'demonstrates an increased risk of developing second cancers for bladder (OR: 1.39), colorectal (OR: 1.68) and rectum (OR: 1.62)'.
questions.fact: An SR and meta-analysis of observational studies comparing patients exposed or unexposed to RT in the course of treatment for PCa demonstrates an increased risk of developing second cancers for bladder (OR: 1.39), colorectal (OR: 1.68) and rectum (OR: 1.62)

questions.question: What is the range of absolute excess risks of developing second cancers over a ten-year period following prostate radiotherapy?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states in Section 8.2.3.b that 'absolute excess risks over ten years are small (1–4%)' and these should be discussed with younger patients in particular.
questions.fact: Absolute excess risks over ten years are small (1–4%) but should be discussed with younger patients in particular

questions.question: Compared to 3D-CRT, how does IMRT affect bowel toxicity in prostate cancer radiotherapy?

options.text: IMRT demonstrates significantly greater bowel toxicity than 3D-CRT
IMRT demonstrates less bowel toxicity than noted previously with 3D-CRT
IMRT and 3D-CRT show equivalent rates of bowel toxicity
IMRT eliminates all bowel toxicity complications

questions.explanation: The text explicitly states in Section 8.2.3.b that 'studies using IMRT demonstrate less bowel toxicity than noted previously with 3D-CRT', noting that ProtecT participants were treated with 3D-CRT.
questions.fact: Participants in the ProtecT study were treated with 3D-CRT and studies using IMRT demonstrate less bowel toxicity than noted previously with 3D-CRT

questions.question: According to the systematic review and meta-analysis, how did whole-gland cryotherapy compare to radical prostatectomy (RP) regarding urinary incontinence at one year?

options.text: Cryotherapy had lower urinary incontinence rates than RP
Cryotherapy had higher urinary incontinence rates than RP
There was no significant difference between cryotherapy and RP
Cryotherapy and RP had identical urinary incontinence rates

questions.explanation: Section 8.2.4.b states that the SR and meta-analysis produced evidence that the rate of urinary incontinence at one year was lower for whole-gland cryotherapy than for RP, though the size of this difference decreased with longer follow-up. This makes cryotherapy favorable for early urinary continence outcomes compared to radical prostatectomy.
questions.fact: The rate of urinary incontinence at one year was lower for whole-gland cryotherapy than for RP, but the size of the difference decreased with longer follow-up [853].

questions.question: What was the urinary incontinence rate at one year when comparing whole-gland cryotherapy to external beam radiotherapy (EBRT)?

options.text: Cryotherapy showed significantly better outcomes than EBRT
Cryotherapy showed significantly worse outcomes than EBRT
No significant difference was seen between cryotherapy and EBRT (<1%)
Cryotherapy had twice the incontinence rate of EBRT

questions.explanation: Section 8.2.4.b explicitly states that no significant difference was seen between cryotherapy versus EBRT in terms of urinary incontinence at one year, with both showing rates of less than 1%. This indicates comparable urinary continence outcomes between these two treatment modalities.
questions.fact: No significant difference was seen between cryotherapy versus EBRT in terms of urinary incontinence at one year (< 1%) [853].

questions.question: According to the systematic review and meta-analysis, how did whole-gland HIFU compare to radical prostatectomy (RP) for urinary incontinence at one year?

options.text: HIFU had higher incontinence rates than RP
HIFU had lower incontinence rates than RP
HIFU and RP had identical incontinence rates
The comparison showed no clear difference between HIFU and RP

questions.explanation: Section 8.2.4.b clearly states that whole-gland HIFU showed lower incontinence rates at one year than RP with an odds ratio of 0.06 (95% CI: 0.01–0.48) [853]. This statistically significant finding indicates a substantial protective effect of HIFU against urinary incontinence compared to radical prostatectomy at one-year follow-up.
questions.fact: Whole-gland HIFU on the other hand showed lower incontinence rates at one year than RP (OR: 0.06, 95% CI: 0.01–0.48) [853].

questions.question: According to the systematic review on focal therapy for prostate cancer, what were the pad-free continence rates reported for focal HIFU and cryotherapy?
questions.explanation: The systematic review (SR) covering 72 studies with 5,827 patients found that focal HIFU and cryotherapy achieved pad-free continence rates above 95%, demonstrating favourable functional outcomes for these focal therapies.
questions.fact: For focal HIFU and cryotherapy, this SR showed pad-free continence rates above 95%

questions.question: What was the median decrease in erectile function reported after focal therapy according to the systematic review?
questions.explanation: The systematic review found that focal HIFU and cryotherapy resulted in a median decrease of erectile function of only 12%, indicating minimal impact on erectile function with focal therapy approaches.
questions.fact: A median decrease of erectile function of only 12%

questions.question: According to a systematic review of only prospective data, what percentage reduction in sexual function scores was observed with focal ablation compared to whole-gland ablation at one year?
questions.explanation: A systematic review with only prospective data found that focal ablation showed only a 9% reduction in sexual function scores compared to 43% for whole-gland ablation at one year, highlighting the functional advantage of focal over whole-gland approaches.
questions.fact: A SR with only prospective data found that focal ablation showed only 9% reduction in sexual function scores, compared to 43% for whole-gland ablation at one year

questions.question: According to the guideline section on sexual outcomes in ADT, what percentage of patients experience cessation of sexual activity?
questions.explanation: The guideline explicitly states that 'Cessation of sexual activity is very common in people undergoing ADT, affecting up to 93%', making this the only directly supported statistic in the text.
questions.fact: Cessation of sexual activity is very common in people undergoing ADT, affecting up to 93%

questions.question: What does androgen-deprivation therapy (ADT) reduce according to the guideline?

options.text: Both libido and the ability to gain and maintain erections

questions.explanation: The guideline directly states that 'Androgen-deprivation therapy reduces both libido and the ability to gain and maintain erections', indicating dual effects on sexual function.
questions.fact: Androgen-deprivation therapy reduces both libido and the ability to gain and maintain erections

questions.question: What is the recommended approach for managing acquired erectile dysfunction in patients on ADT?
questions.explanation: The guideline explicitly states that 'The management of the acquired ED is mostly non-specific', which is the only description of ED management approach provided in the text.
questions.fact: The management of the acquired ED is mostly non-specific

questions.question: In a study comparing bicalutamide monotherapy to castration at twelve months, what domains showed significant advantage for bicalutamide?

options.text: Physical capacity and sexual interest, but not sexual function

questions.explanation: The guideline explicitly states that 'bicalutamide monotherapy showed a significant advantage over castration in the domains of physical capacity and sexual interest (but not sexual function) at twelve months', making this the precise answer.
questions.fact: Using a specific nonvalidated questionnaire, bicalutamide monotherapy showed a significant advantage over castration in the domains of physical capacity and sexual interest (but not sexual function) at twelve months

questions.question: According to the guideline, what is the estimated prevalence of hot flushes in men on androgen deprivation therapy (ADT)?
questions.explanation: The guideline explicitly states that hot flushes are a common side effect of ADT with prevalence estimated between 44–80% of men on ADT, as referenced in the section on ADT-induced adverse effects.
questions.fact: Hot flushes are a common side effect of ADT (prevalence estimated between 44–80% of men on ADT)

questions.question: In the prospective RCT comparing venlafaxine with hormonal therapies for hot flushes, what was the percentage reduction in median daily hot-flush score for venlafaxine at six months?
questions.explanation: The guideline states that venlafaxine was inferior to hormonal therapies with a median daily hot-flush score reduction of -47.2% (interquartile range -74.3 to -2.5) compared to -94.5% in the cyproterone group and -83.7% in the medroxyprogesterone group.
questions.fact: Based on median daily hot-flush score, venlafaxine was inferior -47.2% (interquartile range -74.3 to -2.5) compared to -94.5% (-100.0 to -74.5) in the cyproterone group, and -83.7% (-98.9 to -64.3) in the medroxyprogesterone group

questions.question: Regarding transdermal oestradiol compared to placebo for hot flushes in men on ADT, which statement is correct according to the cited RCT?

options.text: Oestradiol significantly reduced both daily and weekly hot flushes
Oestradiol significantly reduced daily hot flushes but not weekly hot flushes
Oestradiol only reduced weekly hot flushes but not daily
Oestradiol had no significant effect on hot flushes

questions.explanation: The guideline cites an RCT (n=78) showing that after six months, oestradiol reduced daily hot flushes frequency with a mean adjusted difference of -1.6 (p=0.04), but the effect on weekly hot flushes was not significant (MAD -19.6, p=0.11).
questions.fact: After six months, oestradiol reduced daily hot flushes frequency (mean adjusted difference MAD -1,6, p = 0.04), but the effect on weekly hot flushes was not significant (MAD -19,6 p = 0.11)

questions.question: What level of placebo response rate has been reported in hot flush studies according to the guideline?
questions.explanation: The guideline states that considering placebo response rates in up to 30% of patients, prospective RCTs are required to document the efficacy of various treatments including clonidine, veralipride, gabapentin, and acupuncture.
questions.fact: Considering placebo response rates in up to 30% of patients [1538], prospective RCTs are required to document the efficacy of clonidine, veralipride, gabapentin [1539] and acupuncture [1540]

questions.question: According to the EAU guidelines on prostate cancer, what is the current evidence status regarding the association between castration and stroke risk?

options.text: Castration appears to be associated with an increased risk of stroke
Castration definitively causes stroke based on randomized trials
Castration has no association with cardiovascular events
The association between castration and stroke is completely refuted

questions.explanation: The guideline states in section 8.2.6 that castration 'appears to be associated with an increased risk of stroke,' indicating a suspected but not definitive causal relationship.
questions.fact: Castration appears to be associated with an increased risk of stroke

questions.question: Based on the EAU guidelines, what is the current evidence status for the association between castration and depression or cognitive decline, including Alzheimer disease?

options.text: Castration is suspected to be associated with an increased risk for depression and cognitive decline
Castration has been proven to cause depression in all patients
There is no suspected link between castration and any neuropsychological effects
Castration prevents cognitive decline based on strong evidence

questions.explanation: The guideline states in section 8.2.6 that castration is 'suspected to be associated with an increased risk for depression and cognitive decline such as Alzheimer disease,' indicating an uncertain but concerning relationship.
questions.fact: Castration is suspected to be associated with an increased risk for depression and cognitive decline such as Alzheimer disease

questions.question: According to the guideline, what is the minimum number of criteria required to diagnose metabolic syndrome?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that metabolic syndrome 'is defined by the presence of at least three of the following criteria', which includes waist circumference > 102cm, serum triglyceride > 1.7mmol/L, blood pressure > 130/80mmHg or antihypertensive use, HDL cholesterol < 1mmol/L, and glycaemia > 5.6mmol/L or hyperglycaemia medication use.
questions.fact: Metabolic syndrome is defined by the presence of at least three of the following criteria

questions.question: How early can lipid alterations occur after starting androgen deprivation therapy (ADT)?
questions.explanation: The guideline text explicitly states 'Lipid alterations are common and may occur as early as the first three months of ADT', indicating this is a well-documented early metabolic complication of ADT.
questions.fact: Lipid alterations are common and may occur as early as the first three months of ADT

questions.question: In a prospective longitudinal study of 252 men on ADT for a median of 20.4 months, what was the percentage decline in lean body mass at three years?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly reports that the study 'reported a progressive decline in lean body mass over time: 1.0% at one year, 2.1% at two years, and 2.4% at three years', with the decline being more pronounced in men aged 70 years or older.
questions.fact: 2.4% at three years

questions.question: What was the prevalence of sarcopenia at baseline in the study of 110 men with mCRPC?
questions.explanation: The guideline clearly states that 'Sarcopenia at baseline, found in 27% of 110 men with mCRPC, significantly predicted severe toxicity and ER visits', indicating this was the documented baseline prevalence in this patient population.
questions.fact: Sarcopenia at baseline, found in 27% of 110 men with mCRPC

questions.question: According to the systematic review on ADT's impact on body composition, what effect does ADT have on BMI?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that 'An SR on the impact of ADT on body composition found ADT had a relatively stable effect on BMI but increased sarcopenia and subcutaneous adipose tissue', distinguishing the effect on BMI from its effects on body composition components.
questions.fact: ADT had a relatively stable effect on BMI

questions.question: According to the guideline, what is considered the best protective measure against fatigue associated with ADT?
questions.explanation: The guideline explicitly states that 'regular exercise appears to be the best protective measure' for fatigue developing as a side effect of ADT. Section 8.2.5.g directly identifies exercise as the most effective intervention.
questions.fact: Regular exercise appears to be the best protective measure against ADT-related fatigue

questions.question: According to the guideline, what clinical action should be prompted when a patient reports clinically significant fatigue during ADT?

options.text: Referral to endocrinology for thyroid evaluation

questions.explanation: The guideline explicitly states that 'Reporting clinically significant fatigue is associated with severe psychological distress and should prompt screening for anxiety and/or depression.' Section 8.2.5.g identifies this as the appropriate clinical response.
questions.fact: Clinically significant fatigue should prompt screening for anxiety and/or depression

questions.question: Which of the following is listed in the guideline as a potential cause of anaemia that requires evaluation in patients on ADT?

options.text: Medullary invasion, renal insufficiency, iron deficiency, or chronic bleeding
Vitamin B12 deficiency, folate deficiency, or bone marrow metastasis
Prostate cancer direct invasion, splenic sequestration, or autoimmune haemolysis
Chronic infection, liver dysfunction, or thyroid disease

questions.explanation: The guideline explicitly lists the causes requiring evaluation as 'medullar invasion, renal insufficiency, iron deficiency, chronic bleeding.' Section 8.2.5.g specifies these as the causes that must be identified when anaemia is present.
questions.fact: Anaemia evaluation should identify medullar invasion, renal insufficiency, iron deficiency, or chronic bleeding

questions.question: According to the EAU guidelines, what can patients eligible for active surveillance be advised regarding global quality of life compared to radical treatment options?

options.text: Global quality of life is equivalent for up to five years compared to radical prostatectomy or external beam radiotherapy
Global quality of life is superior for at least ten years compared to radical prostatectomy
Global quality of life is inferior but improves significantly after three years
Global quality of life equivalence has only been demonstrated for external beam radiotherapy, not surgery

questions.explanation: The guideline section 8.3.2 explicitly states that patients eligible for active surveillance should be advised that global quality of life is equivalent for up to five years compared to radical prostatectomy or external beam radiotherapy. This is a Strong recommendation.
questions.section_title: 8.3.2. Improving quality of life in men who have been diagnosed with PCa
questions.fact: Advise patients eligible for active surveillance that global quality of life is equivalent for up to five years compared to radical prostatectomy or external beam radiotherapy

questions.question: Which of the following functional domains is negatively impacted by external beam radiotherapy according to the EAU guidelines?
questions.explanation: The guideline section 8.3.2 clearly states that physicians should discuss the negative impact of RT on bowel function with patients, alongside the recommendation to discuss surgery's impact on urinary and sexual function.
questions.section_title: 8.3.2. Improving quality of life in men who have been diagnosed with PCa
questions.fact: Discuss the negative impact of surgery on urinary and sexual function, as well as the negative impact of RT on bowel function with patients

questions.question: Regarding patients treated with brachytherapy, when does irritative urinary symptomatology typically resolve according to the EAU guidelines?

options.text: At one year but not after five years

questions.explanation: The guideline section 8.3.2 specifies that patients treated with brachytherapy should be advised of the negative impact on irritative urinary symptomatology at one year but not after five years, making this a Weak recommendation.
questions.section_title: 8.3.2. Improving quality of life in men who have been diagnosed with PCa
questions.fact: Advise patients treated with brachytherapy of the negative impact on irritative urinary symptomatology at one year but not after five years

questions.question: What is the reported prevalence of fear of cancer recurrence in men with prostate cancer?
questions.explanation: The guideline explicitly states that fear of cancer recurrence has a prevalence of 16% across studies. Section 8.3.1 confirms this specific statistic for men with prostate cancer.
questions.section_title: 8.3.1. Long-term (> twelve months) quality of life outcomes in men with localised disease
questions.fact: Fear of cancer recurrence and PSA anxiety has a prevalence of 16% and 22%, respectively, across studies.

questions.question: Which combination of patient-reported outcome measures (PROMs) has the best evidence for use in patients with localised prostate cancer?
questions.explanation: The guideline specifically states that the tools with the best evidence for psychometric properties and feasibility are EORTC QLQ-C30 and QLQ-PR25. Section 8.3.1 identifies these as the recommended combination for localised PCa.
questions.section_title: 8.3.1. Long-term (> twelve months) quality of life outcomes in men with localised disease
questions.fact: The tools with the best evidence for psychometric properties and feasibility for use in routine practice and research settings to assess PROMs in patients with localised PCa were EORTC QLQ-C30 and QLQ-PR25.

questions.question: According to the guideline, why should EORTC QLQ-C30 be used in conjunction with QLQ-PR25?

options.text: QLQ-PR25 is more cost-effective than standalone use
EORTC QLQ-C30 does not directly assess PCa-specific issues
QLQ-PR25 has better sensitivity for advanced disease
EORTC QLQ-C30 requires additional validation for PCa use

questions.explanation: The guideline explicitly states that because EORTC QLQ-C30 is a general module that does not directly assess PCa-specific issues, it should be adopted in conjunction with the QLQ-PR25 module. This justification is provided in Section 8.3.1.
questions.section_title: 8.3.1. Long-term (> twelve months) quality of life outcomes in men with localised disease
questions.fact: Because EORTC QLQ-C30 is a general module that does not directly assess PCa-specific issues, it should be adopted in conjunction with the QLQ-PR25 module.

questions.question: Which type of psychological intervention has been shown to improve depression, anxiety and distress in men with prostate cancer?
questions.explanation: The guideline explicitly states that combined cognitive- and education-based psychological interventions improve depression, anxiety and distress. This is stated in Section 8.3.1 as evidence-based intervention.
questions.section_title: 8.3.1. Long-term (> twelve months) quality of life outcomes in men with localised disease
questions.fact: Combined cognitive- and education-based psychological interventions improve depression, anxiety and distress.

questions.question: Which adverse effects are specifically associated with androgen deprivation therapy (ADT) used in short- or long-term treatment according to the guideline?

options.text: Sexual problems, fatigue, psychological morbidity, adverse metabolic sequelae, increased cardiovascular and bone fracture risk

questions.explanation: The guideline explicitly lists these specific adverse effects of ADT in Section 8.3.1, including sexual problems, fatigue, psychological morbidity, adverse metabolic sequelae, and increased cardiovascular and bone fracture risk.
questions.section_title: 8.3.1. Long-term (> twelve months) quality of life outcomes in men with localised disease
questions.fact: ADT used in short- or long-term treatment can cause sexual problems, fatigue, psychological morbidity, adverse metabolic sequelae and increased cardiovascular and bone fracture risk.

questions.question: According to the EAU guidelines, what is the approximate cure rate following surgical interventions (sling or artificial urinary sphincter) for post-surgical urinary incontinence in men with prostate cancer?
questions.explanation: The guideline states in Section 8.3.2.b that surgical interventions including sling and AUS have an overall cure rate of approximately 60%, which is directly supported by the text.
questions.fact: The overall cure rate following sling and AUS surgery is approximately 60% and results in improvement in incontinence by approximately 25%.

questions.question: In the multicentre double-blind RCT evaluating tadalafil for penile rehabilitation after nerve-sparing radical prostatectomy (n=423), what was the dosage and frequency that improved EPIC sexual domain scores compared to placebo at nine months?
questions.explanation: Section 8.3.2.b explicitly states that tadalafil 5 mg once per day improved EPIC sexual domain scores (least-squares mean difference +9.6) compared to 20mg on-demand or placebo at nine months in men aged <68 years.
questions.fact: In men aged <68 years with normal pretreatment erectile function undergoing nerve-sparing RP, tadalafil 5 mg once per day improved EPIC sexual domain scores compared to on-demand use or placebo at nine months.

questions.question: Which of the following alternative devices for urinary incontinence may be less efficacious than the artificial urinary sphincter (AUS) according to the guidelines?

options.text: AdVance sling and Virtue male sling
Adjustable Transobturator Male System (ATOMS) and Adjustable Continence Therapy (proACT)
Pelvic floor muscle training with biofeedback

questions.explanation: The guideline in Section 8.3.2.b specifically states that ATOMS and proACT may be an option but appear to be less efficacious than AUS, distinguishing these surgical alternatives from conservative options listed.
questions.fact: Alternative surgical options such as the Adjustable Transobturator Male System (ATOMS) and the Adjustable Continence Therapy (proACT) may be an option but appear to be less efficacious than AUS.

questions.question: According to the guideline, what effect did patient education have on PSA testing and adherence to Active Surveillance protocols?

options.text: Patient education increased PSA testing and decreased adherence to AS protocols
Patient education decreased PSA testing and increased adherence to AS protocols
Patient education had no effect on PSA testing but increased adherence to AS protocols
Patient education increased both PSA testing and adherence to AS protocols

questions.explanation: The guideline states in section 8.3.2.e that patient education decreased PSA testing and increased adherence to AS protocols (references 1667-1669). This demonstrates the positive impact of education on both reducing unnecessary testing and improving protocol compliance.
questions.section_title: 8.3.2.e. Recommendations for quality of life in men undergoing systemic treatments
questions.fact: Patient education decreased PSA testing and increased adherence to AS protocols

questions.question: When patients with metastasised prostate cancer were presented with treatment choices, what did they prioritise over mode of administration and risk of fatigue?

options.text: Side effect profile and recovery time

questions.explanation: The guideline in section 8.3.2.e explicitly states that patients prioritised effectiveness and pain control over mode of administration and risk of fatigue when presented with treatment choice in metastasised PCa (reference 1672). This highlights what matters most to patients in advanced disease treatment decisions.
questions.section_title: 8.3.2.e. Recommendations for quality of life in men undergoing systemic treatments
questions.fact: Patients prioritised effectiveness and pain control over mode of administration and risk of fatigue when presented with treatment choice in metastasised PCa

questions.question: According to the guideline, what are the two essential components that a decision aid should incorporate to optimise efficacy?

options.text: Patient education and promotion of shared decision-making

questions.explanation: The guideline in section 8.3.2.e clearly states that a decision aid should educate as well as promote shared decision-making to optimise efficacy (reference 1674). This dual approach combining education with shared decision-making is emphasized as essential for decision aid effectiveness.
questions.section_title: 8.3.2.e. Recommendations for quality of life in men undergoing systemic treatments
questions.fact: A decision aid should educate as well as promote shared decision-making to optimise efficacy

questions.question: In men with T1-T3 prostate cancer undergoing radiation therapy and androgen deprivation therapy, what components were included in the combined nurse-led psychological support and physiotherapist-led multidisciplinary rehabilitation programme?

options.text: Action planning for lifestyle changes, weight control, toilet habits, sexuality and psychological problems, complemented with pelvic floor muscle therapy
Medication management, dietary counseling, and mindfulness meditation
Chemotherapy education, pain management, and group therapy sessions
Surgical rehabilitation, occupational therapy, and speech therapy

questions.explanation: The text in Section 8.3.2.c states that the intervention involved 'action planning concerning patients' needs related to lifestyle changes, weight control, toilet habits, sexuality and psychological problems, complemented with pelvic floor muscle therapy'. This specific combination of nurse-led and physiotherapist-led components is explicitly described.
questions.fact: The intervention involved action planning concerning patients' needs related to lifestyle changes, weight control, toilet habits, sexuality and psychological problems, complemented with pelvic floor muscle therapy.

questions.question: According to the meta-analysis including 34 studies and 2,741 men, what type of exercise training produced the greatest effects for men with prostate cancer?
questions.explanation: Section 8.3.2.c explicitly states: 'A meta-analysis including 34 studies and 2,741 men confirmed that supervised exercise significantly improves fatigue (SMD –0.32), cancer-specific QoL (SMD 0.24), and muscular strength, with the greatest effects seen in combined aerobic and resistance training.'
questions.fact: A meta-analysis including 34 studies and 2,741 men confirmed that supervised exercise significantly improves fatigue (SMD –0.32), cancer-specific QoL (SMD 0.24), and muscular strength, with the greatest effects seen in combined aerobic and resistance training.

questions.question: What does this chapter primarily review according to the guideline introduction?
questions.explanation: The guideline section explicitly states that 'This chapter reviews the available treatment modalities'. The other options are not mentioned in this introductory text.
questions.section_title: 6.1. Estimating life expectancy and health status
questions.fact: This chapter reviews the available treatment modalities, followed by individual sections addressing treatment for the various disease stages.

questions.question: According to the guideline, what is recommended as a single predictive method for life expectancy in men?
questions.explanation: Section 6.1.3 explicitly states that 'gait speed is a good single predictive method of life expectancy (from a standing start, at usual pace, generally over six meters)'. The other options (PSA, BMI, blood pressure) are not mentioned in this context.
questions.fact: Gait speed is a good single predictive method of life expectancy (from a standing start, at usual pace, generally over six meters)

questions.question: For men at age 75, what is the range of ten-year survival based on gait speed?
questions.explanation: Section 6.1.3 specifically states that 'for men at age 75, ten-year survival ranged from 19% < 0.4 m/s to 87% for ≥ 1.4 m/s'. This demonstrates the significant impact of gait speed on survival predictions.
questions.fact: For men at age 75, ten-year survival ranged from 19% < 0.4 m/s to 87% for ≥ 1.4 m/s

questions.question: According to the guideline, where are life expectancy tables for European men available online?
questions.explanation: Section 6.1.3 explicitly states that 'Life expectancy tables for European men are available online: https://ec.europa.eu/eurostat/'. The guideline directs clinicians to this specific source for population-based survival data.
questions.fact: Life expectancy tables for European men are available online: https://ec.europa.eu/eurostat/

questions.question: What is the median age at diagnosis of prostate cancer?
questions.explanation: The text states that 'Prostate cancer is common in older men (median age 68 years)'. This represents the typical age at which prostate cancer is diagnosed.
questions.fact: Prostate cancer is common in older men (median age 68 years)

questions.question: According to the guideline, what minimum life expectancy is considered mandatory for any benefit from local treatment in localised prostate cancer?
questions.explanation: Section 6.1.2 explicitly states that 'In localised disease, over ten years' life expectancy is considered mandatory for any benefit from local treatment'. This threshold helps identify patients who may benefit from active treatment.
questions.fact: In localised disease, over ten years' life expectancy is considered mandatory for any benefit from local treatment

questions.question: In the referenced RCT, in which age group was the benefit of surgery with respect to death from prostate cancer largest?
questions.explanation: The text states that 'in an RCT the benefit of surgery with respect to death from PCa was largest in men < 65 years of age (RR: 0.45)'. This demonstrates age as an important factor in treatment benefit.
questions.fact: Although in an RCT the benefit of surgery with respect to death from PCa was largest in men < 65 years of age (RR: 0.45)

questions.question: According to the guideline, what percentage of all prostate cancer-related deaths occur in men aged > 75 years?
questions.explanation: Section 6.1.2 states that 'Of all PCa-related deaths, 71% occur in men aged > 75 years'. This highlights the significant disease burden in the elderly population.
questions.fact: Of all PCa-related deaths, 71% occur in men aged > 75 years

questions.question: What minimum radiation dose is required for external beam radiotherapy to show similar cancer control regardless of age?
questions.explanation: The guideline specifies that 'External beam RT shows similar cancer control regardless of age, assuming a dose of > 72Gy when using intensity-modulated or image-guided RT'. This dose threshold is important for age-equivalent outcomes.
questions.fact: External beam RT shows similar cancer control regardless of age, assuming a dose of > 72Gy when using intensity-modulated or image-guided RT

questions.question: What is co-morbidity a major predictor of in localised prostate cancer treated with radical prostatectomy?
questions.explanation: According to section 6.1.3.b, the guideline explicitly states that 'Co-morbidity is a major predictor of non-cancer-specific death in localised PCa treated with RP'. This distinguishes non-cancer causes from cancer-related outcomes.
questions.fact: Co-morbidity is a major predictor of non-cancer-specific death in localised PCa treated with RP and is more important than age

questions.question: According to 10-year outcomes of watchful waiting for prostate cancer, what happened to most men with a high co-morbidity score?

options.text: They died from competing causes irrespective of age or tumour aggressiveness
They survived but developed metastatic disease
They died primarily from prostate cancer
They required salvage therapy within 5 years

questions.explanation: Section 6.1.3.b explicitly states that 'Ten years after watchful waiting for PCa, most men with a high co-morbidity score had died from competing causes, irrespective of age or tumour aggressiveness'. This highlights the impact of co-morbidity on survival outcomes.
questions.fact: Ten years after watchful waiting for PCa, most men with a high co-morbidity score had died from competing causes, irrespective of age or tumour aggressiveness

questions.question: Which of the following are the two validated measures for co-morbidity mentioned in the guideline?

options.text: ECOG Performance Status and Karnofsky Index
ASA Physical Status and Glasgow Coma Scale

questions.explanation: The guideline section 6.1.3.b specifically lists the validated co-morbidity measures as 'Cumulative Illness Score Rating-Geriatrics (CISR-G) [578,579] and Charlson Co-morbidity Index (CCI) [580]', which are the correct instruments for assessing co-morbidity.
questions.fact: Measures for co-morbidity include: Cumulative Illness Score Rating-Geriatrics (CISR-G) and Charlson Co-morbidity Index (CCI)

questions.question: According to the guideline, what percentage of body weight loss over three months indicates 'good nutritional status'?
questions.explanation: The guideline states that good nutritional status is defined as less than 5% weight loss over the previous three months. Weight loss between 5-10% indicates risk of malnutrition, while greater than 10% indicates severe malnutrition.
questions.fact: Good nutritional status is defined as <5% weight loss over the previous three months.

questions.question: Based on the guideline, which weight loss range indicates a 'risk of malnutrition' when estimated from body weight during the previous three months?
questions.explanation: The guideline specifies that a risk of malnutrition is indicated by 5-10% weight loss over the previous three months. This is distinct from good nutritional status (<5% loss) and severe malnutrition (>10% loss).
questions.fact: Risk of malnutrition is defined as 5-10% weight loss over the previous three months.

questions.question: According to the guideline, what percentage of body weight loss indicates 'severe malnutrition' when assessing nutritional status based on weight changes over three months?
questions.explanation: The guideline clearly states that severe malnutrition is indicated by greater than 10% weight loss over the previous three months. This distinguishes it from the risk category (5-10%) and good nutritional status (<5%).
questions.fact: Severe malnutrition is defined as >10% weight loss over the previous three months.

questions.question: At what age does the International SIOG PCa Working Group recommend using the Geriatric 8 (G8) screening tool for treatment decisions?
questions.explanation: The guideline explicitly states that the International SIOG PCa Working Group recommends that treatment for adults over 70 years of age should be based on a systematic evaluation of health status using the Geriatric 8 (G8) screening tool.
questions.fact: International SIOG PCa Working Group recommends that treatment for adults over 70 years of age should be based on a systematic evaluation of health status using the Geriatric 8 (G8) screening tool

questions.question: According to the guideline, what action should be taken for patients with a G8 score of 14 or less?

options.text: Proceed directly with standard cancer treatment
