questions.explanation: Section 6.3.5.f explicitly states that the phase III RCT comparing adjuvant docetaxel against surveillance after RP for locally advanced PCa showed that adjuvant docetaxel did not confer any oncological benefit [965].
questions.fact: adjuvant docetaxel did not confer any oncological benefit

questions.question: What was the reason the TAX3501 trial comparing leuprolide with and without docetaxel ended prematurely?

options.text: Unexpected toxicity in the docetaxel arm
Overwhelming efficacy favoring the combination arm
Regulatory approval of docetaxel for adjuvant use

questions.explanation: Section 6.3.5.f states that the TAX3501 trial comparing the role of leuprolide (18 months) with and without docetaxel (six cycles) ended prematurely due to poor accrual.
questions.fact: ended prematurely due to poor accrual

questions.question: According to the guidelines, PSA persistence after radical prostatectomy (RP) is defined as:

options.text: PSA ≥ 0.1 ng/mL within four to eight weeks of surgery
PSA ≥ 0.2 ng/mL at any time after surgery
PSA ≥ 0.4 ng/mL confirmed on two consecutive measurements

questions.explanation: Section 6.3.6.b defines PSA persistence as a PSA ≥ 0.1 ng/mL within four to eight weeks of surgery. This definition is used to standardize reporting across studies.
questions.section_title: 6.3.6.b. Predictors of PSA persistence after RP
questions.fact: PSA persistence, defined as a PSA ≥ 0.1ng/mL within four to eight weeks of surgery, is observed in 5–20% of patients

questions.question: When PSA is measured within three weeks after radical prostatectomy in unselected patients:

options.text: 21% have PSA > 0.1 ng/mL and most remain detectable on repeat testing
77% have PSA > 0.1 ng/mL, but only 21% remain detectable on repeat testing
77% have PSA > 0.1 ng/mL, and all remain detectable on repeat testing
10% have PSA > 0.1 ng/mL, with 80% becoming undetectable on repeat testing

questions.explanation: Section 6.3.6.b states that among patients tested within three weeks of RP, 77% had PSA > 0.1 ng/mL, but only 21% remained detectable on repeat testing. This highlights the importance of timing in PSA interpretation.
questions.section_title: 6.3.6.b. Predictors of PSA persistence after RP
questions.fact: Among patients tested within three weeks of RP, 77% had PSA > 0.1ng/mL, but only 21% remained detectable on repeat testing

questions.question: Which patient group is particularly prone to misclassification if PSA testing occurs too early after radical prostatectomy?

options.text: Patients with organ-confined disease on final pathology

questions.explanation: Section 6.3.6.b specifically states that patients with preoperative PSA ≥ 20 ng/mL are particularly prone to misclassification if tested too early, as delayed measurements (≥3 months) have shown that some of these patients eventually reach undetectable PSA levels.
questions.section_title: 6.3.6.b. Predictors of PSA persistence after RP
questions.fact: Patients with preoperative PSA ≥ 20ng/mL are particularly prone to misclassification if tested too early, as delayed measurements (≥ 3 months) have shown that some of these patients eventually reach undetectable PSA levels

questions.question: Which of the following pathological features has been associated with persistent PSA after radical prostatectomy?
questions.explanation: Section 6.3.6.c explicitly states that cribriform pattern or intraductal carcinoma have been associated with persistent PSA after radical prostatectomy.
questions.section_title: 6.3.6.c. Natural history of persistently elevated PSA after RP
questions.fact: Cribriform pattern or intraductal carcinoma have also been associated with persistent PSA [981].

questions.question: According to the meta-analysis data, what percentage of patients with miN1 PCa and more than two positive spots and/or extraprostatic extension experience PSA persistence after radical prostatectomy?
questions.explanation: Section 6.3.6.c provides the quantitative finding that more than 50% of patients with miN1 PCa and more than two positive spots and/or extraprostatic extension experience PSA persistence.
questions.section_title: 6.3.6.c. Natural history of persistently elevated PSA after RP
questions.fact: More than 50% of patients with miN1 PCa and more than two positive spots and/or extraprostatic extension experiencing PSA persistence [982,983].

questions.question: According to the guideline section on natural history, what represents one of the strongest predictors of persistently detectable PSA levels after surgery?

options.text: PSMA PET uptake in the pelvic nodes
Choline PET uptake in the prostate bed

questions.explanation: Section 6.3.6.c explicitly identifies that uptake in the pelvic nodes at PSMA PET represents one of the strongest predictors of persistently detectable PSA levels after surgery.
questions.section_title: 6.3.6.c. Natural history of persistently elevated PSA after RP
questions.fact: An uptake in the pelvic nodes at PSMA PET represents one of the strongest predictors of persistently detectable PSA levels after surgery [982,983].

questions.question: What is the threshold value that defines persistent PSA after radical prostatectomy (RP)?
questions.explanation: According to Section 6.3.6.d, persistent PSA is defined as ≥ 0.1 ng/mL after radical prostatectomy, which is strongly correlated with poor oncologic outcomes including metastasis and cancer-specific mortality.
questions.section_title: 6.3.6.d. Imaging in patients with persistently elevated PSA after RP
questions.fact: Persistent PSA is defined as ≥ 0.1 ng/mL

questions.question: According to a large retrospective study (n = 11,605), what was the 15-year metastasis-free survival rate for patients with persistent versus undetectable PSA after RP?
questions.explanation: Section 6.3.6.d reports that at fifteen years post-RP, MFS rates were 53 versus 93% (p < 0.001) for persistent versus undetectable PSA groups, demonstrating the significant prognostic impact of PSA persistence.
questions.section_title: 6.3.6.d. Imaging in patients with persistently elevated PSA after RP
questions.fact: At fifteen years post RP, MFS rates were 53 versus 93% for persistent versus undetectable PSA

questions.question: In multivariable analysis, which combination of factors was significantly associated with the development of distant metastases among patients with persistent PSA after RP?

options.text: PSA slope ≥ 0.05 and pathological ISUP grade group ≥ 3 vs ≤ 2
Initial PSA level and surgical margin status
Age at surgery and PSA velocity
Pathological T-stage and lymph node involvement

questions.explanation: Section 6.3.6.d states that PSA slope ≥ 0.05 (calculated using PSA levels 3-12 months after surgery) and pathological ISUP grade group (≥ 3 vs ≤ 2) were significantly associated with distant metastases in multivariable analysis.
questions.section_title: 6.3.6.d. Imaging in patients with persistently elevated PSA after RP
questions.fact: PSA slope ≥ 0.05 and pathological ISUP grade group (≥ 3 vs ≤ 2) were significantly associated with distant metastases

questions.question: According to multivariable Cox regression models, what was the hazard ratio for metastasis with persistent PSA after RP?
questions.explanation: Section 6.3.6.d reports that persistent PSA represented an independent predictor for metastasis with HR: 3.59 (p < 0.001) in multivariable Cox regression models, indicating nearly a 3.6-fold increased risk.
questions.section_title: 6.3.6.d. Imaging in patients with persistently elevated PSA after RP
questions.fact: Persistent PSA was an independent predictor for metastasis with HR: 3.59

questions.question: How is PSA slope most commonly reported in clinical practice?

options.text: As a linear value in ng/mL per year
As PSA doubling time calculated by log [PSA slope]
As percentage change from baseline PSA
As absolute difference in consecutive PSA measurements

questions.explanation: Section 6.3.6.d states that PSA slope is more commonly reported as PSA doubling time, which is calculated by log [PSA slope], making this the standard method for expressing PSA kinetics.
questions.section_title: 6.3.6.d. Imaging in patients with persistently elevated PSA after RP
questions.fact: PSA slope is more commonly reported as PSA doubling time (calculated by log [PSA slope])

questions.question: In patients with persistently elevated PSA after radical prostatectomy, what percentage had a positive 68Ga-PSMA-PET/CT result?
questions.explanation: The multicentre retrospective study of 191 patients with persistently elevated PSA after RP found that 68Ga-PSMA-PET/CT was positive in 68% of patients.
questions.section_title: 6.3.6.e. Management options for patients with persistent PSA
questions.fact: A multicentre retrospective study included 191 patients with persistently elevated PSA after RP and 68 Ga-PSMA-PET/CT was positive in 68%

questions.question: According to the multicentre retrospective study, what proportion of patients with persistent PSA after RP had disease confined to the pelvis versus distant metastases?
questions.explanation: The study found that 35% of patients had disease confined to the pelvis (obturator, presacral/mesorectal most common) and 33% had distant metastases.
questions.section_title: 6.3.6.e. Management options for patients with persistent PSA
questions.fact: 35% of whom had disease confined to the pelvis (obturator, presacral/mesorectal most common) and 33% had distant metastases

questions.question: In a study of 129 patients who had either persistent PSA (52%) or biochemical recurrence (48%) after radical prostatectomy, what was observed regarding pelvic nodal involvement on PSMA PET/CT?

options.text: Men with persistent PSA had significantly less pelvic nodal involvement
Men with persistent PSA had significantly more pelvic nodal involvement
There was no significant difference between the two groups
Pelvic nodal involvement was only seen in patients with biochemical recurrence

questions.explanation: The study of 129 patients showed that men with persistent PSA had significantly more pelvic nodal involvement on PSMA PET/CT than those with an initially undetectable PSA who developed BCR.
questions.section_title: 6.3.6.e. Management options for patients with persistent PSA
questions.fact: A study of 129 patients who had either persistent PSA (52%) or BCR (48%) after RP, showed that men with a persistent PSA had significantly more pelvic nodal involvement on PSMA PET/CT than those with an initially undetectable PSA

questions.question: What 10-year cancer-specific survival (CSS) rate has been demonstrated with the combination of radical prostatectomy and early adjuvant hormonal therapy in pN+ prostate cancer?
questions.explanation: The text states in section 6.3.5.f.1 that 'the combination of RP and early adjuvant HT in pN+ PCa has been shown to achieve a 10-year CSS rate of 80%'. This represents the survival outcome demonstrated in prospective RCTs for this patient population.
questions.section_title: 6.3.5.g. Recommendations for adjuvant treatment for pN0 and pN1 disease after radical prostatectomy*
questions.fact: The combination of RP and early adjuvant HT in pN+ PCa achieves a 10-year CSS rate of 80%

questions.question: According to the guideline, which patient subgroup with pN1 disease did NOT benefit from immediate adjuvant therapy?

options.text: Men with low-volume nodal disease (<3 LNs) and ISUP grade group 2-5
Men with three to four positive nodes
Men without any adverse pathological features
Men with pT3-4 or R1 disease

questions.explanation: The text in section 6.3.5.f.2 cites a US National Database study reporting that 'Men without any adverse pathological features did not benefit from immediate adjuvant therapy', while men with adverse features showed improved OS with ADT plus EBRT.
questions.section_title: 6.3.5.g. Recommendations for adjuvant treatment for pN0 and pN1 disease after radical prostatectomy*
questions.fact: Men without any adverse pathological features did not benefit from immediate adjuvant therapy

questions.question: What percentage of pN1 patients with one or two positive lymph nodes remained metastasis-free without need of salvage treatment at median follow-up of 60.2 months?
questions.explanation: Section 6.3.5.f.3 states that 'An analysis of 209 pN1 patients with one or two positive LNs at RP showed that 37% remained metastasis-free without need of salvage treatment at a median follow-up of 60.2 months', supporting observation as a viable option in selected patients.
questions.section_title: 6.3.5.g. Recommendations for adjuvant treatment for pN0 and pN1 disease after radical prostatectomy*
questions.fact: 37% of pN1 patients with 1-2 positive LNs remained metastasis-free without salvage treatment at median follow-up of 60.2 months

questions.question: What is the expected outcome for patients who have PSA persistence after radical prostatectomy (RP)?

options.text: Worse outcomes compared to patients without PSA persistence
Better outcomes due to early detection of recurrence
Outcomes similar to patients with detectable but stable PSA
Outcomes that are independent of PSA kinetics

questions.explanation: The guideline states that 'patients with PSA persistence after RP have worse outcomes' compared to those without persistence. This establishes PSA persistence as a negative prognostic factor.
questions.section_title: 6.3.6.g. Recommendations for the management of persistent PSA after radical prostatectomy
questions.fact: patients with PSA persistence after RP have worse outcomes

questions.question: What type of treatment approach do patients with PSA persistence after radical prostatectomy benefit most from?
questions.explanation: According to the guideline, patients with PSA persistence 'serve to benefit most from early aggressive multimodality treatment.' This suggests combining multiple treatment modalities early in the disease course.
questions.section_title: 6.3.6.g. Recommendations for the management of persistent PSA after radical prostatectomy
questions.fact: serve to benefit most from early aggressive multimodality treatment

questions.question: What is the primary limitation preventing firm recommendations for managing PSA persistence after radical prostatectomy?

options.text: Lack of prospective randomized controlled trials
Insufficient number of patients with PSA persistence
Absence of validated PSA cutoff values
Limited availability of multimodality treatment options

questions.explanation: The guideline explicitly states that 'the lack of prospective RCTs makes firm recommendations difficult,' indicating that the evidence base for this clinical scenario is primarily observational rather than from randomized trials.
questions.section_title: 6.3.6.g. Recommendations for the management of persistent PSA after radical prostatectomy
questions.fact: the lack of prospective RCTs makes firm recommendations difficult

questions.question: Which imaging modality is recommended for males with persistent PSA and rising PSA levels after curative intent treatment?

options.text: Prostate-specific membrane antigen positron emission tomography/computed tomography (PSMA PET/CT)

questions.explanation: The guideline weakly recommends offering a PSMA PET/CT scan to males with persistent and rising PSA when results will influence subsequent treatment decisions (Section 6.4).
questions.section_title: 6.4. Management of PSA-only recurrence after treatment with curative intent
questions.fact: Offer a prostate-specific membrane antigen (PSMA) positron emission tomography/computed tomography (PET/CT) scan to males with a persistent prostate-specific antigen (PSA) and rising if the results will influence subsequent treatment decisions.

questions.question: What is the recommended treatment for males with persistent PSA after curative intent treatment who have no evidence of distant metastatic disease?

options.text: Salvage radiotherapy and additional hormonal therapy
Active surveillance with repeated PSA monitoring

questions.explanation: According to Section 6.4, males with persistent PSA and no evidence of distant metastatic disease should be treated with salvage radiotherapy and additional hormonal therapy (weak recommendation).
questions.section_title: 6.4. Management of PSA-only recurrence after treatment with curative intent
questions.fact: Treat males with persistent PSA and no evidence of distant metastatic disease with salvage radiotherapy and additional hormonal therapy.

questions.question: Under what condition should PSMA PET/CT be offered to patients with persistent PSA after curative treatment?

options.text: Only when PSA level exceeds 10 ng/mL
When the results will influence subsequent treatment decisions
Only after confirmation of bone metastases on bone scan
Regardless of whether it will affect treatment planning

questions.explanation: The guideline specifies that PSMA PET/CT should be offered to males with persistent and rising PSA if the results will influence subsequent treatment decisions (Section 6.4).
questions.section_title: 6.4. Management of PSA-only recurrence after treatment with curative intent
questions.fact: Offer a prostate-specific membrane antigen (PSMA) positron emission tomography/computed tomography (PET/CT) scan to males with a persistent prostate-specific antigen (PSA) and rising if the results will influence subsequent treatment decisions.

questions.question: According to the RTOG 9601 trial comparing patients with persistent PSA versus biochemical recurrence (BCR) after radical prostatectomy, what is the 10-year metastatic progression rate for patients with persistent PSA?
questions.explanation: The RTOG 9601 trial showed significantly higher 10-year metastatic progression rate of 29% in patients with persistent PSA compared to 10% in patients with BCR (p < 0.0001). This demonstrates that persistent PSA after radical prostatectomy represents a much poorer prognostic indicator than BCR.
questions.fact: Higher 10-year metastatic progression rate (29% vs. 10%, p < 0.0001)

questions.question: In the ARO 96-02 prospective RCT comparing patients with PSA persistence versus BCR after radical prostatectomy, what was the 10-year overall survival rate for PSA persistence patients who received immediate salvage radiotherapy?
questions.explanation: The ARO 96-02 trial demonstrated that patients with PSA persistence who received immediate SRT had a 10-year overall survival of 68%, compared to 84% for BCR patients, showing worse outcomes for PSA persistence.
questions.fact: 10-year overall survival (68% vs 84%) for PSA persistence vs BCR patients

questions.question: According to the propensity score matching study cited in the guidelines, what was the 10-year cancer-specific survival rate for patients with PSA persistence who received SRT versus those who did not?
questions.explanation: The propensity score matching study showed 10-year cancer-specific survival rates of 94% versus 82% for patients treated with SRT versus no RT (p < 0.01), demonstrating a significant survival benefit for salvage radiotherapy in PSA persistence.
questions.fact: 10-year cancer-specific survival rates were 94 versus 82% for SRT vs no RT

questions.question: What percentage range of patients undergoing radical prostatectomy (RP) or radiotherapy (RT) develop a rising PSA (PSA recurrence)?
questions.explanation: The guideline section 6.4.1.a explicitly states that between 27% and 53% of all patients undergoing RP or RT develop a rising PSA (PSA recurrence).
questions.fact: Between 27% and 53% of all patients undergoing RP or RT develop a rising PSA (PSA recurrence).

questions.question: According to the guideline, what is the relationship between metastatic progression and rising PSA levels?

options.text: Metastatic progression is universally preceded by rising PSA levels
Metastatic progression only occurs in patients with rapidly rising PSA
Metastatic progression is unrelated to PSA levels

questions.explanation: The guideline section 6.4.1.a clearly states that metastatic progression is universally preceded by rising PSA levels, indicating that a rising PSA is always observed before detectable metastases develop.
questions.fact: Metastatic progression is universally preceded by rising PSA levels.

questions.question: When should treatment recommendations for patients with PSA-only recurrence be given according to the guideline?

options.text: After discussion in a multidisciplinary team

questions.explanation: The guideline section 6.4.1.a explicitly emphasizes that treatment recommendations for patients with PSA-only recurrence should be given after discussion in a multidisciplinary team, ensuring comprehensive evaluation of the case.
questions.fact: It should be emphasised that the treatment recommendations for these patients should be given after discussion in a multidisciplinary team.

questions.question: What is the potential natural history of PSA-only recurrence according to the guideline?

options.text: It may be prolonged and measurable PSA may not lead to clinically apparent metastatic disease
It always progresses rapidly to metastatic disease
It typically requires immediate systemic treatment
It invariably leads to death within 5 years

questions.explanation: The guideline section 6.4.1.a states that physicians must inform patients that the natural history of PSA-only recurrence may be prolonged and that a measurable PSA may not necessarily lead to clinically apparent metastatic disease, highlighting the variable course of this condition.
questions.fact: The natural history of PSA-only recurrence may be prolonged and that a measurable PSA may not necessarily lead to clinically apparent metastatic disease.

questions.question: What type of increase in serum PSA does PSA doubling time (PSA-DT) measure?

options.text: Absolute annual increase in serum PSA
Exponential increase in serum PSA over time
Linear increase in serum PSA over time
Rate of change relative to initial PSA value

questions.explanation: The guideline explicitly states that PSA-DT 'measures the exponential increase in serum PSA over time' (Section 6.4.2). This distinguishes it from PSA velocity, which measures absolute annual increase.
questions.section_title: 6.4.2. Controversies in the definitions of clinically relevant PSA relapse
questions.fact: PSA-DT measures the exponential increase in serum PSA over time.

questions.question: According to the guideline, how many PSA measurements are required at minimum for PSA-DT calculation?
questions.explanation: The guideline explicitly states that 'At least three PSA measurements are required' for PSA-DT calculation (Section 6.4.2). This is one of the established rules for PSA-DT calculation.
questions.section_title: 6.4.2. Controversies in the definitions of clinically relevant PSA relapse
questions.fact: At least three PSA measurements are required for PSA-DT calculation.

questions.question: What is the PSA-DT threshold that identifies patients with high-risk non-metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (nmCRPC) who benefit from intensified therapy?
questions.explanation: The guideline explicitly states that PSA-DT 'identifies patients with high-risk nmCRPC who benefit from intensified therapy (PSA-DT threshold < 10 months)' (Section 6.4.2). This threshold distinguishes high-risk from low-risk nmCRPC patients.
questions.section_title: 6.4.2. Controversies in the definitions of clinically relevant PSA relapse
questions.fact: PSA-DT threshold < 10 months identifies patients with high-risk nmCRPC who benefit from intensified therapy.

questions.question: What is the PSA threshold after radical prostatectomy (RP) that best predicts further metastases?

options.text: PSA > 2 ng/mL higher than the nadir

questions.explanation: Section 6.4.3 states that after RP, the threshold that best predicts further metastases is a PSA > 0.4 ng/mL and rising. This is the established endpoint for defining biochemical recurrence after radical prostatectomy.
questions.fact: After RP, the threshold that best predicts further metastases is a PSA > 0.4 ng/mL and rising [1015].

questions.question: According to the RTOG-ASTRO Phoenix Consensus Conference definition, what constitutes PSA failure after primary radiotherapy?

options.text: Any PSA increase > 2 ng/mL higher than the PSA nadir value
Any PSA increase > 1 ng/mL from nadir regardless of concentration
PSA exceeding 10 ng/mL at any point

questions.explanation: Section 6.4.3 defines the Phoenix Consensus Conference definition as 'any PSA increase > 2 ng/mL higher than the PSA nadir value, regardless of the serum concentration of the nadir.' This definition has an accuracy of >80% for clinical failure.
questions.fact: The RTOG-ASTRO Phoenix Consensus Conference definition of PSA failure is 'any PSA increase > 2 ng/mL higher than the PSA nadir value, regardless of the serum concentration of the nadir' [1016].

questions.question: Which of the following statements about PSA endpoints after HIFU or cryotherapy is correct?

options.text: No endpoints have been validated against clinical progression or survival
The Phoenix definition (PSA + 2 ng/mL) should be used
PSA > 0.4 ng/mL is the validated threshold

questions.explanation: Section 6.4.3 explicitly states that after HIFU or cryotherapy, no endpoints have been validated against clinical progression or survival. Therefore, a firm recommendation of an acceptable PSA threshold cannot be offered for these alternative local treatments.
questions.fact: After HIFU or cryotherapy, no endpoints have been validated against clinical progression or survival. Therefore, it is not possible to offer a firm recommendation of an acceptable PSA threshold after these alternative local treatments [994].

questions.question: According to the EAU guidelines, under what condition is imaging considered to have value in the assessment of metastases in prostate cancer?

options.text: When it leads to a treatment change that results in an improved outcome
When it provides accurate anatomical staging information
When it helps in predicting disease progression
When it assists in determining patient prognosis

questions.explanation: The guideline states that 'Imaging is only of value if it leads to a treatment change that results in an improved outcome.' This establishes a clear threshold for the utility of imaging studies in recurrent prostate cancer.
questions.fact: Imaging is only of value if it leads to a treatment change that results in an improved outcome.

questions.question: What does the EAU guideline state about the evidence supporting imaging at recurrence in prostate cancer?

options.text: Very limited data are available regarding the outcome's consequence on imaging at recurrence
Extensive clinical trials have demonstrated clear benefits of imaging at recurrence
Multiple randomized controlled trials support routine imaging protocols
No data exists on the use of imaging at recurrence

questions.explanation: The guideline acknowledges that 'In practice, however, very limited data are available regarding the outcome's consequent on imaging at recurrence.' This highlights the evidence gap in guiding imaging decisions for recurrent disease.
questions.fact: Very limited data are available regarding the outcome's consequent on imaging at recurrence.

questions.question: What is the approximate PSA threshold for MRI positivity in detecting local recurrence after radical prostatectomy?
questions.explanation: Section 6.4.4.b.1 states that the PSA threshold for MRI positivity appears to be between 0.3 and 0.5 ng/mL, and PSA kinetics also influence MRI positivity even at low PSA values.
questions.section_title: 6.4.4.c. Summary of evidence of imaging in case of biochemical recurrence
questions.fact: The PSA threshold for MRI positivity appears to be between 0.3 and 0.5ng/mL

questions.question: According to the guideline, when should biopsies be obtained after initial radiation therapy to be considered a major predictor of outcome?

options.text: At least 6 months after initial treatment
At least 12 months after initial treatment
At least 18 months after initial treatment
At least 24 months after initial treatment

questions.explanation: Section 6.4.4.b.2 explicitly states that in patients with BCR after RT, biopsy status is a major predictor of outcome provided the biopsies are obtained at least 24 months after initial treatment.
questions.section_title: 6.4.4.c. Summary of evidence of imaging in case of biochemical recurrence
questions.fact: biopsy status is a major predictor of outcome, provided the biopsies are obtained at least 24 months after initial treatment

questions.question: What proportion of intra-prostatic recurrences in intermediate- and high-risk patients appeared at the location of the primary tumour in the FLAME trial?
questions.explanation: Section 6.4.4.b.2 states that in the FLAME trial, intra-prostatic recurrences in intermediate- and high-risk patients appeared at the location of the primary tumour in 98% of cases.
questions.section_title: 6.4.4.c. Summary of evidence of imaging in case of biochemical recurrence
questions.fact: Intra-prostatic recurrences in intermediate- and high-risk patients appeared at the location of the primary tumour in 98% of cases

questions.question: Which imaging system has been launched to standardise MRI interpretation in the context of BCR after radical prostatectomy or radiation therapy?
questions.explanation: Section 6.4.4.b.1 states that the Prostate Imaging for Recurrence Reporting (PI-RR) system has been recently launched to standardise MRI interpretation in the context of BCR after RP or RT.
questions.section_title: 6.4.4.c. Summary of evidence of imaging in case of biochemical recurrence
questions.fact: The Prostate Imaging for Recurrence Reporting (PI-RR) system has been recently launched

questions.question: What is the size threshold for a relapse at the vesico-urethral anastomosis that is associated with excellent prognosis at salvage radiation therapy?
questions.explanation: Section 6.4.4.b.1 states that a small (≤ 0.4cc) relapse located at the vesico-urethral anastomosis is associated with excellent prognosis at salvage RT.
questions.section_title: 6.4.4.c. Summary of evidence of imaging in case of biochemical recurrence
questions.fact: A small (≤ 0.4cc) relapse located at the vesico-urethral anastomosis is associated with excellent prognosis at salvage RT

questions.question: According to the EAU guidelines, what is the current status regarding the timing and treatment modality for PSA-only recurrences after radical prostatectomy?

options.text: Both timing and treatment modality are well-established based on robust evidence
Timing and treatment modality remain controversial based on limited evidence
Treatment modality is established but timing remains controversial
Timing is established but treatment modality remains controversial

questions.explanation: Section 6.4.5.a explicitly states that both timing and treatment modality for PSA-only recurrences after RP or RT remain a matter of controversy based on the limited evidence available.
questions.section_title: 6.4.5.a. Treatment of PSA-only recurrences after radical prostatectomy
questions.fact: The timing and treatment modality for PSA-only recurrences after RP or RT remain a matter of controversy based on the limited evidence.

questions.question: What is the primary reason cited in the EAU guidelines for the controversy surrounding treatment of PSA-only recurrences after radical prostatectomy or radiotherapy?

options.text: Lack of standardised PSA measurement protocols across institutions
Limited evidence available in the literature
Conflicting expert opinions among urologists and radiation oncologists
Insufficient patient enrollment in clinical trials

questions.explanation: The guideline section 6.4.5.a specifically states that the controversy regarding timing and treatment modality is based on the limited evidence, making this the primary reason for the current uncertainty.
questions.section_title: 6.4.5.a. Treatment of PSA-only recurrences after radical prostatectomy
questions.fact: The timing and treatment modality for PSA-only recurrences after RP or RT remain a matter of controversy based on the limited evidence.

questions.question: What is the PSA threshold for performing PSMA PET/CT after radical prostatectomy according to EAU guidelines?
questions.explanation: The guideline states PSMA PET/CT should be performed 'if the PSA level is > 0.2 ng/mL' after radical prostatectomy. This specific threshold distinguishes candidates for imaging from those with lower unmeasurable PSA levels.
questions.fact: PSA level is > 0.2 ng/mL

questions.question: Which additional criterion must be satisfied before performing PSMA PET/CT in patients with PSA recurrence after radical prostatectomy?

options.text: Results will influence subsequent treatment decisions
PSA doubling time less than 12 months
Absence of bone metastases on bone scan

questions.explanation: The guideline specifies PSMA PET/CT should be performed 'if the results will influence subsequent treatment decisions (i.e. EAU BCR risk groups)' after radical prostatectomy. This ensures imaging is only used when it will actually guide clinical management.
questions.fact: if the results will influence subsequent treatment decisions (i.e. EAU BCR risk groups)

questions.question: What is the strength rating for performing PSMA PET/CT in patients with PSA recurrence after radiotherapy who are fit for curative salvage treatment?
questions.explanation: The guideline gives PSMA PET/CT a 'Strong' strength rating for patients with PSA recurrence after radiotherapy who are fit for curative salvage treatment. This contrasts with the post-prostatectomy setting where the recommendation is weaker.
questions.fact: Strong - Perform PSMA PET/CT in patients fit for curative salvage treatment (after radiotherapy)

questions.question: According to the systematic review, which salvage treatment modality had the lowest five-year recurrence-free survival rate?
questions.explanation: The systematic review stated that five-year RFS ranged from 50% after cryotherapy to 60% after HDR BT and SBRT, making cryotherapy the modality with the lowest five-year recurrence-free survival rate among those compared.
questions.fact: Five-year RFS ranged from 50% after cryotherapy to 60% after HDR BT and SBRT

questions.question: In the study of 568 patients with rising PSA after primary radiotherapy who underwent PSMA PET/CT, what percentage had isolated local recurrence according to Phoenix criteria?
questions.explanation: The guideline states that a study evaluating 568 patients found isolated local recurrence was detected in only 32% of patients meeting the Phoenix criteria for BCR after RT, while distant metastases were present in 49% of cases.
questions.fact: Isolated local recurrence was detected in only 32% of patients meeting the Phoenix criteria for BCR after RT

questions.question: What type of androgens does androgen deprivation therapy target according to the guideline section on castration level?
questions.explanation: The section explicitly states that androgen deprivation can be achieved by suppressing the secretion of testicular androgens. This is the specific target of castration-level therapy described in this chapter.
questions.fact: Androgen deprivation can be achieved by suppressing the secretion of testicular androgens in various ways.

questions.question: According to the guideline, androgen deprivation is achieved by suppressing the secretion of androgens from which anatomical source?
questions.explanation: The guideline section clearly states that suppressing the secretion of testicular androgens is the method for achieving androgen deprivation at the castration level.
questions.fact: Androgen deprivation can be achieved by suppressing the secretion of testicular androgens in various ways.

questions.question: The guideline states that androgen deprivation by suppressing testicular androgen secretion can be achieved in how many ways?
questions.explanation: The section explicitly mentions that suppressing testicular androgen secretion can be achieved 'in various ways,' indicating multiple methods are available for achieving this type of androgen deprivation.
questions.fact: Androgen deprivation can be achieved by suppressing the secretion of testicular androgens in various ways.

questions.question: According to current guidelines, what should be the preferred target testosterone level for achieving castration in prostate cancer?
questions.explanation: Section 6.5.1.b states that a preferred target level should be defined as <20ng/dL (<0.7 nmol/L), based on current methods showing the mean value after surgical castration is 15ng/dL, which is lower than the historical threshold of 50ng/dL.
questions.fact: A preferred target level should be defined as <20ng/dL (<0.7 nmol/L)

questions.question: The historical definition of castrate testosterone level (<50ng/dL) was established how many years ago?
questions.explanation: Section 6.5.1.b explicitly states that the castration level of testosterone <50ng/dL was defined more than 40 years ago when testosterone testing was less sensitive, which is why the preferred target level has now been updated.
questions.fact: The castration level of testosterone <50ng/dL was defined more than 40 years ago when testosterone testing was less sensitive

questions.question: Which testosterone level is still used by regulatory authorities and in clinical trials addressing castration in prostate cancer?
questions.explanation: Section 6.5.1.b clearly states that the castrate level considered by regulatory authorities and in clinical trials addressing castration in prostate cancer is still the historical <50ng/dL (1.7 nmol/L), even though a lower preferred target is now recommended.
questions.fact: The castrate level considered by regulatory authorities and in clinical trials addressing castration in PCa is still the historical <50ng/dL (1.7 nmol/L)

questions.question: What is considered the primary treatment modality for androgen deprivation therapy (ADT)?
questions.explanation: The guideline states that bilateral orchiectomy or subcapsular pulpectomy is still considered the primary treatment modality for ADT, described as a simple, cheap and low-complication procedure.
questions.fact: Bilateral orchiectomy or subcapsular pulpectomy is still considered the primary treatment modality for ADT

questions.question: How quickly is castration level typically achieved following bilateral orchiectomy?
questions.explanation: According to Section 6.5.1.c, bilateral orchiectomy achieves castration levels within less than twelve hours, making it the quickest way to achieve castration compared to other ADT modalities.
questions.fact: Castration level that is usually reached within less than twelve hours

questions.question: Why does bilateral orchiectomy not allow for intermittent treatment?

options.text: Because it has high complication rates

questions.explanation: Section 6.5.1.c explicitly states that bilateral orchiectomy is irreversible and therefore does not allow for intermittent treatment, unlike reversible forms of ADT that can be paused and restarted.
questions.fact: It is irreversible and therefore does not allow for intermittent treatment

questions.question: When does the testosterone flare or 'flare-up' phenomenon typically begin after the first LHRH agonist injection?

options.text: Two to three days after administration
One to two weeks after administration
At the time of the next scheduled injection

questions.explanation: Section 6.5.1.d states that the 'testosterone surge' or 'flare-up' phenomenon starts two to three days after administration and lasts for approximately one week. The first injection induces a transient rise in LH and FSH, which is the cause of this flare.
questions.fact: The flare-up phenomenon starts two to three days after administration and lasts for approximately one week

questions.question: How does chronic exposure to LHRH agonists achieve testosterone suppression?

options.text: By directly blocking testosterone production in the testes
By causing an initial spike in LHRH receptor activity
By downregulating LHRH receptors, suppressing LH and FSH secretion
By increasing the sensitivity of pituitary gonadotrophs to GnRH

questions.explanation: Section 6.5.1.d explains that chronic exposure to LHRH agonists results in downregulation of LHRH receptors, which suppresses LH and FSH secretion and therefore testosterone production. This mechanism leads to achieving castration levels within two to four weeks.
questions.fact: Chronic exposure to LHRH agonists results in the downregulation of LHRH receptors, suppressing LH and FSH secretion and therefore testosterone production

questions.question: According to the guideline, what is the evidence base for the timing and duration of anti-androgen therapy at LHRH agonist initiation?

options.text: Supported by multiple large randomised controlled trials
Based on strong evidence from meta-analyses
Neither the timing nor the duration are based on strong evidence
Only the duration is evidence-based, timing is not

questions.explanation: Section 6.5.1.d clearly states that anti-androgen therapy is usually continued for four weeks but neither the timing nor the duration of anti-androgen therapy are based on strong evidence. The guideline also notes that more recent analyses question the clinical relevance of the flare in most patients.
questions.fact: Neither the timing nor the duration of anti-androgen therapy are based on strong evidence

questions.question: What is the standard dosage regimen for Degarelix in the first-line treatment of advanced prostate cancer?

options.text: 80 mg loading dose followed by 240 mg monthly injections
240 mg in the first month, followed by monthly injections of 80 mg
160 mg loading dose followed by 80 mg biweekly injections

questions.explanation: According to Section 6.5.1.e, Degarelix is administered at a standard dosage of 240 mg in the first month, followed by monthly injections of 80 mg. This loading dose regimen ensures adequate testosterone suppression.
questions.fact: The standard dosage is 240 mg in the first month, followed by monthly injections of 80 mg.

questions.question: By which time point do most patients achieve castrate testosterone levels following Degarelix initiation?

options.text: At the end of the first month

questions.explanation: Section 6.5.1.e states that most patients achieve a castrate level at day three following initiation of Degarelix, reflecting the rapid onset of testosterone suppression achieved by LHRH antagonists.
questions.fact: Most patients achieve a castrate level at day three [1153].

questions.question: Which adverse effects are reported to occur LESS frequently with LHRH agonists compared to LHRH antagonists?

options.text: Decreased libido, hot flushes, ED, weight gain and injection site reactions

questions.explanation: According to Section 6.5.1.e, short-term follow-up data indicate that while LHRH antagonists are associated with lower cardiovascular events, other adverse effects including decreased libido, hot flushes, ED, weight gain, and injection site reactions are seen less often with agonists.
questions.fact: Other adverse effects, such as decreased libido, hot flushes, ED, weight gain and injection site reactions, are seen less often with the agonists [1158,1159].

questions.question: What is the primary advantage of Relugolix over other available LHRH antagonists?

options.text: It has a longer duration of action requiring only quarterly dosing
It is administered as an oral formulation
It does not require a loading dose
It can be used in combination with chemotherapy

questions.explanation: Section 6.5.1.e specifically identifies Relugolix as an oral LHRH antagonist, distinguishing it from injectable formulations like Degarelix. This oral administration route represents a practical advantage over existing injectable options.
questions.fact: Relugolix is an oral LHRH antagonist.

questions.question: What is the primary mechanism of action of paclitaxel derivatives in treating prostate cancer?

options.text: Promotion of microtubule assembly and inhibition of depolymerisation
Direct binding to DNA causing strand breaks

questions.explanation: According to Section 6.5.3, paclitaxel derivatives promote the assembly of microtubules and inhibit their subsequent depolymerisation, impairing the tubulin dynamics necessary for mitotic spindle assembly during mitosis.
questions.fact: Paclitaxel derivatives promote the assembly of microtubules and inhibit the subsequent depolymerisation, impairing the tubulin dynamics that foster the mitotic spindle assembly during interphase in mitosis.

questions.question: In addition to its microtubule-binding effects, what additional mechanism does cabazitaxel use to inhibit androgen receptor activity?

options.text: Binding to microtubule-associated motor protein dynein to prevent androgen receptor nuclear translocation
Directly blocking androgen receptor binding sites on DNA
Inhibiting testosterone synthesis in the adrenal glands
Degrading androgen receptor proteins through proteasome activation

questions.explanation: Section 6.5.3 states that cabazitaxel inhibits androgen receptors by binding to microtubules and microtubule-associated motor protein dynein, which prevents androgen receptor nuclear translocation.
questions.fact: Cabazitaxel also inhibits androgen receptors by binding to the microtubules and microtubule-associated motor protein dynein. As a consequence, androgen receptor nuclear translocation is prevented.

questions.question: What is the stoichiometric ratio at which docetaxel binds to β-tubulin dimers?
questions.explanation: According to Section 6.5.3, docetaxel binds β-tubulin dimers in a 1:1 stoichiometric ratio, which allows it to exhibit its inhibitory effect on tubulin depolymerisation.
questions.fact: Docetaxel binds β-tubulin dimers in a 1:1 stoichiometric ratio, exhibiting a stronger dynamic instability using its inhibitory effect in tubulin depolymerisation.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT listed as a common side effect of taxane therapy in the guideline?
questions.explanation: Section 6.5.3 explicitly lists peripheral neuropathy, myalgias, neutropenia, and arthralgia as common side effects. Alopecia is not mentioned in the text as a common side effect of these drugs.
questions.fact: Common side effects include peripheral neuropathy, myalgias, neutropenia and arthralgia.

questions.question: PARP inhibitors (PARPi) were developed to selectively target cancer cells harbouring which of the following?

options.text: BRCA mutations and other mutations inducing homologous recombination deficiency
TP53 mutations and increased cellular proliferation
Androgen receptor overexpression and PSA production
PTEN loss and PI3K pathway activation

questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that PARPi were developed to target cancer cells harbouring BRCA mutations and other mutations inducing homologous recombination deficiency. This is the foundational concept for understanding PARPi mechanism of action.
questions.fact: PARPi were developed aiming to selectively target cancer cells harbouring BRCA mutations and other mutations inducing homologous recombination deficiency

questions.question: What type of genetic interaction exists between BRCA and PARP, as described in the guideline?

options.text: Synthetic lethal effect, where simultaneous functional loss of both genes leads to cell death
Complementary effect, where one gene compensates for loss of the other
Additive effect, where both gene defects together increase cancer aggressiveness
Epistatic effect, where one gene masks the phenotype of the other

questions.explanation: The guideline explicitly states that the interaction between BRCA and PARP is a form of synthetic lethal effect, meaning simultaneous functional loss of both genes leads to cell death, while a defect in any single gene only has limited effect on cell viability.
questions.fact: The interaction between BRCA and PARP is a form of synthetic lethal effect, which means the simultaneously functional loss of two genes leads to cell death, while a defect in any single gene only has a limited effect on cell viability

questions.question: BRCA mutations in prostate cancer have which of the following effects according to the guideline?

options.text: They predispose patients to PCa development and make tumours more responsive to PARPi
They only predispose to PCa development without affecting treatment sensitivity
They cause resistance to all forms of systemic therapy including PARPi
They only affect treatment response without increasing cancer risk

questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that BRCA mutations predispose patients to the development of PCa whilst also making tumours more responsive to PARPi. Both effects are directly mentioned in the guideline.
questions.fact: BRCA mutations predispose patients to the development of PCa whilst also making tumours more responsive to PARPi

questions.question: According to the guideline, in which patient population should BRCA status be assessed for optimal treatment selection?

options.text: All patients with any stage of prostate cancer

questions.explanation: The guideline explicitly states that BRCA status should be assessed in patients with metastatic PCa for optimal treatment selection. This is the clinical recommendation derived from the biological rationale discussed in the section.
questions.fact: BRCA status should be assessed in patients with metastatic PCa for optimal treatment selection

questions.question: Which checkpoint inhibitor has been approved by the FDA for advanced prostate cancer patients with MSI-H/dMMR?
questions.explanation: Section 6.5.3.c states that for advanced PCa patients who are MSI-H/dMMR, the PD-1 inhibitor pembrolizumab has been approved by the FDA. The other listed inhibitors are not mentioned as being approved for this indication.
questions.fact: For advanced PCa patients who are MSI-H/dMMR, the PD-1 inhibitor pembrolizumab has been approved by the FDA but not by the EMA.

questions.question: Which regulatory authority has NOT approved pembrolizumab for MSI-H/dMMR advanced prostate cancer?
questions.explanation: Section 6.5.3.c explicitly states that pembrolizumab has been approved by the FDA but not by the EMA for advanced PCa patients with MSI-H/dMMR, making the EMA the correct answer.
questions.fact: The PD-1 inhibitor pembrolizumab has been approved by the FDA but not by the EMA.

questions.question: Checkpoint inhibitors target which of the following molecules?
questions.explanation: Section 6.5.3.c clearly states that checkpoint inhibitors target the molecules CTLA-4, programmed cell death protein 1 (PD-1), and programmed death-ligand 1 (PD-L1). VEGF, HER2, LAG-3, and TIM-3 are not mentioned as targets.
questions.fact: Checkpoint inhibitors target the molecules CTLA-4, programmed cell death protein 1 (PD-1), and programmed death-ligand 1 (PD-L1).

questions.question: The FDA approval of pembrolizumab for MSI-H/dMMR advanced prostate cancer is based on what type of label?
questions.explanation: Section 6.5.3.c explicitly states that the label for pembrolizumab in MSI-H/dMMR advanced PCa is tumour agnostic, meaning it is approved based on the biomarker rather than the specific tumour type.
questions.fact: The label is tumour agnostic.

questions.question: According to the current guideline section on molecular testing, which imaging modalities have most prospective data relied upon for the definition of M1 disease?

options.text: CT scan or MRI and bone scintigraphy

questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that 'most prospective data available rely on the definition of M1 disease based on CT scan or MRI and bone scintigraphy.' Other imaging modalities such as PET-CT, PSMA PET, or transrectal ultrasound are not mentioned as sources for the prospective data.
questions.fact: Most prospective data available rely on the definition of M1 disease based on CT scan or MRI and bone scintigraphy

questions.question: What does the guideline state regarding the influence of modern, more accurate imaging on treatment and patient outcomes in metastatic prostate cancer?

options.text: It has been validated in prospective RCTs and improves outcomes
It has been validated in prospective RCTs but showed no significant difference
It has been validated in retrospective studies only
Its influence has not yet been validated in prospective RCTs

questions.explanation: The text specifically states that 'its influence on treatment and patient outcomes has not yet been validated in prospective RCTs.' This directly indicates the lack of prospective RCT validation for modern imaging despite its increasing clinical use.
questions.fact: Modern, more accurate imaging is increasingly being applied in clinical practice; however, its influence on treatment and patient outcomes has not yet been validated in prospective RCTs

questions.question: How are AKT inhibitors administered?
questions.explanation: The guideline states that 'AKT inhibitors are orally administered ATP-competitive molecules,' making oral administration the correct route of delivery.
questions.fact: AKT inhibitors are orally administered ATP-competitive molecules that target the three AKT isoforms.

questions.question: Which cellular functions does the PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway promote according to the guideline?

options.text: Cell survival, proliferation, metabolism, and growth
Cellular differentiation, embryonic development, and hormone production
Immune activation, antigen presentation, and cytokine release
DNA replication, transcription, and protein synthesis

questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that 'AKT propagates signals from the PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway which is central for promoting cell survival, proliferation, metabolism, and growth.'
questions.fact: AKT propagates signals from the PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway which is central for promoting cell survival, proliferation, metabolism, and growth.

questions.question: What is the molecular mechanism by which AKT inhibitors work on cancer cells?

options.text: Locking AKT into an inactive state and preventing downstream phosphorylation
Directly inducing cancer cell apoptosis through cytochrome c release
Blocking androgen receptor signaling at the nuclear level
Inhibiting DNA synthesis by interfering with nucleotide incorporation

questions.explanation: The guideline clearly states that 'AKT inhibitors interrupt key growth and survival signals in cancer cells by locking AKT into an inactive state and preventing downstream phosphorylation.'
questions.fact: AKT inhibitors interrupt key growth and survival signals in cancer cells by locking AKT into an inactive state and preventing downstream phosphorylation.

questions.question: How many AKT isoforms do AKT inhibitors target?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that 'AKT inhibitors are orally administered ATP-competitive molecules that target the three AKT isoforms.'
questions.fact: AKT inhibitors are orally administered ATP-competitive molecules that target the three AKT isoforms.

questions.question: In terms of which outcomes is older generation non-steroidal anti-androgen (NSAA) monotherapy inferior to ADT?

options.text: Overall survival, clinical progression, treatment failure, and treatment discontinuation due to adverse events
Overall survival and PSA response rates only
Clinical progression and quality of life scores only
Treatment failure and biochemical recurrence rates only

questions.explanation: Section 6.6.4.b explicitly states that older generation NSAA monotherapy is inferior to ADT in terms of overall survival, clinical progression, treatment failure, and treatment discontinuation due to adverse events.
questions.section_title: 6.6.4.b. Intermittent versus continuous androgen deprivation therapy
questions.fact: Older generation non-steroidal anti-androgen (NSAA) monotherapy is inferior to ADT (either medical or surgical) in terms of OS, clinical progression, treatment failure and treatment discontinuation due to AEs [1227] and is generally not recommended.

questions.question: Which of the following is the recommended treatment approach for combination treatment of metastatic hormone-sensitive prostate cancer (mHSPC)?

options.text: Non-steroidal anti-androgen monotherapy combined with radiotherapy

questions.explanation: Section 6.6.4.b clearly states that for combination treatment of mHSPC, ADT is the standard of care, making older generation NSAA monotherapy generally not recommended.
questions.section_title: 6.6.4.b. Intermittent versus continuous androgen deprivation therapy
questions.fact: For combination treatment of mHSPC ADT is the standard of care.

questions.question: What is the status of older generation non-steroidal anti-androgen (NSAA) monotherapy in prostate cancer management?

options.text: It is the preferred first-line treatment for all prostate cancer stages
It is an acceptable alternative to ADT with similar efficacy
It is recommended only for patients with contraindications to ADT

questions.explanation: Section 6.6.4.b explicitly states that older generation NSAA monotherapy is generally not recommended due to its inferiority compared to ADT in multiple outcomes.
questions.section_title: 6.6.4.b. Intermittent versus continuous androgen deprivation therapy
questions.fact: Older generation non-steroidal anti-androgen (NSAA) monotherapy is inferior to ADT (either medical or surgical) in terms of OS, clinical progression, treatment failure and treatment discontinuation due to AEs [1227] and is generally not recommended.

questions.question: What proportion of M1b patients can be expected to be candidates for intermittent androgen deprivation therapy according to the SWOG 9346 trial?
questions.explanation: Section 6.6.4.c states that of 3,040 screened patients in SWOG 9346, only 1,535 met inclusion criteria, highlighting that only about 50% of M1b patients can be expected to be candidates for IAD, i.e., the best PSA responders.
questions.section_title: 6.6.4.c. Early versus deferred androgen deprivation therapy
questions.fact: Only about 50% of M1b patients can be expected to be candidates for IAD

questions.question: How many of the eight RCTs included in the reviews of intermittent androgen deprivation therapy were conducted in patients with exclusively M1 disease?
questions.explanation: Section 6.6.4.c states that all reviews included eight RCTs, of which only three were conducted in patients with exclusively M1 disease.
questions.section_title: 6.6.4.c. Early versus deferred androgen deprivation therapy
questions.fact: Only three of the eight RCTs were conducted in patients with exclusively M1 disease

questions.question: What was the outcome of the SWOG 9346 noninferiority trial regarding intermittent androgen deprivation in M1b patients?

options.text: Inconclusive results - the prespecified noninferiority limit was not achieved
Clear evidence of superiority for intermittent therapy
Clear evidence of inferiority for intermittent therapy
Proven noninferiority with statistically significant results

questions.explanation: Section 6.6.4.c states that SWOG 9346 was a noninferiority trial leading to inconclusive results: the actual upper limit was above the prespecified 90% upper limit of 1.2, and the prespecified noninferiority limit was not achieved.
questions.section_title: 6.6.4.c. Early versus deferred androgen deprivation therapy
questions.fact: The prespecified noninferiority limit was not achieved

questions.question: Why has intermittent androgen deprivation therapy been superseded as a treatment approach?

options.text: Continuous ADT-based combination therapy has become standard of care
It was found to be inferior in all patient subgroups
It caused unacceptable side effects in clinical trials
Regulatory authorities revoked approval for its use

questions.explanation: Section 6.6.4.c states that the use of intermittent ADT has been superseded as continuous ADT-based combination therapy has become standard of care.
questions.section_title: 6.6.4.c. Early versus deferred androgen deprivation therapy
questions.fact: Continuous ADT-based combination therapy has become SOC

questions.question: For how long has primary androgen deprivation therapy (ADT) been the standard of care in prostate cancer treatment?
questions.explanation: The guideline explicitly states that 'Primary ADT has been the standard of care for over 50 years' (Section 6.6.4.a), establishing the historical context for this treatment approach.
questions.fact: Primary ADT has been the standard of care for over 50 years

questions.question: Which types of androgen deprivation therapy reduce testosterone levels the fastest?
questions.explanation: According to Section 6.6.4.a, 'The level of testosterone is reduced much faster with orchiectomy and LHRH antagonist.' This rapid testosterone reduction is clinically significant for certain patient populations.
questions.fact: The level of testosterone is reduced much faster with orchiectomy and LHRH antagonist

questions.question: In patients with impending spinal cord compression, which androgen deprivation therapy options are specifically recommended?
questions.explanation: Section 6.6.4.a states that due to the faster testosterone reduction, 'they [orchiectomy and LHRH antagonists] are recommended in patients with impending spinal cord compression or other potential impending complications.'
questions.fact: Therefore, they [orchiectomy and LHRH antagonists] are recommended in patients with impending spinal cord compression or other potential impending complications

questions.question: In patients with pre-existing cardiovascular disease, which androgen deprivation therapy is preferred according to the guideline?
questions.explanation: Section 6.6.4.a indicates that 'cardiovascular side effects may be less frequent in patients treated with LHRH antagonists than patients treated with LHRH agonists,' and therefore 'LHRH antagonists may be preferred in patients with pre-existing cardiovascular disease or other cardiovascular risk factors.'
questions.fact: Cardiovascular side effects may be less frequent in patients treated with LHRH antagonists than patients treated with LHRH agonists. Therefore, LHRH antagonists may be preferred in patients with pre-existing cardiovascular disease or other cardiovascular risk factors

questions.question: What level of evidence is available regarding which specific type of ADT is superior for oncological outcomes?

options.text: No high-level evidence favors any specific type

questions.explanation: Section 6.6.4.a explicitly states that 'No high-level evidence is available in favour of a specific type of ADT for oncological outcomes - neither for orchiectomy, nor for a LHRH agonist or antagonist.'
questions.fact: No high-level evidence is available in favour of a specific type of ADT for oncological outcomes - neither for orchiectomy, nor for a LHRH agonist or antagonist

questions.question: According to the SWOG 9346 study, what is the median survival for mHSPC patients with PSA level >4 ng/mL after 7 months of ADT monotherapy?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states in Table 6.6.2 that patients with PSA >4 ng/mL after 7 months of ADT have a median survival of 13 months, which is the shortest among the three prognostic groups defined in the SWOG 9346 cohort.
questions.fact: Table 6.6.2 shows that PSA >4 ng/mL after 7 months of ADT is associated with median survival of 13 months on ADT monotherapy.

questions.question: According to the CHAARTED trial, which of the following criteria defines HIGH volume metastatic disease?

options.text: ≥4 bone metastases including ≥1 outside vertebral column or pelvis AND/OR visceral metastasis
>2 bone metastases with ISUP Grade Group ≥3
Presence of lymph node metastases only

questions.explanation: Table 6.6.1 in section 6.6.4 explicitly defines high volume in CHAARTED as ≥4 bone metastases including ≥1 outside the vertebral column or pelvis AND/OR visceral metastasis, with lymph nodes not considered visceral metastases.
questions.fact: CHAARTED defines high volume as ≥4 bone metastases including ≥1 outside vertebral column or pelvis AND/OR visceral metastasis.

questions.question: What is the median survival for patients with newly diagnosed synchronous mHSPC treated with ADT alone?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that median survival of patients with newly diagnosed metastases (synchronous mHSPC) is approximately 50 months with ADT alone, though this is highly variable since the M1 population is heterogeneous.
questions.fact: Median survival of patients with newly diagnosed metastases (synchronous mHSPC) is approximately 50 months with ADT alone.

questions.question: According to the text, which type of metastatic disease has been shown to have a generally better prognosis?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that 'metachronous' metastatic disease (after radical local treatment of the primary tumour) versus synchronous (or de novo) metastatic disease has been shown to have generally a better prognosis.
questions.fact: Metachronous metastatic disease (after radical local treatment of the primary tumour) vs synchronous metastatic disease has been shown to have generally a better prognosis.

questions.question: According to the LATITUDE study, what PSA level after six months of treatment was shown to be correlated with long-term outcomes?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that reaching PSA levels of ≤0.1 ng/mL after six months was shown to be correlated with long-term outcomes in the LATITUDE study, distinguishing it from the PSA ≤0.2 ng/mL threshold used in other studies.
questions.fact: Reaching PSA levels of ≤0.1 ng/mL after six months were shown to be correlated with long-term outcomes in the LATITUDE study.

questions.question: What type of ADT have combination therapies with older generation NSAA (bicalutamide, flutamide, nilutamide) been studied with?
questions.explanation: Section 6.6.5.a explicitly states that all of these combination therapies have been studied with continuous ADT, not intermittent ADT.
questions.section_title: 6.6.5.a.‘Combined’ androgen blockade with older generation NSAA (bicalutamide, flutamide, nilutamide)
questions.fact: All of these combination therapies have been studied with continuous ADT, not intermittent ADT.

questions.question: According to the 2019 Cochrane analysis, what does early ADT probably extend in patients with metastatic hormone-sensitive disease?

options.text: Time to death from any cause and time to death from PCa
Quality of life scores and symptom-free survival

questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that the 2019 Cochrane analysis concluded early ADT probably extends time to death of any cause and time to death from PCa. This is stated in Section 6.6.5.
questions.fact: The 2019 Cochrane analysis concluded that early ADT probably extends time to death of any cause and time to death from PCa [1234].

questions.question: What limitation of the 2019 Cochrane analysis on early ADT is highlighted in the guideline?

options.text: It only included a very limited number of metastatic patients
It only evaluated patients with castration-resistant disease
It excluded studies using long-term ADT protocols
It primarily reviewed retrospective cohort studies

questions.explanation: Section 6.6.5 explicitly states that the analysis included only a very limited number of metastatic patients, which is a key limitation noted in the guideline.
questions.fact: The analysis included only a very limited number of metastatic patients.

questions.question: For men with oligometastatic prostate cancer, which imaging techniques may be used when delaying ADT to offer RT/SABR?

options.text: Multi-parametric MRI of the prostate or choline PET

questions.explanation: Section 6.6.5 specifically mentions that highly sensitive imaging techniques like PSMA-PET/CT or whole body MRI are used when delaying ADT for men with oligometastatic PCa to offer RT/SABR.
questions.fact: Delaying ADT, often using highly sensitive imaging techniques like PSMA-PET/CT or whole body MRI, in order to offer RT/SABR for men with oligometastatic PCa is the focus of multiple studies.

questions.question: What is the approximate survival advantage of combined androgen blockade using a NSAA compared to monotherapy with surgical castration or LHRH agonists?
questions.explanation: The guideline states that combined androgen blockade using a NSAA provides a small survival advantage (<5%) versus monotherapy with surgical castration or LHRH agonists.
questions.section_title: 6.6.5.b. Androgen deprivation combined with other agents
questions.fact: Combined androgen blockade using a NSAA provides a small survival advantage (<5%) versus monotherapy

questions.question: Which of the following is mentioned as monotherapy options against which combined androgen blockade with a NSAA was compared?

options.text: Radical prostatectomy or external beam radiotherapy

questions.explanation: The guideline specifically states that combined androgen blockade was compared against monotherapy consisting of surgical castration or LHRH agonists.
questions.section_title: 6.6.5.b. Androgen deprivation combined with other agents
questions.fact: Monotherapy comparators are surgical castration or LHRH agonists

questions.question: Which newer agent was specifically mentioned in the guideline as being tested against a NSAA in phase III trials and showing more effective combination therapy?
questions.explanation: The guideline specifically mentions enzalutamide as the newer agent tested against NSAA in phase III trials which demonstrated more effective combination therapy compared to the small survival advantage of traditional combined androgen blockade.
questions.section_title: 6.6.5.b. Androgen deprivation combined with other agents
questions.fact: Enzalutamide was tested against NSAA in phase III trials

questions.question: What is the minimum PSA level required for biochemical progression in castration-resistant prostate cancer (CRPC) diagnosis?
questions.explanation: According to Section 6.7.2, biochemical progression requires three consecutive rises in PSA at least one week apart resulting in two 50% increases over the nadir, AND a PSA > 2 ng/mL.
questions.fact: Biochemical progression requires PSA > 2 ng/mL

questions.question: Which of the following is TRUE regarding symptomatic progression in the diagnosis of CRPC?

options.text: It must be questioned and is not sufficient alone to diagnose CRPC
It is sufficient alone if PSA is elevated
It must be present along with PSA progression
It requires documentation of performance status decline

questions.explanation: Section 6.7.2 explicitly states that symptomatic progression alone must be questioned and subject to further investigation. It is not sufficient to diagnose CRPC.
questions.fact: Symptomatic progression alone is not sufficient to diagnose CRPC

questions.question: According to the EAU guidelines, what constitutes radiological progression for diagnosing CRPC?

options.text: Two or more new bone lesions on bone scan, or a soft tissue lesion using RECIST
One new bone lesion confirmed on CT scan
An increase in size of existing bone lesions
Any soft tissue lesion visible on imaging

questions.explanation: Section 6.7.2 specifies that radiological progression includes either two or more new bone lesions on bone scan (ideally confirmed), or a soft tissue lesion using RECIST criteria.
questions.fact: Radiological progression requires two or more new bone lesions on bone scan, or a soft tissue lesion using RECIST

questions.question: What serum testosterone threshold defines adequate castration for CRPC diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Section 6.7.2 states that castration resistance requires serum testosterone to be < 50 ng/dL or 1.7 nmol/L.
questions.fact: Castrate serum testosterone < 50 ng/dL or 1.7 nmol/L

questions.question: Which of the following is explicitly listed as a factor influencing treatment selection for metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC)?

options.text: Previous treatment for non-metastatic HSPC (mHSPC) only
Previous treatment for mHSPC and for non-mHSPC
Only the patient's age at diagnosis

questions.explanation: Section 6.7.2.a explicitly states that treatment selection depends on 'previous treatment for mHSPC and for non-mHSPC', indicating that both metastatic and non-metastatic hormone-sensitive disease history matters.
questions.fact: Selection of treatment for mCRPC depends on previous treatment for mHSPC and for non-mHSPC

questions.question: According to the guidelines, which molecular alteration specifically prompts consideration of platinum or targeted therapy such as PARP inhibitors (PARPi)?

options.text: Known histological variants and DNA repair deficiency

questions.explanation: Section 6.7.2.a explicitly states that known histological variants and DNA repair deficiency should be considered to guide the use of platinum or targeted therapy like PARPi, highlighting the importance of molecular profiling.
questions.fact: Known histological variants and DNA repair deficiency (to consider platinum or targeted therapy like PARPi)

questions.question: Which two molecular biomarkers are explicitly mentioned together as influencing mCRPC treatment selection in the context of immunotherapy?
questions.explanation: Section 6.7.2.a specifically states that known genetic alterations and 'microsatellite instability–high (MSI-H)/mismatch repair-deficient (dMMR) status' influence treatment selection, which is particularly relevant for immunotherapy decisions.
questions.fact: Known genetic alterations and microsatellite instability–high (MSI-H)/mismatch repair-deficient (dMMR) status

questions.question: When selecting treatment for mCRPC, which of the following is NOT explicitly listed as a consideration in the guideline?

options.text: Known cross resistance between androgen receptor pathway inhibitors (ARPI)
Patient's family history of prostate cancer
Quality of response and pace of progression on previous treatment

questions.explanation: Section 6.7.2.a lists multiple factors for mCRPC treatment selection, including cross resistance between ARPIs, drug interactions, and response to previous treatment. Patient's family history is not explicitly mentioned as a selection criterion.
questions.fact: Selection of treatment for mCRPC is multifactorial and depends on multiple factors including previous treatments, molecular markers, and patient-specific considerations

questions.question: Which of the following agents has been approved for the treatment of non-metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (nmCRPC) in Europe?
questions.explanation: According to the guideline, apalutamide, darolutamide, and enzalutamide are the agents approved for nmCRPC, while docetaxel, cabazitaxel, and radium-223 are approved for metastatic CRPC (mCRPC) only.
questions.fact: For nmCRPC, apalutamide, darolutamide and enzalutamide have been approved.

questions.question: According to the guideline, when is sequencing of androgen receptor pathway inhibitors (ARPIs) specifically NOT recommended?

options.text: When patients have low PSA doubling time
When response to ADT and first ARPI was ≤6-12 months with high-risk features
When patients have bone metastases only

questions.explanation: The guideline states that sequencing of ARPIs like abiraterone and enzalutamide is not recommended, particularly if the time of response to ADT and to the first ARPI was short (≤ six to twelve months) and high-risk features of rapid progression are present.
questions.fact: Sequencing of ARPIs like abiraterone and enzalutamide is not recommended particularly if the time of response to ADT and to the first ARPI was short (≤ six to twelve months) and high-risk features of rapid progression are present.

questions.question: In patients with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer and a known BRCA alteration, which treatment approach should be prioritized according to the guideline?
questions.explanation: The guideline specifically states that in case of a known BRCA alteration, the use of a PARP inhibitor should always be prioritised because these patients harbour an adverse prognosis and don't respond well to most other treatments. PARP inhibitor use significantly improves rPFS and OS in these patients.
questions.fact: In case of a known BRCA alteration, the use of a PARP inhibitor should always be prioritised as these patients harbour an adverse prognosis and don't respond well to most other treatments.

questions.question: According to section 6.7.5.a, what does the remainder of this section focus on?

options.text: Management of men with proven mCRPC on conventional imaging
Comparison of conventional versus novel imaging modalities
Prevention strategies for castration resistance development

questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that 'The remainder of this section focuses on the management of men with proven mCRPC on conventional imaging', indicating the specific patient population and imaging modality covered.
questions.fact: The remainder of this section focuses on the management of men with proven mCRPC on conventional imaging.

questions.question: According to the guideline, approximately what percentage of non-metastatic men treated with ADT who develop biochemical progression will develop bone metastases within two years?
questions.explanation: Section 6.7.5 states that 'approximately one-third will develop bone metastases within two years, detected by conventional imaging'. This reflects the natural history of nmCRPC progression.
questions.fact: Approximately one-third of non-metastatic men treated with ADT who develop biochemical progression will develop bone metastases within two years.

questions.question: According to the RADAR consensus statement, at what PSA threshold should a bone scan and CT scan first be performed in asymptomatic men on ADT?
questions.explanation: Section 6.7.5 cites the RADAR consensus statement that suggested 'a bone scan and a CT scan when the PSA reached 2ng/mL'. If negative, the scan should be repeated at 5ng/mL and after every PSA doubling.
questions.fact: The RADAR consensus statement suggested a bone scan and CT scan when PSA reaches 2ng/mL in asymptomatic men.

questions.question: Which three phase III RCTs evaluated metastasis-free survival as the primary endpoint in patients with nmCRPC (M0 CRPC)?
questions.explanation: Section 6.7.5 explicitly states that 'Three large phase III RCTs, PROSPER, SPARTAN and ARAMIS, evaluated MFS as the primary endpoint in patients with nmCRPC (M0 CRPC)' with enzalutamide, apalutamide, and darolutamide respectively.
questions.fact: PROSPER, SPARTAN, and ARAMIS are the three phase III RCTs that evaluated MFS as primary endpoint in nmCRPC.

questions.question: What was the maximum PSA doubling time (PSA-DT) required for patients to be included in the nmCRPC phase III trials (PROSPER, SPARTAN, ARAMIS)?
questions.explanation: Section 6.7.5 states that 'Only patients at high risk for the development of metastasis with a short PSA-DT of ≤ 10 months were included.' The text also notes that about two-thirds had PSA-DT <6 months.
