questions.fact: Only patients with PSA-DT ≤ 10 months were included in the nmCRPC phase III trials.

questions.question: Which factor has NOT been associated with time to first bone metastasis, bone MFS, and OS in men with CRPC and no detectable clinical metastases?
questions.explanation: Section 6.7.5 states that 'baseline PSA level, PSA velocity and PSA-DT have been associated with time to first bone metastasis, bone MFS and OS'. Gleason score is not mentioned as a factor in this specific context.
questions.fact: Baseline PSA level, PSA velocity, and PSA-DT are the factors associated with metastasis outcomes in non-metastatic CRPC.

questions.question: What happens to men with prostate cancer despite castration?

options.text: They eventually show evidence of disease progression
They are permanently cured of the disease
They show minimal symptoms that resolve quickly

questions.explanation: Section 6.7.6 states that 'men with PCa will show evidence of disease progression despite castration,' establishing that CRPC inevitably develops.
questions.fact: Eventually men with PCa will show evidence of disease progression despite castration.

questions.question: What did two trials demonstrate about patients remaining on LHRH analogues during second- and third-line therapies?

options.text: Complete remission in the majority of patients
Improved quality of life without survival benefit

questions.explanation: Section 6.7.6 explicitly states that 'Two trials have shown only a marginal survival benefit for patients remaining on LHRH analogues during second- and third-line therapies.'
questions.fact: Two trials have shown only a marginal survival benefit for patients remaining on LHRH analogues during second- and third-line therapies.

questions.question: Why should androgen suppression be continued in patients with castration-resistant prostate cancer?

options.text: All subsequent treatments have been studied in men with ongoing androgen suppression
It prevents the development of bone metastases
It significantly improves PSA response rates
It reduces tumor burden more effectively than other agents

questions.explanation: Section 6.7.6 states that 'all subsequent treatments have been studied in men with ongoing androgen suppression, therefore, it should be continued in these patients.' This is the rationale for continuing treatment.
questions.fact: All subsequent treatments have been studied in men with ongoing androgen suppression, therefore, it should be continued in these patients.

questions.question: Which patient population was excluded from the COU-AA-302 trial evaluating abiraterone acetate in chemo-naive mCRPC patients?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that patients with visceral metastases were excluded from the phase III COU-AA-302 trial. This exclusion criterion is clearly stated in the section describing the trial design and patient eligibility.
questions.fact: Patients with visceral metastases were excluded from the trial.

questions.question: What were the co-primary endpoints in the COU-AA-302 trial?

options.text: Radiographic progression-free survival and PSA response rate
Overall survival and radiographic progression-free survival
Overall survival and time to opioid use
PSA progression-free survival and pain response

questions.explanation: The guideline text explicitly states that 'Overall survival and rPFS were the co-primary endpoints' of the COU-AA-302 phase III trial. This is a direct factual recall from the section.
questions.fact: Overall survival and rPFS were the co-primary endpoints.

questions.question: According to the COU-AA-302 trial subset analysis, how did abiraterone acetate perform in patients over 75 years of age?

options.text: It showed reduced efficacy compared to younger patients
It was equally effective as in the general study population
It showed improved tolerability but reduced efficacy
Subgroup analysis was not performed for elderly patients

questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that 'Subset analysis of this trial showed the drug to be equally effective in an elderly population (>75 years).' This direct statement from the section indicates comparable efficacy across age groups.
questions.fact: Subset analysis showed the drug to be equally effective in an elderly population (>75 years).

questions.question: What is the inevitable outcome for all patients who receive treatment for metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC)?
questions.explanation: Section 6.7.7.a explicitly states that 'All patients who receive treatment for mCRPC will eventually progress,' indicating that disease progression is inevitable regardless of treatment choice.
questions.fact: All patients who receive treatment for mCRPC will eventually progress.

questions.question: According to the guideline, what factor determines the sequence of treatments for mCRPC?
questions.explanation: The guideline clearly states that 'Treatment sequences will depend on which agents were used previously,' emphasizing the importance of prior treatment history in selecting subsequent therapy.
questions.fact: Treatment sequences will depend on which agents were used previously.

questions.question: What limitation exists regarding high-level evidence for treatment sequencing in mCRPC?

options.text: There is extensive high-level data available for all sequences
There is a paucity of high-level data regarding sequence of treatments
Data is only lacking for treatment-naive patients
High-level data exists only for first-line treatments

questions.explanation: The guideline explicitly mentions 'There is a paucity of high-level data with regards to the sequence of treatments,' particularly noting this issue in patients pretreated for mHSPC with ARPI and/or docetaxel.
questions.fact: There is a paucity of high-level data with regards to the sequence of treatments, particularly in case of pretreatment for mHSPC with ARPI and/or docetaxel or other agents.

questions.question: In the TROPIC phase III trial, what was the primary endpoint that showed a significant improvement with cabazitaxel compared to mitoxantrone in patients with mCRPC?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that 'Overall survival was the primary endpoint which was significantly longer with cabazitaxel (median: 15.1 vs. 12.7 months).' While improvements in PFS, RECIST response, and PSA response were also noted, these were secondary endpoints.
questions.section_title: 6.7.7.b. Abiraterone acetate after docetaxel for mCRPC
questions.fact: Overall survival was the primary endpoint which was significantly longer with cabazitaxel (median: 15.1 vs. 12.7 months, p < 0.0001)

questions.question: According to post-marketing randomised phase III trials, what dose of cabazitaxel is preferred in the second-line setting?
questions.explanation: The text states that 'in the second-line setting in terms of OS, 20 mg/m² cabazitaxel was not inferior to 25 mg/m², but less toxic. Therefore, the lower dose should be preferred.' This clearly indicates 20 mg/m² is the preferred dose.
questions.section_title: 6.7.7.b. Abiraterone acetate after docetaxel for mCRPC
questions.fact: In the second-line setting in terms of OS, 20 mg/m² cabazitaxel was not inferior to 25 mg/m², but less toxic. Therefore, the lower dose should be preferred.

questions.question: What supportive measure should preferably be given when administering cabazitaxel?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that 'Cabazitaxel should preferably be given with prophylactic granulocyte colony-stimulating factor (G-CSF).' This is recommended due to the high rate of haematological toxicity observed in the TROPIC trial (68.2% grade 3-4 haematological adverse events).
questions.section_title: 6.7.7.b. Abiraterone acetate after docetaxel for mCRPC
questions.fact: Cabazitaxel should preferably be given with prophylactic granulocyte colony-stimulating factor (G-CSF)

questions.question: According to the EAU guidelines, what is the standard first-line chemotherapy regimen for metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC)?

options.text: Docetaxel 75 mg/m² three-weekly plus prednisone 5 mg BID up to ten cycles
Docetaxel 50 mg/m² two-weekly plus prednisone 10 mg BID up to six cycles
Mitoxantrone 12 mg/m² three-weekly plus prednisone 5 mg BID
Cabazitaxel 25 mg/m² three-weekly plus prednisone 5 mg BID

questions.explanation: The guideline explicitly states that docetaxel 75 mg/m² given in three-weekly doses combined with prednisone 5 mg twice daily (BID) up to ten cycles is the standard first-line chemotherapy. The alternative dose of 50 mg/m² every two weeks is only recommended for patients who cannot tolerate the standard regimen.
questions.fact: The standard first-line chemotherapy is docetaxel 75 mg/m², three-weekly doses combined with prednisone 5 mg twice a day (BID), up to ten cycles.

questions.question: Based on the independent prognostic factors for docetaxel response, what is the median overall survival (OS) estimate for patients classified in the HIGH risk group?
questions.explanation: The guideline clearly states that patients can be categorised into three risk groups: low risk (0 or 1 factor) showing median OS of 25.7 months, intermediate (2 factors) showing 18.7 months, and high risk (3 or 4 factors) showing 12.8 months. The high-risk group corresponds to 12.8 months median OS.
questions.fact: High risk (3 or 4 factors), and show three significantly different median OS estimates of 25.7, 18.7 and 12.8 months, respectively.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT listed as an independent prognostic factor for stratifying response to docetaxel in mCRPC?
questions.explanation: The guideline explicitly lists the following independent prognostic factors: visceral metastases, pain, anaemia (Hb < 13 g/dL), bone scan progression, and prior estramustine. Prior ketoconazole use is not mentioned as a prognostic factor in this section.
questions.fact: The following independent prognostic factors: visceral metastases, pain, anaemia (Hb < 13 g/dL), bone scan progression, and prior estramustine may help stratify the response to docetaxel.

questions.question: What has been demonstrated regarding combination therapies with docetaxel in unselected mCRPC patients?

options.text: Combination with pembrolizumab has shown superior overall survival
No combination has proven superior to docetaxel alone
Combination therapy is now the standard of care
PARP inhibitor combinations significantly improve progression-free survival

questions.explanation: The guideline clearly states that 'no combination with docetaxel has proven to be superior to docetaxel alone in unselected mCRPC patients, including the combination with the checkpoint inhibitor pembrolizumab.' This includes combinations with PARP inhibitors as the section title suggests, none have shown superiority.
questions.fact: So far, no combination with docetaxel has proven to be superior to docetaxel alone in unselected mCRPC patients, including the combination with the checkpoint inhibitor pembrolizumab.

questions.question: What improvement in median survival has been demonstrated with docetaxel compared to mitoxantrone plus prednisone?
questions.explanation: The guideline explicitly states that a statistically significant improvement in median survival of 2.0-2.9 months has been shown with docetaxel compared to mitoxantrone plus prednisone.
questions.fact: A statistically significant improvement in median survival of 2.0–2.9 months has been shown with docetaxel compared to mitoxantrone plus prednisone.

questions.question: What was the planned hazard ratio (HR) in favour of abiraterone acetate plus prednisone (AAP) in the COU-AA-301 trial?
questions.explanation: Section 6.7.7.c. explicitly states that the primary endpoint was overall survival 'with a planned HR of 0.8 in favour of AAP'. The actual HR achieved was 0.74, which was better than the planned threshold.
questions.fact: The primary endpoint was OS, with a planned HR of 0.8 in favour of AAP.

questions.question: According to the final analysis of the COU-AA-301 trial with median follow-up of 20.2 months, what was the median overall survival in the abiraterone acetate plus prednisone group?
questions.explanation: Section 6.7.7.c. clearly states that 'the median survival in the AAP group was 15.8 months compared to 11.2 months in the placebo arm' after median follow-up of 20.2 months.
questions.fact: After a median follow-up of 20.2 months, the median survival in the AAP group was 15.8 months compared to 11.2 months in the placebo arm (HR: 0.74, p < 0.0001).

questions.question: Which adverse events were reported to be more frequent in the abiraterone acetate plus prednisone group compared to placebo in the COU-AA-301 trial?

options.text: Mineralocorticoid-related side effects (fluid retention, oedema, hypokalaemia)

questions.explanation: Section 6.7.7.c. explicitly states that 'mineralocorticoid-related side effects were more frequent in the AAP group, mainly grade 1–2 (fluid retention, oedema and hypokalaemia)', while 'the incidence of the most common grade 3–4 AEs did not differ significantly between arms'.
questions.fact: The incidence of the most common grade 3–4 AEs did not differ significantly between arms, but mineralocorticoid-related side effects were more frequent in the AAP group, mainly grade 1–2 (fluid retention, oedema and hypokalaemia).

questions.question: In the AFFIRM study, what was the hazard ratio for overall survival in favor of enzalutamide compared to placebo?
questions.explanation: The AFFIRM study demonstrated a hazard ratio of 0.63 (p < 0.001) for overall survival favoring enzalutamide over placebo, which was statistically significant. The expected HR benefit mentioned was 0.76, but the actual observed HR was 0.63.
questions.fact: After a median follow-up of 14.4 months, the median survival in the enzalutamide group was 18.4 months compared to 13.6 months in the placebo arm (HR: 0.63, p < 0.001).

questions.question: What proportion of patients in the AFFIRM study received corticosteroids?
questions.explanation: Corticosteroids were not mandatory in the AFFIRM study but were received by approximately 30% of the patients. The study allowed corticosteroid use without requiring it for all participants.
questions.fact: Corticosteroids were not mandatory, but could be prescribed, and were received by about 30% of the patients.

questions.question: What was the median overall survival in the enzalutamide group in the AFFIRM study?
questions.explanation: The AFFIRM study showed that patients receiving enzalutamide had a median overall survival of 18.4 months compared to 13.6 months in the placebo arm, representing a 4.8-month improvement.
questions.fact: After a median follow-up of 14.4 months, the median survival in the enzalutamide group was 18.4 months compared to 13.6 months in the placebo arm.

questions.question: In the AFFIRM study, what was the incidence of seizures in the enzalutamide group?
questions.explanation: There was a 0.6% incidence of seizures in the enzalutamide group compared to none in the placebo arm. This was the only notable difference in adverse events between the two treatment groups.
questions.fact: There was a 0.6% incidence of seizures in the enzalutamide group compared to none in the placebo arm.

questions.question: How were patients randomized in the AFFIRM study?
questions.explanation: The AFFIRM study randomized 1,199 patients with mCRPC in a 2:1 fashion to enzalutamide or placebo, meaning twice as many patients received enzalutamide compared to placebo.
questions.fact: This trial randomised 1,199 patients with mCRPC in a 2:1 fashion to enzalutamide or placebo.

questions.question: What gene alterations were required for patient selection in the TRITON-3 trial?
questions.explanation: Section 6.7.7.f explicitly states that patients were selected for a BRCA1, BRCA2, or ATM alteration in the TRITON-3 trial. The small ATM subgroup did not derive a benefit from rucaparib treatment.
questions.fact: Patients were selected for a BRCA1, BRCA2, or ATM alteration

questions.question: What was the median radiographic progression-free survival (rPFS) in the intention-to-treat group for patients treated with rucaparib in the TRITON-3 trial?
questions.explanation: Section 6.7.7.f reports that the first endpoint rPFS in the intention-to-treat group was significantly better with rucaparib at median 10.2 months compared to 6.4 months in the control group (HR 0.61; p<0.001).
questions.fact: Median rPFS was 10.2 months with rucaparib

questions.question: What percentage of patients on rucaparib in the TRITON-3 trial required at least one blood transfusion?
questions.explanation: Section 6.7.7.f states that the most frequent adverse events with rucaparib were fatigue, nausea and anaemia, including 24% Grade ≥3 anaemia and 29% of patients on rucaparib required at least one blood transfusion.
questions.fact: 29% of patients on rucaparib required at least one blood transfusion

questions.question: In the PSMAfore trial, what was the median radiographic progression-free survival (rPFS) for patients treated with 177Lu-PSMA-617?
questions.explanation: The updated analysis of the PSMAfore trial showed median rPFS of 11.60 months (95% CI: 9.30-14.19) in the 177Lu-PSMA-617 group compared to 5.59 months in the ARPI change group. Section 6.7.8.
questions.section_title: 6.7.8. Treatment after docetaxel and one line of ARPI for mCRPC
questions.fact: median rPFS was 11.60 months (95% CI: 9.30–14.19) in the 177Lu-PSMA-617 group vs. 5.59 months (4.21–5.95) in the ARPI change group

questions.question: What was the hazard ratio for overall survival (OS) comparing 177Lu-PSMA-617 to ARPI change in the final analysis of PSMAfore?
questions.explanation: The final analysis showed median OS of 24.48 months with 177Lu-PSMA-617 versus 23.13 with ARPI change with HR 0.91 (95% CI 0.72-1.14; p=0.20), which was not statistically significant. Section 6.7.8.
questions.section_title: 6.7.8. Treatment after docetaxel and one line of ARPI for mCRPC
questions.fact: The median OS was 24.48 months with 177Lu-PSMA-617 versus 23.13 with ARPI change (HR 0.91; 95% CI 0.72–1.14; p = 0.20)

questions.question: What percentage of patients experienced dry mouth in the 177Lu-PSMA-617 arm of the PSMAfore trial?
questions.explanation: The text states dry mouth occurred in 135/227 participants (59.5%; 2/227 grade ≥3) in the 177Lu-PSMA-617 arm. Section 6.7.8.
questions.section_title: 6.7.8. Treatment after docetaxel and one line of ARPI for mCRPC
questions.fact: Dry mouth occurred in 135/227 participants (59.5%; 2/227 grade ≥ 3) in the 177Lu-PSMA-617 arm

questions.question: In the PSMAfore trial, what was the exposure-adjusted incidence of grade ≥3 treatment-emergent adverse events per 100 patient-treatment years for 177Lu-PSMA-617 versus ARPI change?
questions.explanation: The text states exposure-adjusted incidences of grade ≥3 treatment-emergent adverse events were 60.8 versus 85.1 per 100 patient-treatment years for 177Lu-PSMA-617 versus ARPI change, respectively. Section 6.7.8.
questions.section_title: 6.7.8. Treatment after docetaxel and one line of ARPI for mCRPC
questions.fact: exposure-adjusted incidences of grade ≥ 3 and serious treatment-emergent adverse events were 60.8 versus 85.1 and 32.5 versus 49.9 per 100 patient-treatment years, respectively

questions.question: In which year was the first patient treated with 177Lu-PSMA-617?
questions.explanation: Section 6.7.8.b.2 states that 'The first patient was treated in 2014' with 177Lu-PSMA-617, and early clinical studies evaluating safety and efficacy demonstrated promising results.
questions.fact: The first patient was treated in 2014

questions.question: In the TheraP trial, what was the primary endpoint?

options.text: Reduction of at least 50% in PSA

questions.explanation: Section 6.7.8.b.2 explicitly states that 'The primary endpoint was a reduction of at least 50% in PSA', which was met with 66% vs 37% for 177Lu-PSMA-617 vs cabazitaxel respectively.
questions.fact: The primary endpoint was a reduction of at least 50% in PSA

questions.question: What was the median overall survival in the TheraP trial for 177Lu-PSMA-617 compared to cabazitaxel?
questions.explanation: Section 6.7.8.b.2 reports that 'Overall survival was similar between randomly assigned patients in the two groups (19.1 vs. 19.6 months; difference -0.5, 95% CI: -3.7 to 2.7; p = 0.77)'.
questions.fact: Overall survival was 19.1 vs 19.6 months in the TheraP trial

questions.question: For men progressing quickly on AR targeted therapy (less than twelve months), which treatment is supported by the best data according to the CARD trial?
questions.explanation: Section 6.7.8.b states that for men progressing quickly on AR targeted therapy (<12 months), 'cabazitaxel is the treatment supported by the best data.' The CARD trial evaluated cabazitaxel after docetaxel and one line of ARPI, demonstrating superior outcomes.
questions.fact: For men progressing quickly on AR targeted therapy (<12 months), cabazitaxel is the treatment supported by the best data.

questions.question: What percentage reduction in risk of death was observed with cabazitaxel compared to ARPI in the CARD trial?
questions.explanation: Section 6.7.8.b explicitly states that cabazitaxel 'reduced the risk of death by 36% vs. ARPI' in the CARD trial, which was an open-label randomised phase III trial.
questions.fact: Cabazitaxel reduced the risk of death by 36% vs. ARPI.

questions.question: What response rate was observed in men with homozygous deletions or deleterious mutations in DNA-repair genes treated with olaparib?
questions.explanation: Section 6.7.8.b states that men with homozygous deletions or deleterious mutations in DNA-repair genes 'showed an 88% response rate to olaparib' in clinical trials.
questions.fact: Men previously treated with at least one ARPI or both an ARPI and docetaxel and whose tumours demonstrated homozygous deletions or deleterious mutations in DNA-repair genes showed an 88% response rate to olaparib.

questions.question: In a randomised phase I/II trial, the combination of cabazitaxel and carboplatin improved median PFS from 4.5 months to how many months in pretreated mCRPC patients?
questions.explanation: According to Section 6.7.9, the randomised phase I/II trial evaluating cabazitaxel and carboplatin in pretreated mCRPC patients showed improved median PFS from 4.5 months to 7.3 months (95% CI: 5.5-8.2; HR: 0.69, p=0.018).
questions.section_title: 6.7.9. Treatment emergent neuroendocrine PCa and neuroendocrine subtype
questions.fact: The combination improved the median PFS from 4.5 months (95% CI: 3.5-5.7) to 7.3 months (95% CI: 5.5-8.2; HR: 0.69, 95% CI: 0.50-0.95, p = 0.018)

questions.question: In a meta-analysis of 23 studies with 901 BRCA-positive mCRPC patients, what was the PSA 50 response rate for platinum chemotherapy?
questions.explanation: Section 6.7.9 reports a meta-analysis of 23 studies with 901 BRCA-positive mCRPC patients showing PSA 50 response rates of 69% for PARPi versus 74% for platinum (CI: 49-90%).
questions.section_title: 6.7.9. Treatment emergent neuroendocrine PCa and neuroendocrine subtype
questions.fact: In a MA of 23 studies with 901 BRCA-positive mCRPC patients the PSA 50 response rates for PARPi and platinum were 69% (CI: 53-82%), and 74% (CI: 49-90%), respectively.

questions.question: According to the guideline, which molecular alterations are associated with improved efficacy of platinum in aggressive variant prostate cancer?
questions.explanation: Section 6.7.9 states there is preliminary evidence that platinum adds efficacy in patients with aggressive variant PCa molecular signatures including TP53, RB1, and PTEN.
questions.section_title: 6.7.9. Treatment emergent neuroendocrine PCa and neuroendocrine subtype
questions.fact: On a histopathological and molecular level, there is preliminary evidence that platinum adds efficacy in patients with aggressive variant PCa molecular signatures including TP53, RB1, and PTEN.

questions.question: What was the hazard ratio for overall survival comparing PARPi and platinum treatments in the meta-analysis of BRCA-positive mCRPC patients?
questions.explanation: Section 6.7.9 reports that analyses of OS data showed no difference between PARPi and platinum treatments with HR: 0.86 (CI: 0.49-1.52; p=0.6).
questions.section_title: 6.7.9. Treatment emergent neuroendocrine PCa and neuroendocrine subtype
questions.fact: Analyses of OS data showed no difference between PARPi and platinum treatments (HR: 0.86; CI: 0.49-1.52; p = 0.6).

questions.question: In contemporary retrospective series, what percentage of unselected patients and patients without DDR gene alterations showed a PSA decline when treated with platinum?
questions.explanation: Section 6.7.9 states that in contemporary retrospective series, unselected patients as well as patients without DDR gene alterations also showed a 50% PSA decline when treated with platinum in up to 36% of patients.
questions.section_title: 6.7.9. Treatment emergent neuroendocrine PCa and neuroendocrine subtype
questions.fact: Interestingly, in contemporary retrospective series, unselected patients as well as patients without DDR gene alterations also showed a 50% PSA decline when treated with platinum in up to 36% of patients.

questions.question: According to the guideline, when should treatment change be considered in men with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer?

options.text: Preferentially before development of new or worsening existing symptoms
When Eastern Cooperative Oncology Group Performance Status reaches 3

questions.explanation: The guideline states that any treatment change should preferably precede development of de novo symptoms or worsening of existing symptoms. Section 6.7.12 emphasises proactive treatment modification before symptom deterioration.
questions.section_title: 6.7.12. Symptomatic management in metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer
questions.fact: Any treatment change should preferably precede development of de novo symptoms or worsening of existing symptoms

questions.question: Which drug was established as superior third-line treatment in the CARD trial for docetaxel-pretreated patients after one androgen receptor pathway inhibitor?
questions.explanation: The guideline specifically cites the CARD trial as establishing cabazitaxel as the better third-line treatment in docetaxel-pretreated patients after one ARPI, compared to using a second ARPI. This is the only head-to-head comparison explicitly mentioned in the section.
questions.section_title: 6.7.12. Symptomatic management in metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer
questions.fact: The CARD trial clearly established cabazitaxel as the better third-line treatment in docetaxel pretreated patients after one ARPI compared to the use of a second ARPI

questions.question: According to the guideline, patients with an ECOG Performance Status greater than 2 are described as:

options.text: Likely to tolerate all available treatments
Less likely to benefit from treatment

questions.explanation: The guideline states that men with a PS of >2 are less likely to benefit from treatment, while those with PS 0-1 are likely to tolerate treatments. This stratification using ECOG PS is recommended for patient selection.
questions.section_title: 6.7.12. Symptomatic management in metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer
questions.fact: Men with a PS of 0-1 are likely to tolerate treatments and those with a PS of >2 are less likely to benefit

questions.question: According to the APCCC 2015 recommendations, what is the minimum recommended frequency for bone scintigraphy and CT scans in men with CRPC, even in the absence of clinical indication?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that the APCCC suggested bone scintigraphy and CT scans should be performed 'at least every six months, even in the absence of a clinical indication', establishing this as the minimum recommended interval.
questions.fact: Bone scintigraphy and CT scans at least every six months, even in the absence of a clinical indication.

questions.question: According to the guideline, how many of the three criteria (PSA progression, radiographic progression, clinical deterioration) must be fulfilled to justify stopping treatment in CRPC?

options.text: Criteria are only guidelines, not requirements

questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that 'treatments should not be stopped for PSA progression alone' and 'at least two of the three criteria (PSA progression, radiographic progression and clinical deterioration) should be fulfilled to stop treatment', requiring a minimum of two criteria.
questions.fact: At least two of the three criteria (PSA progression, radiographic progression and clinical deterioration) should be fulfilled to stop treatment.

questions.question: Why is PSA alone considered insufficient for monitoring disease activity in advanced CRPC according to the guideline?

options.text: PSA tests are too expensive to perform regularly
PSA levels are always elevated in CRPC patients regardless of disease status
Visceral metastases may develop in men without rising PSA
PSA testing methods are unreliable in castration-resistant disease

questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that 'Prostate-specific antigen alone is not reliable enough for monitoring disease activity in advanced CRPC, since visceral metastases may develop in men without rising PSA', providing the reason why PSA monitoring alone is insufficient.
questions.fact: Visceral metastases may develop in men without rising PSA.

questions.question: What concept did PCWG3 introduce to distinguish between first evidence of progression and the clinical need to change treatment?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that 'the updated PCWG3 put more weight on the importance of documenting progression in existing lesions and introduced the concept of no longer 'clinically benefiting' to underscore the distinction between first evidence of progression and the clinical need to terminate or change treatment'.
questions.fact: The updated PCWG3 put more weight on the importance of documenting progression in existing lesions and introduced the concept of no longer 'clinically benefiting'.

questions.question: According to the guideline on castration-resistant prostate cancer, which of the following healthcare professionals are specifically listed as part of the required multidisciplinary approach?

options.text: Urologists, medical oncologists, radiation oncologists, nurses, psychologists and social workers
Urologists, medical oncologists, radiation oncologists, physiotherapists and palliative care specialists
Urologists, medical oncologists, radiation oncologists, dietitians and cardiologists
Urologists, medical oncologists, radiation oncologists, pharmacists and geriatricians

questions.explanation: Section 6.7.12.a explicitly states that 'A multidisciplinary approach is required with input from urologists, medical oncologists, radiation oncologists, nurses, psychologists and social workers.' None of the other professional groups (physiotherapists, palliative care specialists, dietitians, cardiologists, pharmacists, or geriatricians) are mentioned in the text.
questions.section_title: 6.7.12.a. Common complications due to bone metastases
questions.fact: A multidisciplinary approach is required with input from urologists, medical oncologists, radiation oncologists, nurses, psychologists and social workers

questions.question: Which of the following symptoms is NOT listed among the critical palliation issues that must be addressed when considering additional systemic treatment for castration-resistant prostate cancer?
questions.explanation: Section 6.7.12.a specifically lists 'management of pain, constipation, anorexia, nausea, fatigue and depression' as the critical palliation issues. Fever is not mentioned in the text as one of these issues.
questions.section_title: 6.7.12.a. Common complications due to bone metastases
questions.fact: Critical issues of palliation must be addressed when considering additional systemic treatment, including management of pain, constipation, anorexia, nausea, fatigue and depression

questions.question: Which patient population is specifically described as being 'usually' affected by castration-resistant prostate cancer?
questions.explanation: Section 6.7.12.a explicitly states that 'Castration-resistant PCa is usually a debilitating disease often affecting the elderly male.' The text specifically identifies elderly males as the population typically affected.
questions.section_title: 6.7.12.a. Common complications due to bone metastases
questions.fact: Castration-resistant PCa is usually a debilitating disease often affecting the elderly male.

questions.question: What is described as the first intervention when spinal cord compression is suspected in a patient with CRPC and bone metastases?

options.text: High-dose corticosteroids followed by MRI as soon as possible

questions.explanation: The guideline explicitly states that once spinal cord compression is suspected, high-dose corticosteroids must be given and MRI performed as soon as possible. This precedes any surgical or radiation intervention.
questions.fact: Once suspected, high-dose corticosteroids must be given and MRI performed as soon as possible.

questions.question: Which of the following is listed as a common complication of bone metastases in CRPC?
questions.explanation: The guideline states that common complications due to bone metastases include vertebral collapse or deformity, pathological fractures and spinal cord compression. The text mentions vertebral collapse, not skull fractures.
questions.fact: Common complications due to bone metastases include vertebral collapse or deformity, pathological fractures and spinal cord compression.

questions.question: When is a third-generation bisphosphonate infusion considered appropriate according to the guideline?

options.text: As first-line therapy for all CRPC patients with bone metastases
When external beam radiotherapy is not available
Only in combination with systemic therapy

questions.explanation: The guideline specifically states that a single infusion of a third-generation bisphosphonate could be considered when RT is not available. This is presented as an alternative option, not first-line therapy.
questions.fact: A single infusion of a third-generation bisphosphonate could be considered when RT is not available.

questions.question: What testosterone level threshold must be confirmed before diagnosing castrate-resistant prostate cancer (CRPC)?
questions.explanation: According to Section 6.7.14, the guideline strongly recommends ensuring that testosterone levels are confirmed to be <50ng/dL before diagnosing CRPC. This is the established threshold for confirming castrate status.
questions.section_title: 6.7.14. Recommendations for systemic treatments of castrate-resistant disease
questions.fact: Testosterone levels must be confirmed to be <50ng/dL before diagnosing CRPC

questions.question: How should patients with metastatic castrate-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC) be managed?

options.text: By a primary care physician with specialist consultation

questions.explanation: Section 6.7.14 strongly recommends that patients with mCRPC should be managed in a multidisciplinary team, ensuring comprehensive evaluation and coordinated care.
questions.section_title: 6.7.14. Recommendations for systemic treatments of castrate-resistant disease
questions.fact: Patients with mCRPC should be managed in a multidisciplinary team

questions.question: Which of the following testing modalities should be offered to mCRPC patients if not done previously?

options.text: Somatic and/or germline molecular testing, as well as testing for mismatch repair deficiencies or microsatellite instability
Imaging studies and bone scan only
Complete blood count and liver function tests only

questions.explanation: Section 6.7.14 states that mCRPC patients should be offered somatic and/or germline molecular testing, as well as testing for mismatch repair deficiencies or microsatellite instability if not done previously. This testing is important for treatment planning and identifying potential targeted therapies.
questions.section_title: 6.7.14. Recommendations for systemic treatments of castrate-resistant disease
questions.fact: Offer mCRPC patients somatic and/or germline molecular testing, as well as testing for mismatch repair deficiencies or microsatellite instability, if not done previously

questions.question: According to the EAU guidelines, which of the following is recommended for patients with M0 castrate-resistant prostate cancer and a high risk of developing metastasis (PSA doubling time < 10 months)?
questions.explanation: The guideline states a Strong recommendation to 'Offer apalutamide, darolutamide or enzalutamide to patients with M0 castrate-resistant PCa and a high risk of developing metastasis (PSA doubling time < 10 months) to prolong time to metastases and overall survival.' Docetaxel, radium-223, and abiraterone acetate are not listed as the recommended first-line options for this specific clinical scenario.
questions.fact: Strong recommendation to offer apalutamide, darolutamide or enzalutamide to patients with M0 castrate-resistant PCa and PSA doubling time < 10 months

questions.question: What is the recommended EBRT dose for metastatic (M1) hormone-sensitive prostate cancer according to Figure 6.5?

options.text: Equivalent of up to 54 Gy in 2 Gy fractions
Equivalent of up to 64 Gy in 2 Gy fractions
Equivalent of up to 72 Gy in 2 Gy fractions
Equivalent of up to 78 Gy in 2 Gy fractions

questions.explanation: According to Figure 6.5 treatment notes, EBRT for M1 disease is delivered as IMRT/VMAT + IGRT of the prostate with an equivalent of up to 72 Gy in 2 Gy fractions. This is a specific technical specification provided in the guideline.
questions.fact: EBRT: IMRT/VMAT + IGRT of the prostate (equivalent of up to 72 Gy in 2 Gy fractions) for M1 disease

questions.question: What is the status of triple therapy compared to ADT plus ARTA in metastatic hormone-sensitive prostate cancer according to the EAU guidelines?

options.text: Triple therapy has been proven superior to ADT plus ARTA in randomised trials
Triple therapy was better than ADT plus docetaxel but randomised data comparing it to ADT plus ARTA is missing
ADT plus ARTA has been proven superior to triple therapy
Triple therapy and ADT plus ARTA are equivalent based on indirect comparisons

questions.explanation: The guideline explicitly states: 'Triple therapy was better than ADT plus docetaxel but randomised data comparing it to ADT plus ARTA is missing.' This indicates a knowledge gap regarding the comparison between triple therapy and ADT plus ARTA.
questions.fact: Triple therapy was better than ADT plus docetaxel but randomised data comparing it to ADT plus ARTA is missing
